description:
In this task, four stimuli are presented that are the combination of two levels of one stimulus dimension (e.g., John and Sam, where the dimension would be face identity) and two levels of a second stimulus dimension (e.g., neutral and happy, where the dimension would be face emotion). Participants are briefly presented with each of the four stimuli and required to report their value in both dimensions. 
https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-295X.93.2.154

description: A not B with Invisible Displacement: This task asks children to inhibit their responses based on the location of previous toys and to remember where a toy is hidden. A toy is hidden at midline and shifted to one side under a tub, then a screen is placed to obstruct the child’s view and a second tub is placed on the opposite side. After two consecutive correct trials, the location is reversed. The DV is the percent correct of 12 trials. To facilitate simultaneous EEG recordings and reduce movement artifacts, a ‘looking’ version has been adapted rather than requiring reaching.
title: A not B with Invisible Displacement

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Adequacy of Services and Supports Scale assesses adequacy of supports and services, regardless of whether participants receive formal disability services. Includes support from service organizations, agencies, or programs; people paid to help; and unpaid support from family, friends, and others in the participant's life. Major adaptation of Quality of Care Scale (QCS). Original Quality of Care Scale available at DOI: 10.1016/j.ridd.2010.12.030
title: AASPIRE Adequacy of Services and Supports Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Alcohol and Drug Use Module assesses use of alcohol and drugs (non-prescribed medications or illicit substances)
title: AASPIRE Alcohol and Drug Use Module

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Autistic Burnout Scale assesses experience of chronic autistic burnout, including big negative long-term changes lasting three months or longer and compared to what is "usual". Revision of the original AASPIRE Autistic Burnout scale. Publication on the original AASPIRE Autistic Burnout scale available at DOI: 10.1002/aur.3129
title: AASPIRE Autistic Burnout Scale - Revised

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Barriers to Communication Scale evaluates frequency and severity of barriers to communication, or things that got in the way of successful communication.
title: AASPIRE Barriers to Communication Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Barriers to Healthcare Checklist assesses issues impacting ease and accessibility of (good) healthcare services. Includes both current and theoretical barriers. Minor revisions from the original AASPIRE Barriers to Healthcare Checklist - Short Form (https://nda.nih.gov/data-structure/bth01). Original checklist available at DOI: 10.1177/1362361316661261
title: AASPIRE Barriers to Healthcare Checklist - Short Form - Revised

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Choices and Decisions Scale assesses whether a participant feels they are allowed and able to make their own choices and decisions in a variety of realms, regardless of qualification to do so.
title: AASPIRE Choices and Decisions Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Communication Characteristics module is designed to assess self-reported receptive, expressive, and overall communication fluency, reading and writing level, and use of alternatives to speech.

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Community Participation Scale assesses participation in, accessibility of, and sense of belonging in community activities or other activities outside the house that involve interacting with other people.
title: AASPIRE Community Participation Scale

title: AASPIRE LGBTQ+ Experiences with Developmental Disability Services Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The LGBTQ+ Related Quality of Healthcare Scale assesses patient experiences with gender and sexual minority related aspects of healthcare.
title: AASPIRE LGBTQ+ Related Quality of Healthcare Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The LGBTQ+ Sense of Comfort and Safety Scale assesses comfort with and experience of safety related to gender identity and sexual orientation among LGBTQ+ populations. Includes section about comfort and safety with sexual orientation for all LGBTQ+ participants, as well as additional sections specific to trans and agender participants.
title: AASPIRE LGBTQ+ Sense of Comfort and Safety Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Overall Employment Satisfaction Scale is an overall employment satisfaction assessment, compatible with traditional employment, self-employment, and employment outside of traditional organizational structures.
title: AASPIRE Overall Employment Satisfaction Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Overall Health Scale assesses general overall health (including physical, mental, and social health and quality of life) and identifies issues that are affecting daily life function.
title: AASPIRE Overall Health Scale

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Patient-Provider Communication Scale assesses quality of and confidence in communication with primary care providers and/or general medical care. Minor revisions from the original AASPIRE PPCS-8, which itself was adapted from items included in the Health Information National Trends Survey (HINTS). Original AASPIRE PPCS-8 available at DOI: 10.1177/1362361320967178.
title: AASPIRE Patient-Provider Communication Scale - Revised

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Quality of Life Item is a quality of life scale meant to capture overall sense of well-being and life satisfaction. Major adaptation of Quality of Life item developed by Z. Williams. Original item available at DOI: 10.13140/RG.2.2.11706.93126
title: AASPIRE Quality of Life Item

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Satisfaction with Developmental Disability Services is an adaptation of Quality of Care scale for the assessment of satisfaction with developmental disability services and support. Major adaptation of Quality of Care Scale (QCS). Original Quality of Care Scale available at DOI: 10.1016/j.ridd.2010.12.030
title: AASPIRE Satisfaction with Developmental Disability Services

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Social Support Scales assess companionship (doing things with another person), emotional support (wanting support from another person), informational support (wanting information or advice from another person), practical support (someone to help do things), and isolation (feeling isolated from others). Major adaptation of the 5 PROMIS Social Support Scales by AASPIRE. See also companion01, prsupport01, and prsi01. Original PROMIS scales available at DOI: 10.1037/hea0000055
title: AASPIRE Social Support Scales

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The Unmet Healthcare Needs Checklist is a checklist of the types of healthcare a participant felt they needed but did not receive within the last 6 months. Revised from the original AASPIRE Unmet Healthcare Needs Checklist, which itself was adapted from an item in the 2002/2003 Joint US Canada Survey. Original AASPIRE Unmet Healthcare Needs Checklist available at DOI: 10.1177/1362361320967178
title: AASPIRE Unmet Healthcare Needs Checklist - Revised

title: ABCD Stanford Mental Arithmetic Response Time Evaluation

description: This file contains parameters derived from the Stop Signal Task reaction time distribution including Gaussian (mean, median, reaction time standard deviation, accuracy, kurtosis, skew), ex-Gaussian (mu, sigma, tau), and diffusion model (drift, boundary separation, non-decision time, and intertrial variability) parameters in 4 equal blocks (2 derived from run 1 and 2 derived from run 2) of the Stop Signal Task obtained at the baseline visit. Reaction times for correct go trials in the Stop Signal Task collected at baseline visit were used to generate these parameters. The following exclusion criteria were utilized: < 66% on Go trial accuracy, <90 correct Go trials, <150msec Go RT, Stop trial probability <.25 or >.75, drift diffusion model fit parameter <.05.
title: ABCD Stop Signal Task: Block Variables

description: This file contains parameters derived from the Stop Signal Task reaction time distribution including Gaussian (mean, median, reaction time standard deviation, accuracy, kurtosis, skew), ex-Gaussian (mu, sigma, tau), and diffusion model (drift, boundary separation, non-decision time, and intertrial variability) parameters, for each of the two runs of the Stop Signal Task obtained at the baseline visit. Reaction times for correct go trials in the Stop Signal Task collected at baseline visit were used to generate these parameters. The following exclusion criteria were utilized: < 66% on Go trial accuracy, <90 correct Go trials, <150msec Go RT, Stop trial probability <.25 or >.75, drift diffusion model fit parameter <.05.
title: ABCD Stop Signal Task: Reaction Time Variables (RTV)

description: This instrument includes sleep level aggregated to the week level for all valid days (i.e. >180 minutes of sleep time).  Metrics include sum of and average number of instances of, and number of minutes in, each sleep stage.  Average HR within each stage is also included.  Weeks are broken into weekday only, weekend only, and all days of week.

description: The AURORA-COVID Impact Survey (AURORA-CIS) is a newly designed short survey to learn and understand the impact of the coronavirus (COVID-19/SARS-CoV-2) pandemic on the trauma survivors currently enrolled in the AURORA Study. This supplementary survey was created to assess the additional impact of the pandemic on the mental health outcomes of the study participants beyond that which could be associated with the traumatic event that led to their emergency room visit.

description:
AIMSweb (Achievement Improvement Monitoring System) is a research-based system that provides tools to implement key components of Response to Intervention (RTI) programs, including universal screening and progress monitoring using Curriculum-Based Measurement (CBM). It helps educators identify students at risk, monitor their progress, and make data-driven decisions about interventions. AIMSweb is a web-based system that offers tools for reading, math, spelling, and writing assessments. 
(Shinn & Shinn, 2002)
title: Achievement Improvement Monitoring System (AIMSweb)

The Acquired Capability With Rehearsal for Suicide Scale (ACWRSS) was devised to capture the key facets of acquired capability - pain tolerance and fearlessness of death - while also incorporating deliberate and active means to increase preparedness for suicide. The original Acquired Capability for Suicide Scale (ACSS; Bender, Gordon, Bresin, & Joiner, 2011; Van Orden et al., 2008; sui_acss01) refer to both product and process of suicide while the ACWRSS also includes preparation for suicide. 

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1037/pas0000276
title: Acquired Capability With Rehearsal for Suicide Scale (ACWRSS)

description: The Action Control Scale (ACS-90) measures action-state orientation (ASO), which describes individual differences in volitional processes such as goal initiation and maintenance. Subscales evaluate action orientation subsequent to failure vs. preoccupation (AOF); prospective and decision-related action orientation vs. hesitation (AOD); action orientation during (successful) performance of activities (intrinsic orientation) vs. volatility (AOP). DOI: 10.1080/00223891.2021.1981344
title: Action Control Scale (ACS-90)

description:
Delinquency was measured using items from the National Longitudinal Study on Adolescent Health (Add Health; Harris et al., 2009). The Add Health dataset included 15 delinquency items. description: The Addiction Research Center Inventory (ARCI) is a subjective drug effects questionnaire to evaluate whether a respondent is currently affected by or under the influence of a psychologically active drug. The self-reported questionnaire was developed in the early 1960s at the National Institute of Mental Health Addiction Research Center from the use of sentence completion and other association techniques on male subjects under drug and placebo conditions. The full form of ARCI contains 550 true-false items to delineate schizoid and psychopathic characteristics, and shortened versions are widely used for both males and females. PhenX Toolkit Protocol 520101, Acute Subjective Response to Substances - Current - Specific - Drugs derived from https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt1971122part1245 and https://doi.org/10.1007/bf02245075
title: Addiction Research Center Inventory (ARCI)

description:

Seligman, M. E. P., Abramson, L. Y., Semmel, A., & von Baeyer, C. (1979). Depressive attributional style. Journal of Abnormal Psychology, 88, 242-247.

description:

https://doi.org/10.1037/t01546-000

description: The Adolescent Relationship Scale Questionnaire (ARSQ) is a revision of Griffin and Bartholomew's Relationship Scales Questionnaire (resq01, 1994). Four subscales (secure, fearful, preoccupied, dismissing) compose the Self and Other models.

description: The Adult ADHD Clinician Diagnostic Scale v 1.2 is a semi-structured interview that establishes the documentation of current adult symptomatology, with suggested probes for the clinician to administer to thoroughly establish the presence and extent of adult ADHD symptoms. The 18 items in the ACDS match the 18 symptom domains of ADHD noted in DSM-IV.
title: Adult ADHD Clinical Diagnostic Scale

description: The Adult ADHD Investigator Symptom Rating Scale (AISRS; Spencer et al., 2010) measures ADHD symptoms in adults patients. This 18-item scale directly corresponds to the 18 DSM-IV symptoms of ADHD where 9 inattentive items alternate with 9 hyperactive-impulsive items. Each item is scored as follows: 0 ("none"), 1 ("mild"), 2 ("moderate"), and 3 ("severe"); the maximum total score for the scale is 54 points, with 27 points for each subscale. The AISRS total score is the sum of the inattentive and hyperactive-impulsive subscales. https://doi.org/10.1037/t16509-000
title: Adult ADHD Investigator Symptom Rating Scale (AISRS)

description:
The Adult Attachment Scale (AAS) was officially developed in 1990 but built on the earlier work of Hazen & Shaver (1987) and Levy & Davis (1988).  The scale was developed by decomposing the original three prototypical descriptions (Hazen & Shaver, 1987) into a series of 18 items. The scale consists of 18 items scored on a 5 point likert-type scale.  It measures adult attachment styles named “Secure”, “Anxious” and “Avoidant”.
Collins, N. L., & Read, S. J. (1990). Adult attachment, working models, and relationship quality in dating couples. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 58(4), 644-663.
title: Adult Attachment Scale

description:
The Adult Concentration Index (ACI) is an adult self-report measure of sluggish cognitive tempo (SCT)using items that distinguish SCT from ADHD inattention. Items are rated on a 4-point scale with higher scores representing greater SCT symptoms.

description:
The adult hope scale (AHS) measures Snyder's cognitive model of hope which defines hope as "a positive motivational state that is based on an interactively derived sense of successful (a) agency (goal-directed energy), and (b) pathways (planning to meet goals)" (Snyder, Irving, & Anderson, 1991, p. 287). The adult hope scale contains 12 items. Four items measure pathways thinking, four items measure agency thinking, and four items are fillers. Participants respond to each item using a 8-point scale ranging from definitely false to definitely true and the scale takes only a few minutes to complete.

Snyder, C. R., Harris, C., Anderson, J. R., Holleran, S. A., Irving, L. M., Sigmon, S. T., et al.(1991). The will and the ways: Development and validation of an individual-differences measure of hope. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 60, 570-585.
title: Adult Hope Scale

The ABS will serve as a subjective index of emotional reactivity and will be completed before and after each of the two decision-making tasks.

description: The Affective Misattribution Procedure (AMP) is a task that assesses implicit “liking” or “disliking” of stimuli (negative, positive, neutral, suicide, and self-injurious) that is misattributed to a non-valenced, unknown symbol because of the speed with which each image is displayed (75ms and 100ms, respectively). On each trial, participants are instructed to view the screen and select between “pleasant” or “unpleasant” buttons based on how they feel about the unknown random symbol that appears. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797612458805 ,  https://doi.org/10.1111/spc3.12148

description: The Agonistic Continuum Scale is an alternative model for the relation between paraphilic coercion and sadism, arguing that the two constructs are distributed along a single continuum. The items are rated on a 5 point Likert scale.

description:
The Alcohol and Drugs Harms to Others Survey (ADHTO) is a nationwide study on how other people's use of alcohol, drugs, and tobacco impacts health and well-being. The Helpseeking section measures the people/programs the participants use to receive support for physical or mental health problems due to someone else's use of alcohol and/or drugs, how satisfied participants are with receiving help, and why people don't reach out for help.
https://doi.org/10.15288/jsad.23-00387

description: The Alexian Brothers Urge to Self-Injure Scale (ABUSI) assesses the motivation of an individual to engage in self-injury. It was adapted from the Penn Alcohol Craving Scale (PACS, pacs01) and assesses frequency, intensity, and duration of the urge, the difficulty of resisting the urge, and the overall urge to self-injure. Responses are on a 7-point Likert scale with higher scores representing more intense urges to self-injure. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psychres.2010.05.018

description: Allostatic Load refers to the cumulative physiological burden imposed by chronic stress and repeated activation of allostatic (adaptive) systems, including neuroendocrine, cardiovascular, metabolic, and immune pathways. description:
The Amsterdam Resting-State Questionnaire (ARSQ) is a 50-item questionnaire used to measure resting-state cognition. The elements are scored on a 5-point Likert scale where 1 = Completely disagree and 5 = Completely agree. The assessment also contains seven dimensions of resting-state cognition using factor analysis: Discontinuity of Mind, Theory of Mind, Self, Planning, Sleepiness, Comfort, and Somatic Awareness.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23964225/

description: The Approach and Avoidance of Alcohol Questionnaire (AAAQ) was designed to assess mild and intense inclinations to drink, as well as inclinations to avoid drinking over the past week.  Respondents were instructed to consider each AAAQ item with reference to their disposition toward alcohol over the previous week and to rate how strongly they agreed with each item on a nine-point Likert-type scale, ranging from ‘not at all’ (0) to ‘very strongly’ (8).  https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1360-0443.2004.00714.x
title: Approach and Avoidance of Alcohol Questionnaire (AAAQ)

description: The Appropriated Racial Oppression Scale (AROS) measures appropriated racial oppression and its dimensions. It is a 24-item measure that was created to assess the beliefs, attitudes, and emotional reactions to appropriate racial oppression in people of color The AROS has four subscales: Emotional Reactions (7 items), American Standard of Beauty (6 items), Devaluation of Own Group (8 items), and Appropriation of Negative Stereotypes (3 items). The 24-item scale is rated on a 7-point Likert-type scale, (1= Strongly Disagree) to (7=Strongly Agree). Item scores were summed for each of the four subscales and then the subscale scores were summed to create a total score. https://doi.org/10.1037/cdp0000037
title: Appropriated Racial Oppression Scale (AROS)

description: The Attachment Doll Play Assessment classifies children into four patterns of parental attachment after performing a task. The task involves a child selecting various dolls to represent their "self" and to construct a family, adult introducing four open-ended stories related to attachment, and the child using the dolls to enact each story's completion. The four patterns of attachment are secure, avoidant, ambivalent, and disorganized/controlling.

description:
The Attentional Fixation on Suicide Experiences Questionnaire (AFSEQ) was developed to measure attentional fixation on suicide, a cognitive process characterized by a preoccupation with suicide as a solution. This 15-item self-report measure assesses participants' experiences immediately prior to a suicidal crisis with higher scores (ranging from 0 to 45) indicating greater attentional fixation.
DOI: 10.1111/sltb.12904
title: Attentional Fixation on Suicide Experiences Questionnaire (AFSEQ)

description: "A modification of the Lithium Attitudes Questionnaire (Harvey 1991) which evaluates an individual's attitudes towards mood stabilizing medication (Adams and Scott 2000). The AMSQ comprises 19 items grouped into 7 subscales: general opposition to prophylaxis (4 items), denial of illness severity (3 items), negative attitudes toward drugs in general (3 items), and lack of information about mood stabilizers (1 item). Higher scores on each subscale represent more negative attitudes toward mood stabilizers. Reliability for the 19 items ranges from 57.6 % to 96.9%." Source: https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT00183703
title: Attitudes Towards Mood Stabilizers Questionnaire

description:
A scale designed to measure attraction to sexual aggression is described, based on earlier work assessing self‐reported likelihood of committing rape. This scale's associations with measures of theoretically relevant attitudes, perceptions, and behavioral inclinations are examined in comparison with briefer measures, and with a number of other scales measuring attraction to various types of sexual interactions. These include conventional sex (e.g., heterosexual intercourse), homosexuality, bondage, unconventional sex (e.g., group sex), and deviant sex (e.g., pedophilia).
doi/abs/10.1080/00224498909551491
title: Attraction to Sexual Aggression

description:
The Audio-Visual Abnormalities Questionnaire (AVAQ) is an 85 item questionnaire used to characterize anomalies in visual and auditory perception in schizophrenia spectrum disorders. The AVAQ demonstrated excellent reliability, internal consistency and construct validity. Accordingly, the instrument could be useful for characterizing sensory dysfunctions across the schizophrenia spectrum that could guide interventions as well as aid the development of biomarkers.
DOI:10.1016/j.schres.2019.03.016
title: Audio-Visual Abnormalities Questionnaire (AVAQ)

description: The Autism Family Experience Questionnaire (AFEQ) was developed through focus groups and online consultation with parents, and reflected parental priorities. The 48 item questionnaire contains items organized into four pre-specified domains: experience of being a parent; family life; child development, understanding and social relationships; and child symptoms. The AFEQ includes both positively and negatively worded statements and was scored on an order scale: 1 = always to 5 = never, with an option for “Not Applicable”.

description:
The  BRIC  is  a  5-item  scale  designed  for  clinicians  to quickly  rate  a  child’s  global  behavior  during  a  structured assessment  session. The  scale  was  purposely  kept  short  to reduce  clinician  burden  by  allowing  it  to  be  completed within a minute or two. Three of the items assessed the three main  symptoms  of ADHD  (i.e.,  hyperactivity,  impulsivity, and inattention) and are therefore referred to as the ADHD Symptom Triad. The other two items assessed indicators of impairment often associated with ADHD symptomatology (i.e.  mood  and  sociability)  and  are  thus  called  the  Mood/Sociability  Factor.  These  two  items  were  selected  as  they were  thought  to  reflect  areas  of  impairment  related  to ADHD  symptoms  that  are  likely  to  arise  in  the  preschool years. All five items were rated on a 5-point Likert Scale. 
DOI: 10.1177/1087054709332476

description: This behavioral task assesses how individuals choose between risky options (outcome probabilities < 1) of varying magnitude under differing levels of ambiguity. Participants select between two urns of differing magnitudes on each trial. Outcome probability for each urn is given by the percentage of 'X' tokens relative to all tokens (both 'X' and 'O') in the urn. On one-half of trials, complete information regarding outcome probability is given for both urns (i.e., all tokens are shown for both urns). On the other half of trials, one urn is made ambiguous through the masking of varying levels of information regarding outcome probability.
title: Behavioral Ambiguity

description: This task uses the emotional Stroop cognitive interference paradigm to assess body image flexibility. Participants are presented with either body image related stimuli (e.g., words: fat, thighs, stomach) or neutral stimuli (e.g., words: door, table, chair) in four different colors (blue, red, yellow, green). Participants are asked to press one of four response keys to indicate the color of the stimuli regardless of content. Dependent variables are reaction time (ms) and accuracy. doi:10.1053/comp.2002.33497

Brief report: The Brief Alcohol Social Density Assessment (BASDA): convergent, criterion-related, and incremental validity. J Stud Alcohol Drugs. 2013 Sep;74(5):810-5. doi: 10.15288/jsad.2013.74.810. PMID: 23948542; PMCID: PMC3749323
title: Brief Alcohol Social Density Assessment (BASDA)

description:
The Brief Health Literacy Screen (BHLS) is a short, 1 minute health literacy assessment that has been validated for use in outpatient and emergency department settings. The BHLS consists of three items on a 5-point response scale that are summed such that higher scores indicating higher subjective health literacy

DOI: 10.1007/s11606-013-2568-0

description: The BMSLSS is a self-assessment where participants indicate their satisfaction with life. The Brief Music in Mood Regulation scale (BMMR) is a 21-item self-report instrument for assessing the use of seven different music-related mood-regulation strategies. Items are answered with a 5-point Likert scale rating agreement.

description:
The Brief Obsessive-Compulsive Scale (BOCS), derived from the Yale-Brown Obsessive-Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS) and the children’s version (CY-BOCS), is a short self-report tool used to aid in the assessment of obsessive-compulsive symptoms and diagnosis of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). The BOCS consists of a 15-item Symptom Checklist including three items (hoarding, dysmorphophobia and self-harm) accompanied by a single six-item Severity Scale for obsessions and compulsions combined.

DOI: 10.3109/08039488.2014.884631
title: Brief Obsessive-Compulsive Scale (BOCS)

description:
The Brief Resilient Coping Scale, is a a 4-item measure, that captures tendencies to cope with stress adaptively. The scale focuses on the tendency to effectively use coping strategies in flexible, committed ways to actively solve problems despite stressful circumstances.
DOI: 10.1177/1073191103258144
title: Brief Resilient Coping Scale (BRCS)

description:
BabyBees Brief Screening Questionnaire for Infant Sleep (BISQ): This is a parent reported questionnaire on infants/toddler sleep. We used this scale for our COVID-19 and Perinatal Experiences (COPE) cohort, which comprises of 800+ families across New York City. This measure was administered to mothers during their infant's 6 and 12 month time point.

 DOI: 10.1542/peds.113.6.e570
title: Brief Screening Questionnaire for Infant Sleep (BISQ)

description: The Brief Test of Attention is a standardized assessment tool that measures an individual’s ability to divide verbal-linguistic attention and consists of a Numbers section and a Letters section, each consisting of 10 trials in which participants are verbally presented with a series of numbers and letters After each trial in the Numbers section, participants are asked to tell the rater how many numbers were in the trial. After each trial in the Letters section, participants are asked to tell the rater how many letters were in the trial.  Throughout the BTA, participants are not permitted to count on their fingers; they must count in their heads instead. https://doi.org/10.1080/13854049608406666
title: Brief Test of Attention (BTA)

description:
The BTQ is a brief self-report questionnaire that is derived from the Brief Trauma Interview (Schnurr et al., 1995). The BTQ was originally designed to assess traumatic exposure according to DSM‐IV but specifically asked only about Criterion A.1 (life threat/serious injury) because of the difficulty of accurately assessing A.2 (subjective response) in a brief self-report
format. Criterion A.2 has been eliminated from the PTSD diagnostic criteria in DSM- 5, so the BTQ provides a complete assessment of Criterion A. 

https://www.ptsd.va.gov/professional/assessment/documents/BTQ.pdf
title: Brief Trauma Questionnaire

Part A assesses pre-planned physical activity (PPA) while Part B assesses unplanned lifestyle physical activity (UPA). description:
The Burden Assessment Scale (BAS) contains 19 items that capture both objective and subjective consequences of providing ongoing care to the seriously mentally ill. The scale evaluates perceived burden related to a family member's severe mental illness. The elements are rated on a Likert scale from 1 to 4 where 1 = Not at all; 2 = A little; 3 = Some; 4 = A lot.
https://doi.org/10.1016/0149-7189(94)90004-3
title: Burden Assessment Scale

description: The Burden Scale for Family Caregivers (BSFC) assesses subjective burden of family caregivers. It is designed for use to identify caregiver needs and evaluate service effectiveness of a program. The original version includes 28 items to which the subject agrees or disagrees on a 4-point scale. The short version (BSFC-s) contains 10 items. https://doi.org/10.1186%2F1471-2318-14-23
title: Burden Scale for Family Caregivers (BSFC)

description:
The Sniffin’ Sticks test is a psychophysical designed to assess a patient's olfactory performance by means of 3 subtests: threshold test, identification test and discrimination test. It is one of the essential tools to assess an individual's olfactory performances and to monitor the course of these performances as a function of physiological (ageing) or pathological events.
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anorl.2015.08.004
title: Burghart Sniffin' Sticks Identification Test

description:
The Buss-Durkee Hostility Inventory (BDHI) is a 75-item true-false self-report used to measure hostility and aggression and to estimate the intensity of various subclasses of hostility. The BDHI is also divided into 8 subscales: Assault, Indirect Hostility, Irritability, Negativism, Resentment, Suspicion, Verbal Hostility, and Guilt.
Buss, A. H., & Durkee, M. (1957). The Buss-Durkee Hostility Inventory. Palo Alto, CA: Consulting Psychologists Press.
title: Buss-Durkee Hostility Inventory

description: The Bystander Decisional Balance Scale (BDBS) assess positive benefits and negative consequences for intervening “in a situation where you thought someone might be being hurt or was at risk of being hurt.” Five positive and six negative statements are rated on a five-point scale of importance. A total decisional balance score was obtained by subtracting the “cons” score from the “pros” score. Thus, higher scores indicate greater perceptions of positive aspects of bystander behavior when compared to negative aspects. https://doi.org/10.1002/jcop.20159
title: Bystander Decisional Balance Scale (BDBS)

description: The below measures of efficacy come from the Bystander Efficacy Scale (Banyard, 2008), which assesses a person’s confidence in performing bystander behaviors. Participant responses can range from 0 (can’t do) to 100 (very certain). Banyard, V. L. (2008). Measurement and correlates of pro-social bystander behavior: The case of interpersonal violence. description:
We have developed a battery of measures related to bystander behavior in alcohol-related risk situations—the The Bystanders to Alcohol Risk Scales-Strategies (BARS-S) measure bystander behavior in alcohol-related risk situations and bystander strategies. Strategies are the behaviors that bystanders engage in with the intention of reducing another's risk of harm due to alcohol, which can be used before, during, or after an alcohol-related consequence.

DOI: 10.15288/jsad.23-00040
title: Bystander to Alcohol Risk Scales-Strategies (BARS-S)

description: The C-Section Anxiety Sensitivity (C-SAS) was created to measure anxiety specific to cesarean delivery rather than generalized anxiety. title: C-Section Anxiety Sensitivity (C-SAS) Pre- and Post-Delivery

description: The Word List Memory tasks are derived from the Consortium to Establish a Registry for Alzheimer's Disease (CERAD) Neuropsychological Battery (Morris et al 1989). The memory/learning task consists of three trials during which the subject is asked to remember 10 words presented to them in random orders. The recall task tests delayed memory from the ten words in the memory task. The recognition task tests recognition for the ten words among ten additional "distractor" words. https://doi.org/10.1080/09084282.1995.9645357
title: CERAD Neuropsychological Battery - Word List Memory (Learning, Recall, Recognition)

From the EAT I includes: Food Self-Efficacy and subscales.

description:
This self-report questionnaire assesses weekly frequency and duration of a variety of lifestyle physical activities that are meaningful and appropriate for older adults. It includes activities of various intensities (from light to vigorous) such as walking, running, hiking, swimming, bicycling, dancing, tennis, aerobics, yoga/tai chi, gardening, and housework. Intensity is reflected in the item stem (e.g., light and heavy gardening are separate items). 

Citation: Stewart A, Mills KM, King AC, Haskell WL, Gillis D, and Ritter PL. CHAMPS Physical Activity Questionnaire for Older Adults: Outcomes for Interventions. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise. 2001, 33(7), 1126-1141. PMID 11445760.
title: CHAMPS: Community Healthy Activities Model Program for Seniors

description: The CHICa (Cold, Hunger, Irritation, Cognitive Attenuation) Scale measures subjects reactions to sleep deprivation. The final 26 item assessment results in four sub scores: cold (four items), hunger (five items), irritability (eight items) and cognitive attenuation (nine items). https://doi.org/10.1111/jsr.12115
title: CHICa Scale

description: The The COVID and Life Stressors Impact on Mental Health and Well-being Study (CLIMB) assesses COVID-19–related life stressors and hardships, including financial strain, job loss, bereavement, relationship problems, and disruptions to daily life. It also evaluates emotional impacts such as loneliness and overall stress related to the pandemic. https://doi.org/10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2020.19686
title: CLIMB: The COVID and Life Stressors Impact on Mental Health and Well-being Study

description:
The Cognitive Neuroscience Test Reliability and Clinical applications for Schizophrenia (CNTRaCS) Consortium was formed to optimize and provide psychometric data on theoretically motivated tasks from cognitive neuroscience so that they can be used as measures of treatment effects in translational research seeking to improve cognition and clinical and functional outcomes in schizophrenia. Tasks assess working memory, reinforcement learning working memory, sensory precision and episodic memory.

DOI: 10.1093/schbul/sbae038
title: CNTRACS Memory Tasks

description:
During this task, participants breathe 7.5% CO2 enriched air through a facemask, mimicking the physiologic symptoms of panic, with participants rating how distressing they find these symptoms to be. Participants breathe normally for five minutes (baseline), followed by two minutes of breathing ambient air through a face mask (pre-CO2 phase), eight minutes of breathing only the CO2 enriched air (CO2 phase), and five minutes in which they breathe ambient room air (recovery phase). Participants will then be offered a breathing relaxation exercise if needed, to reduce any lingering anxiety.  
DOI: 10.1001/archpsyc.62.1.73
title: CO2 Challenge Task

description:
This is a 9 question scale that probes differential treatment in a variety of  everyday circumstances or settings. Responses reflect the  frequency of occurrence of each over the lifetime, ranging from 1 (“almost every day”) to 6 (“Never”).  An overall follow-up question to any positive response asks the respondent to identify the main reason for the experiences, with 12 categories including ancestral/national origins, gender, race, age, religion, height, weight, other physical appearance aspects, sexual orientation, education/income, physical disability, tribal affiliation. An additional category allows the respondent to specify another reason. This scale was developed by Williams DR et al. and has been widely used in many studies of physical and mental health.
Williams DR, Yu Y, Jackson JS, Anderson NB. Racial differences in physical and mental health: Socioeconomic status, Stress, and Discrimination.  J Health Psychology 1997; 2:  335-351.
title: COGA Lifespan Everyday Discrimination Questionnaire

description: The Colorado Assessment Tests (CATs) by Haskar Davis (2002) assess planning and problem-solving aspects of executive functions using the Tower of London Test (TOLT) and visuospatial short-term and working memory using the Visual Span Test (VST). Davis HP, and Keller F (2002). Colorado Assessment Tests (CATs), Version 1.2, Colorado Springs, Colorado (manual)
title: COGA Neuropsych Colorado Assessment Test

description: The SSAGA-5, similar to prior versions of the SSAGA based on earlier DSM classification, covers a range of symptoms and behaviors that map onto DSM-5 criteria in order to yield DSM5 lifetime diagnoses of specific substance use disorders (tobacco, alcohol, cannabis, cocaine, other stimulants, opiates, and sedatives). DSM-4, 3R and ICD-10 substance dependence and abuse are also covered.  Other lifetime disorders assessed are major depression, antisocial personality disorder, social phobia, panic with and without agoraphobia, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and PTSD.  As well, nondiagnostic sections covering demographics, medical history, suicidality, current social relationships and religiosity are included.  In the SUD sections, questions probing timing and specifics of treatment history and recovery are included.  The SSAGA-5 has been programmed in Blaise and is designed for administration by a trained interviewer.  Diagnostic scoring algorithms are available.
title: COGA SSAGA5: Agoraphobia

DSM-4, 3R and ICD-10 substance dependence and abuse are also covered.  Other lifetime disorders assessed are major depression, antisocial personality disorder, social phobia, panic with and without agoraphobia, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and PTSD.  As well, nondiagnostic sections covering demographics, medical history, suicidality, current social relationships and religiosity are included.  In the SUD sections, questions probing timing and specifics of treatment history and recovery are included.  The SSAGA-5 has been programmed in Blaise and is designed for administration by a trained interviewer.  Diagnostic scoring algorithms are available.
title: COGA SSAGA5: Marijuana

description:
The UPPS-P Short Form is a 20 item, psychometrically validated self-report instrument designed to assess five distinct facets of impulsivity: Negative Urgency, Positive Urgency, Lack of Premeditation, Lack of Perseverance, and Sensation Seeking. It is widely utilized in both clinical and research settings to evaluate impulsive behaviors and associated risk factors.
Cyders MA, Littlefield AK, Coffey S, Karyadi KA. Examination of a short English version of the UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale. Addict Behav. 2014 Sep;39(9):1372-6. doi: 10.1016/j.addbeh.2014.02.013. Epub 2014 Mar 3. PMID: 24636739; PMCID: PMC4055534.
title: COGA UPPS-P Short Form
COINS Pre and Post Scan Questionnaire
COMBO (Common Measure, Better Outcomes) Health Behavior Measures

title: COMPASS Rating Scale Form

description:
COVID 19 Vaccine Hesitancy Survey- Adults: This measure was modified by our team to fit hesitancy to take the COVID vaccine, original measure reference: Domek, G. J., O'Leary, S. T., Bull, S., Bronsert, M., Contreras-Roldan, I. L., Ventura, G. A. B., ... & Asturias, E. J. (2018). Measuring vaccine hesitancy: Field testing the WHO SAGE Working Group on Vaccine Hesitancy survey tool in Guatemala. Vaccine, 36(35), 5273-5281.
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.vaccine.2018.07.046
title: COVID 19 Vaccine Hesitancy Survey - Adults

description: A questionnaire about the impacts COVID-19 had on the participants life, including their employment, family life, and other relationships. The questionnaire also includes questions about whether the participant knows how to protect themselves from the virus.
title: COVID Impacts Questionnaire

description: Created and adapted by the Boston Children's Hospital asks parents to answer on behalf of their child(ren) regarding schooling during the COVID-19 pandemic.
title: COVID Schooling Questionnaire

description: This survey assesses the stress impact of the COVID-19 pandemic. Questions assess stress levels due to social, well-being, job/financial, and health changes during the pandemic.
title: COVID Stress Survey

description:
The COVID-19 Attitudes Scale is composed of nine scales, each composed of three to five items, to measure beliefs about the health risks and social distancing requirements about COVID-19, forms of scientific and medical distrust, and politically-motivated considerations pertaining to the pandemic response.

10.1371/journal.pone.0263191
title: COVID-19 Attitudes Scale

description: Used to evaluate exposure and vulnerability to contracting COVID-19.
title: COVID-19 Experiences (COVEX) Questionnaire

description: The COVID-19 Exposure and Family Impact Scales (CEFIS) has strong factors assessing Exposure to events related to COVID-19, and the Impact of these events on families of children in pediatric healthcare. Participants reported a range of COVID-19-related events on the bidirectional 4-point impact scale. Factor analysis identified six factors for Exposure (COVID-19 experiences, Access to essentials, Disruptions to living conditions, Loss of income, Family caregiving and activities, and Designation as an essential worker). There were three factors for Impact (Personal well-being, Family interactions, and Distress). https://doi.org/10.1093%2Fjpepsy%2Fjsab026
title: COVID-19 Exposure and Family Impact Scales

description: The Collaborative Study on the Genetics of Alcoholism (COGA)'s COVID-19 questionnaires included 33 COVID-19-related stress and coping activities items adapted from the Coronavirus Health Impact Survey (CRISIS) and the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study COVID questionnaires. Assessment items covered perceived stresses, social disconnection, relationship quality, COVID-19 illness in the respondents and their family members (including prolonged hospitalization or death due to COVID-19 illness), economic hardships resulting from the pandemic (e.g., job loss, loss of household income, food insecurity), and coping strategies. Self-reported changes in alcohol and other substance use were also assessed.
title: COVID-19 Pandemic Stress Questionnaire

description: Based on the John Hopkins Module COVID-19 Community Response Survey (CCS), derived from the PhenX Toolkit. Additional sources: AIDS Linked to Intravenous Experience (ALIVE), National Survey on Drug Use and Health (NSDUH), Population Assessment of Tobacco and Health (PATH)
title: COVID-19 Pandemic and Substance Use

description: COVID-19 Psychological Impact Survey:  is an 3-item self-report measure of the psychological impacts of COVID-19 and associated risk factors. Adapted from Browning, M. H. E. M., Larson, L. R., Sharaievska, I., Rigolon, A., McAnirlin, O., Mullenbach, L.,Cloutier, S., Vu, T. M., Thomsen, J., Reigner, N., Metcalf, E. C., D'Antonio, A., Helbich, M.,Bratman, G. N., & Alvarez, H. O. (2021). COVID-19 Psychological Impact Survey [Database record]. Retrieved from PsycTESTS. https://dx.doi.org/10.1037/t78908-000
title: COVID-19 Psychological Impact Survey

description: The COVID-19 Survey Beliefs assesses subjects' moods, agreement, and opinions regarding COVID-19. The survey also includes general demographic information and subject history pertaining to COVID-19
title: COVID-19 Survey Beliefs

description: The COVID-19 Symptoms and Testing Experience (STE) is a module within the Johns Hopkins COVID-19 Community Response Survey which helps researchers understand how the novel coronavirus (COVID-19) pandemic affected people’s lives. The STE specifically examines the participants' own experiences with COVID-19 and the different symptoms and testing they went through during the pandemic.
title: COVID-19 Symptoms and Testing Experience

description: Evaluates perceptions around safety, efficacy and ill-intent of COVID-19 vaccines among participants in the CHASING COVID Cohort, DOI: 10.3390/vaccines11111665. https://cunyisph.org/wp-content/uploads/V12_Revised-051823.pdf
title: COVID-19 Vaccine Perceptions Survey

description: Informed by established measures of worry, the COVID-19 Worry Index is a 15-item measure developed by the current research team to specifically assess worry about contracting COVID-19 related symptoms and associated health consequences. This measures assesses worry specific to the COVID-19 pandemic across various topics, including but not limited to, health, social relationships, and finances. For each item, participants were asked to rate how worried they feel about each item on a scale from 1 (Not at all) to 10 (A great deal). DOI: 10.1016/j.brat.2020.103715
title: COVID-19 Worry Index

description: Evaluates COVID-19 status and vaccination status in addition to vaccine hesitancy among those who reported not being vaccinated
title: COVID-19 and Vaccination Status

CTAS is a questionnaire that checks the patient’s current understanding of some of the basic concepts of cognitive therapy.

description:
The Caffeine Questionnaire (Supplemental Beverage Questions) is a list of 13 caffeine-containing beverages available to supplement the general Food Frequency Questionnaire on the dietary intake of caffeine. It can also be used independently of the general FFQ.

Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center. (2004). Caffeine questionnaire. Retrieved from
http://sharedresources.fhcrc.org/documents/caffeine-questionnaire

Reference: Song YJ, Kristal AR, Wicklund KG, Cushing-Haugen KL, Rossing MA.  Coffee, tea, colas, and risk of epithelial ovarian cancer. Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev 2008; 17(3): 712-6.
title: Caffeine Questionnaire

description: Calgary Depression Rating Scale
title: Calgary Depression Rating Scale

description: California Bullying Victimization Scale - Retrospective (CBVS-R) (Green et al., 2018) is an adaptation of the CBVS designed to retrospectively assess bullying among adults. In addition to assessing the core components of bullying (repetition, intentionality, imbalance of power), the CBVS-R includes questions about times in childhood when the respondents experienced bullying. California Bullying Victimization Scale (CBVS) (Felix et al., 2011) assesses youth bullying victimization and aggression by asking youth about their experiences with each of the core components of bullying without using the term "bullying." DOI: 10.1037/pas0000592 and 10.1002/ab.20389
title: California Bullying Victimization Scale

description: The California Psychological Inventory Socialization Scale (CPI-Socialization Scale) indexes prosocial behavior. Participants respond to 46-items with either True or False. Low scores on this scale (<30) suggest a tendency toward norm violation, impulsive decision-making, and lack of guilt for social offenses. DOI: 10.2466/pr0.1994.75.1.651
title: California Psychological Inventory Socialization Scale

description: The California Psychotherapy Alliance Scale (CALPAS) was designed to assess four theoretically derived alliance dimensions, divided among four scales: Patient Commitment (PC), reflecting the therapeutic alliance; Patient Working Capacity (PWC), reflecting the working alliance; Therapist Understanding and Involvement (TUI), reflecting the therapist's contribution to the alliance; and Working Strategy Consensus (WGSC), reflecting the agreement on the goals and tasks of therapy. https://doi.org/10.1037/t02049-000
title: California Psychotherapy Alliance Scale (CALPAS)

title: California Verbal Learning Test Part 1

description: California Verbal Learning Test Third Edition is the most comprehensive assessment of verbal learning and memory for older adolescents and adults. In the CVLT3, an examinee listens to series of words and is then asked to recall the terms and the category to which they belong. This structure consists of the "Summary" elements
title: California Verbal Learning Test V3 - Summary

title: Camberwell Assessment of Need Short Appraisal Schedule

description: The Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q) is a self-report measure of social camouflaging behaviours in adults. It may be used to identify autistic individuals who do not currently meet diagnostic criteria due to their ability to mask their autistic proclivities. https://embrace-autism.com/cat-q/
title: Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q)

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21163593/
title: Cannabis Expectancy Questionnaire

description: The Cannabis Experiences Questionnaire (CEQ) was developed to investigate the subjective effects of cannabis use. It has been particularly useful in studying the association between cannabis consumption and psychosis-like experiences. DOI: 10.1159/000092678
title: Cannabis Experiences Questionnaire

description:
Questionnaire about an individual's cannabis use. Original version of CUDIT and revised version of CUDIT are included.
DOI: 10.1016/j.drugalcdep.2010.02.017
DOI: 10.1080/0959523031000154454
title: Cannabis Use Disorder Identification Test

description: The Cannabis Use Norms measures the relationships among perceived social norms, social outcome expectancies, and marijuana use and related consequences. Descriptive norms and expectancies were both positively associated with marijuana-related consequences, but, at high levels of both of these variables, injunctive norms were negatively associated with consequences. Results highlight the importance of distinguishing between descriptive and injunctive norms and between marijuana use and related consequences. https://doi.org/10.1037/0893-164x.22.3.433
title: Cannabis Use Norms

description: The CUPIT was designed to stimulate thought and discussion with cannabis users around the hazards of cannabis use, a valuable component in a health/lifestyle interview. Bashford, J., Flett, R. and Copeland, J. (2010), The Cannabis Use Problems Identification Test (CUPIT): development, reliability, concurrent and predictive validity among adolescents and adults. description:

The participants are prompted to respond whether the new square is the same color as the original in its location. The responses are used to create an estimate for the participants' working memory capacity.

description: Change in Fast Food Consumption during COVID-19: This item measures changes in fast food consumption as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic. For scoring, the five responses are to be recoded, such that "a lot more" and "a bit more" receive a value of 3, "about the same" receives a value of 2, and "a little less" and "a lot less" receive a value of 1. Those who answer "Do not consume" are treated as missing. Other research questions included in the survey include food purchasing behaviors and food acquisition methods. Chenarides, L., Grebitus, C., Lusk, J. L., & Printezis, I. (2021). Food consumption behavior during the COVID‐19 pandemic. Agribusiness, 37(1), 44-81. https://doi.org/10.1002%2Fagr.21679

description: The CDS is a 14-item child-rated measure of disgust sensitivity, with items scored on a 3-point scale ranging from 0–2. The Disgust Avoidance score is derived from the mean of 9 items (range=0–2); the Disgust Affect score is derived from the mean of 5 reverse-scored items (range=0–2). description: The Children’s Hope Scale (CHS) is a measure of youth hopefulness and can be helpful in tracking hope throughout treatment. The CHS asks youth to report on their ability to generate paths toward goals and persevere toward those goals. The CHS measures pathways (i.e., children who can identify a means to carry out goals) and agency (i.e., the ability to initiate and sustain action towards goals). Respondents rate “how they are” in the situations listed (e.g., “I am doing just as well as other kids my age”) on a scale from “None of the time” to “All of the time”. DOI: 10.1093/jpepsy/22.3.399

description: The CFM-MF is a 26-item measure of specific phobia, with subscales that reflect the DSM-5 diagnostic criteria. The measure consists of a screener item (i.e., Please check the box of the object or situation you are most afraid of), which allows for the identification of most feared stimuli, followed by 25 items assessing reactions to the feared stimuli. Participants rate their agreement with each of the 25 statements on  5-point Likert scale from 0 (Strongly disagree) to 5 (Strongly agree), with higher scores reflecting greater fear responding to the most feared stimuli. The CFM-MF has demonstrated high internal consistency (a = .94), as well as good criterion validity with a measure of functional impairment (McCraw & Valentiner, 2015).
title: Circumscribed Fear Measure, Most Feared

description:
Clean-up: only the codes listed under "Individual code proportions" on both sheets and "global codes" on the parent discipline sheet.
Parent and child were told by an assessor that it was time to put toys used in another task away into a bin. Parents were instructed to have their child put toys away on their own. Once the task begins, the dyad is given three minutes to clean up the toys. At the end of three minutes, the assessor let the dyad know that they would move on to another task. These 3-minute interactions are recorded and later coded by a team of reliable coders. Please refer to the coding manual to see how items for this interaction were coded.
title: Clean-Up Coding System

description: Motivational Interviewing (MI) is a counseling approach that is person centered, collaborative and focused on strengthening a client’s internal motivation and commitment to change. The Client Experiences of Motivational Interviewing (CEMI) is a 16 item self-report measure aimed at assessing client perceptions of clinician use of MI across two factors. The CEMI also may provide a way to assess the quality of MI delivered to clients. Participants use a four point Likert-type scale (1 = not at all to 4 = a great deal) to rate the degree to which the MI counselor demonstrated each of 16 behaviors during their most recent session. Negative items are reverse scored and higher CEMI scores represent more MI consistent behavior. https://doi.org/10.1177/0748175614544687
title: Client Experiences of Motivational Interviewing (CEMI)

description:
The Clinical Anger Scale (CAS), is an objective self-report instrument designed to measure the syndrome of clinical anger. Participants are given a cluster of items and asked to identify the statement that best reflects how they feel. Each cluster of statements was scored on a 4-point Likert scale, with A = 0, B = 1, C = 2, and D = 3. Subjects' responses on the CAS were summed so that higher scores corresponded to greater clinical anger (21 items; range 0 - 63). CAS scores is accomplished through the following interpretive ranges: 0-13 - minimal clinical anger; 14-19 - mild clinical anger; 20-28 - moderate clinical anger; and 29-63 - severe clinical anger.
DOI: 10.1002/1097-4679(199503)51:2<215::aid-jclp2270510211>3.0.co;2-z
title: Clinical Anger Scale (CAS)

description: The Co-Dampening and Co-Enhancing Questionnaire (CoDEQ) is an 18 item questionnaire developed to assess interpersonal responses to positive affect. Items were constructed to assess interpersonal dampening and enhancing responses to happy feelings within dyads. In the questionnaire instructions, respondents are asked to answer the questions according to their same-sex best friend. Respondents have to indicate how often they respond in the described way when one of them feels glad or happy and they are talking about this. The rating scale has four response options: almost never (1), sometimes (2), often (3), and almost always (4). DOI: 10.1007/s10802-017-0296-3

description: The cold pressor test is a cardiovascular test performed by immersing the hand into an ice water container, usually for one minute, and measuring changes in blood pressure and heart rate. These changes relate to vascular response and pulse excitability. Other measures can also be obtained from the cold pressor such as pain threshold and pain tolerance. This is done by requiring a participant to place their hand in the cold pressor for as long as they can. Once pain is present, they let the researcher know. Once the pain is unbearable, the participant removes their hand. This provides a measure of threshold (first feeling pain) and tolerance (total time minus threshold).
title: Cold Pressor Test (CPT)

description:
The Collaborative Initiative on Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders (CIFASD) is a multidisciplinary consortium of domestic and international projects established by NIAAA in 2003 to address prevention of FASD, diagnosis of the full range of birth defects associated with prenatal alcohol exposure, and ameliorative interventions for affected individuals. CIFASD aims to accelerate the translation of key research findings by fostering collaboration and by coordinating clinical, basic, and translational research. The Child Health Questionnaire section measures the participant's child's health and health care access. 
https://www.cifasd.org/

description: The modified Colorado Symptom Index (CSI) is a 14-item, self-report scale designed to assess frequency of positive mood and cognitive symptoms. Higher scores equate to greater pathology.

description: The Coronavirus Racial Bias Scale (CRBS) is a brief self-report questionnaire developed by Fordham University’s Center for Ethics Education and Applied Developmental Psychology Program as part of the Pathways to Health Study. Each item on the CRBS is rated on a 4-point scale, from 1 (strongly disagree) to 4 (strongly agree).  Fisher, C. B., Tao, X., & Yip, T. (2020). The Effects of Coronavirus Victimization Distress and Coronavirus Racial Bias on Mental Health Among Black, Indigenous and Latinx Young Adults in the United States.
title: Coronavirus Racial Bias Scale

description: The Cost of Care Index (CCI) has been developed as a case management tool to assist professionals in family assessments and to identify actual or perceived problem areas of families in the care of elderly relatives. The CCI has been used for family screening, intervention, and peer group interaction. The five dimensions of four items each are labeled Personal and Social Restrictions, Physical and Emotional Health, Value, Provocateur, and Economic. https://doi.org/10.1093/geront/26.3.273

description: The CEQ is a self-report measure designed to assess two key aspects of treatment belief: credibility, the perceived logic and plausibility of the treatment, and expectancy, the patient’s belief about the likelihood that the treatment will help. Higher scores indicate higher cultural humility. https://doi.org/10.1037/t29547-000

description:
The Cultural Missed Opportunities Measure (CMO) assess whether clients felt that their therapist adequately addressed their cultural background within the sessions. Participants rate whether their therapist was responsive and prompted/promoted dialogue during the session on a scale from 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree).

DOI: 10.1037/t48958-000
title: Cultural Missed Opportunities Measure

description: Culture Orientation Scale - A 16-item scale designed to measure four dimensions of collectivism and individualism: Vertical Collectivism – seeing the self as a part of a collective and being willing to accept hierarchy and inequality within that collective; Vertical Individualism – seeing the self as fully autonomous, but recognizing that inequality will exist among individuals and that accepting this inequality; Horizontal Collectivism –seeing the self as part of a collective but perceiving all the members of that collective as equal.; Horizontal Individualism –seeing the self as fully autonomous, and believing that equality between individuals is the ideal. All items are answered on a 9-point scale, ranging from 1= never or definitely no and 9 = always or definitely yes.
title: Cultural Orientation Scale

description:
The Cumulative Adversity Interview is a well-validated measure shown to be predictive of physical and mental health problems in large population-based studies. The CAI is a semistructured interview that assesses 140 events encompassing childhood and adult trauma, recent or major adverse life events, and chronic stress including events pertaining to violence, death and loss of loved ones, natural disasters, work/job, school and education, finance/income, relationship and marital status, and living environment (Turner et al, 1995). The CAI consists of four subscales: three subscales related to the objective count of CA (major life events, recent life events, and life trauma) resulting from both external forces and subjects' own behaviors and a chronic stress subscale pertaining to subjective response to stress and adversity
doi: 10.1038/npp.2013.250
title: Cumulative Adversity Interview

description: Participants complete a series of IATs (Implicit Association Tasks) and mood/suicidal ideation items (partly based on the PANAS). IATs consistently held me=positive or me=negative blocks first and included: death/life, worthy/worthlessness, stay/escape. At baseline, these are completed before and after the Cyberball task along with HRV and impedance physiology scores. An image-based self-injury cutting/not cutting IAT was also administered post-Cyberball. The order of tasks at baseline are: 1. Resting baseline HRV and impedance physiology (5 min, sitting quietly and still); 2. Pre-Cyberball IATs; 3. Pre-Cyberball mood/suicidal ideation items; 4. Cyberball with HRV and impedance physiology measures during the three periods (Full Inclusion, Partial Exclusion, Full Exclusion); 5. Post-Cyberball IATs and self-injury image; 6. Post-Cyberball mood/suicidal ideation items; 7. Need Threat Scale. At followup visits, participants complete the IATs followed by the mood items, without Cyberball.
title: Cyberball, Implicit Association, and Mood Task

description: Evaluates experience and occurrence of cyberbullying
title: Cyberbullying Victimization Index

description:
The DSM-5 Level 1 Cross-Cutting Symptom Measure is a self- or informant-rated measure that assesses mental health domains that are important across psychiatric diagnoses. In addition, the measure may be used to track changes in the individual’s symptom presentation over time.

description: The DSM-5 Level 1 Cross-Cutting Symptom Measure is a self- or informant-rated measure that assesses mental health domains that are important across psychiatric diagnoses. description: The DHS is a self-report measure that evaluates the relationship between hassles – defined as the irritating and frustrating stresses that make up one’s everyday interactions in their environment – and health by asking participants to recall how frequently they experienced a specific hassle in the past month.
title: Daily Hassles Scale (DHS)

description:
The Daily Inventory of Stressful Events (DISE) is an interview-based approach to the measurement of multiple aspects of daily stressors through daily interviews. The DISE consists of four components: a list of seven “stem” items that pertain to
occurrences of stressful events in various life domains; a series of open-ended probe questions that ascertain a description of the stressful event; a question regarding the perceived severity of the stressor; and a list of structured primary appraisal questions, inquiring about goals and values that were “at risk” because of the event.
title: Daily Inventory of Stressful Events

description: Daily Life Experiences: The Daily Life Experiences (DLE) scale is a subscale of the Racism and Life Experiences Scale (RaLes). The DLE is a self-report measure that assesses daily hassles or the frequency of “microaggressions” because of race in the past year.
title: Daily Life Experiences

description: The Daily Memory Lapses in Adults assessed retrospective and prospective memory lapses as well as the impact of these lapses (how irritating, interfering, and consequential). Affect was assessed using ratings of daily positive and negative affect. https://doi.org/10.1093/geronb/gbx012
title: Daily Memory Lapses in Adults

description:
Adapted from the original assessment developed to aid in the diagnosis and evaluation of DSM-IV Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD), this notes the degree to which participants experienced each of the problems listed on a daily basis.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16172836/

description:
This is a translationally valid  two-step decision making task developed by Daw and colleagues. In this sequential two-stage task, participants choose between one of two choices in each stage, leading to a rewarded or non-rewarded outcome of varying probabilities. In the first stage, choices are associated with a transition to a likely (70%) or unlikely (30%) state, where participants make their final selection and receive feedback. In this task, model free, or habitual, decision-making would result in the repetition of a previously rewarded action, even if the transition was unlikely; whereas model-based, or goal-directed, decision-making considers the likelihood of the transition for future decision making. 
DOI: 10.1016/j.neuron.2011.02.027
title: Daw Two Step Task

description: The Decisional Balance for PrEP is a self-report measure designed to assess individuals’ perceived pros and cons of initiating pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for HIV prevention. It is used in behavioral health and HIV prevention research to predict readiness, inform intervention strategies, and support shared decision-making in clinical contexts.

description: Delay of Gratification: This experiment measures a child’s self control skills and ability to delay responding.The child is told the examiner is going to leave the room to get ready for the next activity and if they wait without eating/touching the reward or standing up then they will receive the larger reward, but if they prefer not to wait they could ring the bell to summon the examiner immediately and would then receive only the smaller reward. The rewards are left on the table in front of the child and the DVs are the duration children wait and whether they pass the entire delay duration (5 minutes for 4-5 year olds; 10 minutes for 6 year olds)
title: Delay of Gratification (10 min)

description:
Neutral and rejection/abandonment scripts recorded based on past work (Limberg, Barnow, Freyberger, & Hamm, 2011) rephrased to be in the 2nd person. The grocery shopping script was revised to pertain to laundry, given that the COVID-19 may have influenced the neutrality of that particular scenario. Each was recorded to last 45-50 sec, and participants were instructed to imagine the situation vividly for an additional 90 sec (2.5min total). Participants received pseudorandomized sets of scripts across the time points.
Per past work (Koenigsberg et al., 2009; Ochsner et al., 2004), we coached participants to respond to emotions as they naturally would (maintain) or to adopt a third-person, detached perspective (decrease). Participants were presented with a neutral script first, then two negative scripts, counterbalanced with maintain or decrease first and second.
title: Deliberate Emotion Regulation Scale

description: Thandi is a WOMAN. For the last several weeks Thandi has been feeling really down. She wakes up in the morning with a sad mood and heavy feeling that stick with her all day long. She isn’t enjoying things the way she normally would. In fact nothing seems to give him/her pleasure. Even when good things happen, they don’t seem to make Thandi happy. The smallest tasks are difficult to accomplish. She finds it hard to concentrate on anything. She feels out of energy, out of steam and cannot do things she usually does. And even though Thandi feels tired, when night comes she can’t go to sleep. Thandi feels pretty worthless, very discouraged, and guilty. Thandi’s family has noticed that she has lost appetite and weight. She has pulled away from them and just doesn’t feel like talking.

description: The DY-BOCS consists of a series of semi-structured scales for assessing the presence and severity of the various OCD symptom dimensions, and includes a self-report version and a clinician-rated version. The DY-BOCS self-report is divided into six different OC symptom dimensions: (1) Aggression, (2) Sexual/Religious, (3)  Symmetry, (4) Contamination, (5) Hoarding, and (6) Miscellaneous. In addition to the symptom checklist, the DY-BOCS self-report also includes items that ask the patient to assess the overall symptom severity in each of the dimensions for the previous week. Expert ratings of symptom severity involve reviewing the symptoms endorsed in each dimension. Severity for each dimension is measured on three ordinal scales with six anchor points that focus on symptom frequency, the amount of distress they cause, and the degree to which they interfered with functioning during the previous week. 10.1038/sj.mp.4001798
title: Dimensional Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (DY-BOCS)

description:
The Dimensions of Discipline Inventory (DDI) provides information on 26 of the most
frequently used discipline behaviors of parents, such as explaining, deprivation of privileges, and spanking. The DDI also includes scales to measure aspects of context and modes of administration, and parents’ cognitive appraisal of discipline behaviors. A strength of the DDI is that it is both comprehensive and brief. It may be the only instrument to provide information on such a wide range of discipline behaviors and other dimensions that interact with discipline behaviors by parents. There are three DDI forms: Form P is for completion by parents.
Form A is an adult-recall form in which adults provide data on the discipline behaviors by their
parents when they were children. Form C is a child-report form in which children describe the current discipline behaviors of their parents.
title: Dimensions of Discipline Inventory (DDI)

description:
The Drinking trial alcohol return pour calculator is used after each alcohol self-administration session, which occurs twice during each drinking trial, to calculate the amount of alcohol that was consumed by the subject. This form separates the volume of alcohol and mixer that is in each of the 8 drinks so we can estimate the amount of standard drinks they consumed.

The Drinking trial schedule of assessments is used as a time log to keep track of blood alcohol level as well as the completion of alcohol craving self-report forms Alcohol Urge Questionnaire, Visual Analog Scale, and Biphasic Alcohol Effects Scale throughout the drinking trial. This document is utilized to make sure BAC and the alcohol craving forms are completed at the correct time interval.
title: Drinking trial alcohol return pour calculator and schedule of assessments

description: EEG trigger code refers to discrete numeric/event markers embedded into EEG recordings (typically via a dedicated trigger or “TRIG” channel) that time-lock the onset of experimental events (stimulus presentation, responses, task cues) to the EEG signal. These trigger codes allow researchers to segment and analyze EEG data with respect to meaningful events in cognitive, perceptual, or clinical paradigms, enabling computations like event-related potentials (ERPs) or task-locked time-frequency analyses.

description:
Participants are asked to make a decision between completing an easy button pressing task for $1 or a more difficult button pressing task for a higher reward. The easy trials require participants to press buttons 10 times in order to pop the balloon, while the hard task requires 100 button presses to pop the balloon. Participants complete 25 trials, 5 trials at each reward level ($3, $4, $5, $6, and $7). Outcome variables include the percentage of time they choose the hard task at each of the reward levels, the total # of trials, and the total # of difficult trials chosen across all reward levels. 
Reddy, L. F., Horan, W. P., Barch, D. M., Buchanan, R. W., Dunayevich, E., Gold, J. M., ... & Young, J. W. (2015). Effort-based decision-making paradigms for clinical trials in schizophrenia: part 1—psychometric characteristics of 5 paradigms. Schizophrenia bulletin, 41(5), 1045-1054.
title: Effort Expenditure Balloon Task

description: The EMBU-C/P (Castro et al., 1993; Castro et al., 1997) are self-report questionnaires, adapted from the original EMBU (Egna Minnen Beträffande Uppfostran; Swedish for "My Memories of Upbringing"), that measure perceptions of parental child-rearing practices in areas such as rejection, overprotection, emotional warmth. The EMBU-P asks parents to rate their own behavior with their children and the EMBU-C measures youths' perceptions of their parents' rearing practices. The 40-item Modified EMBU (Muris et al., 2003) also includes items about anxious rearing behaviors. DOI: 10.1177/002076409303900105 (EMBU); 10.1007/BF00788243 (EMBU-P); 10.1023/A:1025894928131 (EMBU-C)

description:
Dyad Puzzle task: Parent & child were provided w/ a foam puzzle w/ all of the pieces in a bag. Parents were instructed to dump the pieces onto the table & to allow their child to try to complete the puzzle & give them whatever help they needed. The dyad was given 5 mins to work on the task together. 
Unsolvable puzzle: Child is instructed to sit at a table & given a wooden shape sorter box. A wooden block is placed into the shape sorter to demonstrate to the child how to complete the task. The parent is asked to sit behind the child & to not interact w/ them during this task. The child is then given 10 blocks & told to put them into the right holes on the shape sorter in order to get a prize. 6 of the 10 pieces presented to the child are too large & will not fit into the shape sorter, but the child is not told this. Once all the pieces are placed on the table, the child may begin working on the task. After a period of 5mins, the task is ended.
