description:
Experimental Recombination Paradigm (ERP): The ERP is a performance-based task used to assess prospection and episodic memory. For each trial, participants are asked to either recall a past or imagine a future event in response to personalized person-place-object cues in as much detail as possible. Participants are given 3 minutes per trial, and their audio-recorded event descriptions are coded for number of internal details (information about the central event described) and external details (information not directly relevant to the central event described, or general semantic information) provided. 
Reference: Addis DR, Pan L, Vu MA, Laiser N, Schacter DL. Constructive episodic simulation of the future and the past: Distinct subsystems of a core brain network mediate imagining and remembering. Neuropsychologia. 2009;47(11):2222-2238. doi:10.1016/j.neuropsychologia.2008.10.026. PMID: 19041331
title: Experimental Recombination Paradigm

description:
The Extended Strengths and Weaknesses Assessment of Normal Behavior (E-SWAN) has been developed in order to capture variance associated with both strengths and weaknesses to generate a near-normal distribution of ratings in epidemiological samples. The E-SWAN is a collaborative effort focused on the extension the framework of Strengths and Weakness of ADHD Symptoms and Normal Behavior (SWAN) (Swanson et al, 2000 and Swanson et al., 2012), a preferred measure for measuring positive attention and impulse regulation behaviors that measures more variance at the adaptive end of ADHD symptoms.
The current focus is on the extension of the SWAN framework to a broader range of disorders. Questionnaires have been developed for major depressive disorder, panic disorder, social anxiety, and disruptive mood dysregulation disorder (DMDD).
title: Extended Strengths and Weaknesses Assessment of Normal Behavior DMDD

description:
The Family History Assessment Module (FHAM) was created by individuals working at Washington University as part of the COGA project (collaborative studies on genetics of alcoholism). It is designed to assess major psychiatric disorders (drug dependence, alcoholism, mania, depression, antisocial personality, and schizophrenia) in relatives of the person being interviewed. Eleven questions are asked, and for every question that is answered positively, the interviewer records the name of the relative and their relationship to the participant. Each positive answer is also followed up with an Individual Assessment Module (IAM) for each family member that was listed in the FHAM.

Rice, J. P., Reich, T., Bucholz, K., Neuman, R. J., Fishman, R., Rochberg, N., Hesselbrock, V. M., Numberger, J. I., Shuckit, M. A., & Begleiter, H. (1995). Comparison of Direct Interview and Family History Diagnoses of Alcohol Dependence. Alcoholism: Clinical and Experimental Research, 19, 1018-1023.

description:
The Feelings and Me Questionnaire lists a wide range of responses that can be deployed when feeling sad. The items sample four types of responses through which changes in dysphoric affect are eventuated, namely: behavioral or instrumental strategies, cognitive strategies, interpersonal strategies, and somatic-sensory responses focusing on the physical self. Adaptive or functional responses are operationally defined as those likely to attenuate dysphoric affect in appropriate ways. maladaptive or dysfunctional responses were defined as those likely to exacerbate sadness in the short or the long run.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291709005789

description:
The Feighner Diagnostic Criteria diagnoses adult psychiatric illnesses in five stages: Clinical Description, Laboratory Studies, Delimitation from Other Disorders, Follow-Up Study, and Family Study. In contrast, the American Psychiatric Association Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-II) diagnoses based on the "'best clinical judgement and experience' of a committee and its consultants." The 14 psychiatric diagnoses include Primary Affective Disorder, Secondary Affective Disorders, Schizophrenia, Anxiety Neurosis, Obsessive-Compulsive Neurosis, Phobic Neurosis, Hysteria, Antisocial Personality Disorder, Alcoholism, Drug Dependence, and, Anorexia Nervosa.
https://doi.org/10.1001/archpsyc.1972.01750190059011
title: Feighner Diagnostic Criteria for Use in Psychiatric Research

description:
The Food Choice Task was adapted from the work of Hare et al., 2009 to include images with a greater range of caloric density and macronutrient content. The food items were chosen to be representative of a range of dietary choices, instead of emphasizing “junk foods” and “healthy snack foods,” as in the original task. In each of two blocks, 43 food items were presented on a computer screen, of which 25 were low-fat and 18 were high-fat (images and list of food items available in Supplemental Materials, image files available upon request). Items with greater than 30% of calories derived from fat were categorized as “high-fat.” In the first block, participants provided ratings of how healthy each of a series of images of food were; in the second block, the participants rated how tasty each image was. There was no time limit for responding.
DOI: 10.1126/science.1168450

description: This inventory consists of 35 questions measuring four sub-scales of perfectionism: Concern over mistakes and doubts about actions. Excessive concern with parents’ expectations and evaluation. Excessively high personal standards. Concern with "precision, order and organization". Setting excessively high standards is the most prominent feature of perfectionism, accompanied by tendencies for overly critical evaluations of one’s own behavior, expressed in over concern for mistakes and uncertainty regarding actions and beliefs. This scale can be useful in highlighting that being highly perfectionist is in fact unhelpful. This test is appropriate for people 15 years and older and is especially appropriate for individuals with critical parents, anorexia, OCD traits or an anxiety disorder. Perfectionistic beliefs may be an important underlying cause of a range of psychopathologies.
title: Frost Multidimensional Perfectionism Scale

description: Future Thinking is the process of imagining or anticipating future events, actions, or outcomes; it involves mental time travel, where individuals project themselves into the future to plan, set goals, or anticipate challenges. Future Experiences is the cognitive and emotional process of imagining or predicting future events or experiences; it explores how individuals think about and emotionally respond to hypothetical or anticipated future situations. The Future Thinking Task is a cognitive assessment used to evaluate an individual's ability to project themselves into the future and generate potential future events. Participants are typically asked to think of positive and negative future scenarios within different timeframes (e.g., days, months, or years ahead) and describe them in detail. 10.1111/j.2044-8260.1998.tb01394.x; 10.1037//0021-843x.105.2.286; 10.1017/s003329179600459x

description: The Full GOASSESS (Calkins et al., 2014, 2015) is a computerized structured interview designed to be administered by trained assessors. While other clinical assessment tools are devoted to specific disorders and may vary in their concentration on disorder-specific symptoms, the GOASSESS comprehensively assesses a broader spectrum of major psychopathology domains. The GOASSESS is abbreviated and modified from the epidemiologic version of the NIMH Genetic Epidemiology Research Branch Kiddie-SADS (Merikangas et al, 2009). Modifications included: (a) addition of the diagnostic screening questions from the adolescent version of the World Health Organization Composite International Diagnostic Interview (CIDI) (Merikangas et al., 2009); (b) less restrictive criteria to complete the diagnostic modules; and (c) derivation of dimensional ratings of distress and impairment associated with symptoms in each diagnostic section. https://doi.org/10.1002/wps.20152 and https://doi.org/10.1111/jcpp.12416

description: The Goldsmiths Musical Sophistication Index (Gold-MSI) is a self-report inventory assessing individual differences in musical sophistication. It measures the ability to engage with music in a flexible, effective and comprehensive way. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0089642
title: Goldsmiths Musical Sophistication Index

description: Good emotional self-control measures capacity to plan, regulate emotions, and persist toward goals versus  tendency toward impulsivity, distractibility, and emotional dysregulation. From https://doi.org/10.1521/jscp.2012.31.6.568 and includes excerpts from the Self-Control Rating Scale (SCRS) https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-006X.47.6.1020
title: Good Emotional Self-Control

description:
The Good Health Practices Scale is a 16-tem assessment which measured participants' wellness. 
https://doi.org/10.1177/1359105316687629
title: Good Health Practices Scale

description: In this task, participants are asked to push the button with their right index finger when presented with images of cities, but to withhold their press when presented with images of mountains. The task is preceded by instructions and 20 seconds of practice; there is one block, 360 seconds in duration, 10% target (i.e., mountains) probability. Each trial is 0.8 seconds long with 16 frames per trial (i.e., rate = 0.05s) such that each image “fades” into the next. Adapted from Esterman et al. (2012), Rosenberg et al. (2013), and Rosenberg et al. (2016).
title: Gradual Onset Continuous Performance Task

description:
Participants were asked about their alcohol use on a graduated frequency approach where they were asked on how many days that had a specific number of drinks for the past month or past year, depending on how often they report drinking. These items can be used to create frequency and continuous volume of alcohol use. https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/009145090903600320
https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1360-0443.1994.tb00957.x
title: Graduated Drinking Frequencies and Continuous Volume of Alcohol

description: Gray Oral Reading Tests- Fifth Edition
title: Gray Oral Reading Tests

description: A 15-item questionnaire that asks the patient to rate the degree of avoidance of specific situations related to bereavement.
title: Grief-Related Avoidance Questionnaire

description:
The Griffith Empathy Measure (GEM; Dadds et al., 2008) is a brief parent rating measure of empathic behavior in children and adolescents. The GEM was adapted from Bryant’s Index of Empathy for Children and Adolescents (Bryant, 1982) and consists of 23 items using nine-point Likert scales.
https://doi.org/10.1037/t64021-000
title: Griffith Empathy Measure

description: The Grooved Pegboard is a manipulative dexterity test consisting of 25 holes with randomly positioned slots. Pegs with a key along one side must be rotated to match the hole before they can be inserted. This test requires more complex visual-motor coordination than most pegboard tests. Some common uses are student labs, screening procedures in industry and evaluating lateralized brain damage. The pegs are conveniently stored under the nameplate.
title: Grooved Pegboard Test

description:
study aimed to examine the psychometric properties of the seven-item Group Cohesiveness Scale (GCS) consists of cohesion and engagement elements derived on the process used in group therapy and its outcomes. The GCS uses three different forms: a group member form, a leader or co-leader form, and an observer form, and all three forms contained the same questions.
https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/j.1744-6163.2012.00342.x
title: Group Cohesiveness Scale

description: he Group-Based Medical Mistrust Scale (GBMMS) is a 12-item scale measuring race-based medical mistrust on a 5-point Likert scale from strongly disagree to strongly agree. It is scored by the sum of responses with higher scores indicating more mistrust. The scale has three subscales: suspicion, group disparities in healthcare, and lack of support. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ypmed.2003.09.041
title: Group-Based Medical Mistrust Scale (GBMMS)

description: Brief questionnaire about gun carriage and gun usage
title: Gun Carriage Questionnaire

description: HARK screens for intimate partner violence in the past year through yes/no questions. HARK is an acronym for four questions that represent the components of intimate partner violence (IPV): Humiliation, Afraid, Rape, and Kick.https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2296-8-49
title: HARK Abuse Assessment

description:
Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS) is a measure of current and past psychopathology. This KSADS version involves participants ages 12-21 as a self-report and parents of participants ages 5-17 as a parent-about-child report.
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dcn.2017.10.010

description:
The Faces, Legs, Activity, Cry, and Consolability (FLACC) scale is a behavioral tool used to assess pain in children that are unable to verbally report pain levels. The FLACC is a composite of 5 behaviors considered indicative of pain that can be detected and graded by an observer on a 0-2 scale.

The Familism Scale is a 15 item measure of familism under three constructs: familial obligations, support from the family, and family as referents. Items in the Familism Scale are rated using a 5-point Likert scale.

DOI: 10.1177/07399863870094003

Pain. 1993;52(2):157-68.

description: The General Sensory Sensitivity (GSS-8) measures a person's overall sensitivity to sensory stimuli across different modalities, including sight, sound, touch, smell, taste, and internal bodily sensations (interoception). Sensory sensitivity and symptom severity represent unique dimensions of chronic pain: a MAPP Research Network study. Pain 2018;159:2002-11.

description:
The Miller Behavioral Style Scale (MBSS) is a self-report questionnaire designed to measure an individual's information-seeking behavior in response to both physical and psychological stressors. The MBSS divides individuals into high and low monitoring or blunting based on their preferences for information or distraction, respectively, in a variety of stressful situations.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.52.2.345

description:
The Modified Yale Preoperative Anxiety Scale (m-YPAS) is used to assess the effectiveness of anxiety reduction interventions for children undergoing surgery. The assessment can be used for children aged two years or older, and is based on observations rather than reported responses.

The Postoperative Pain Measure for Parents is a behavioral measure assessing various changes in children following surgical procedures. description:
A case report form describing Quantitative Sensory Testing (QST) sessions for the TKA cohort in the A2CPS study.  Includes information about data availability and pain ratings.  A2CPS measures pressure pain thresholds (PPTs) at both the site of pain (surgery) and at a distant site, repeated Neuropen probe tests (temporal summation; TS) of pain, and the conditioned pain modulation (CPM) assessment to indirectly measure inhibitory pain modulation.

DOIs: 10.1016/j.pain.2013.05.047, 10.1186/s12891-016-1124-6, 10.1016/j.jpain.2019.11.013

description:
A case report form describing Quantitative Sensory Testing (QST) sessions for the thoracic cohort in the A2CPS study.  Includes information about data availability and pain ratings.  A2CPS measures pain evoked by tangential movement across the skin (dynamic mechanical allodynia; DMA), pressure pain thresholds (PPTs) at both the site of pain (surgery) and at a distant site, repeated Neuropen probe tests (temporal summation; TS) of pain, and the conditioned pain modulation (CPM) assessment to indirectly measure inhibitory pain modulation.

DOIs: 10.1016/j.pain.2013.05.047, 10.1186/s12891-016-1124-6, 10.1016/j.jpain.2019.11.013
title: HEAL Quantitative Sensory Testing Case Report Form (A2CPS Thoracic Cohort)

Simpatia, a term that captures the tendency to prefer and create social interactions characterized by warmth and emotional positivity while also avoiding conflict and overt negativity, is a relevant factor of Latino culture. description:
The scale includes five items adapted from previous measures of substance use stigma and perceived adverse effects of alcohol use in relation to taking medications (i.e., interactive toxicity beliefs).16 Items have been reframed to reflect the perception that drinking alcohol with HIV when taking Antiretroviral therapy (ART) should be socially condemned including evoking shame, attributing irresponsibility, prohibiting drinking, and restricting access to HIV treatment for those who drink.

The structure also includes the HIV Treatment Engagement, that evaluates antiretroviral (ARV) treatment adherence among participants in a study in Sub-Saharan Africa (South Africa).
title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy (ART) and Treatment (ARV) Survey

description:
Trial-by-trial behavioral dataset from a hierarchical maze habit-learning task, including subject/session metadata (GUID, subject ID, date, age, sex) and task variables (block, trial, stage, maze position/arm, reversal status, key press, correctness, reaction time, time elapsed, and condition). The structure is designed to quantify learning, reversal, and habit expression across avatars and positions, enabling analyses of speed/accuracy, learning curves, and post-break performance changes.
Source-not provided
title: Habits Formation Maze Task

description: The Hashtag T1T2 task is a speeded forced-choice response task in which participants are expected to indicate by button press whether they see a number '1' or '2' on the computer screen. They are expected to make this response as quickly as possible. We attempt to manipulate their reaction time with an occasional memory requirement, sometimes present sometimes absent. The primary dependent measure is the reaction time to the T2 stimulus. Also of interest is the condition being administered ("T2 only" or "T1T2"), the specific T1 and T2 stimuli (X, Y, #, and 1, 2), the accuracy of each response (T1 acc and T2 acc), the reaction time to T1, and the participant's diagnostic group (healthy control or person with schizophrenia).
title: Hashtag T1T2

description: The Hooked on Nicotine Checklist (HONC) is a 10-item instrument used to determine the onset and strength of tobacco dependence. The HONC is designed to identify the point at which an adolescent has lost full autonomy over their use of tobacco. This is defined by the authors as occurring when the sequelae of tobacco use, either physical or psychological, present a barrier to quitting. A positive response to any HONC item signals a loss of autonomy and the onset of dependence. The number of positive responses is proposed to reflect the degree of dependence. Although the HONC has only been used with adolescents so far, the authors will be testing its utility for adult smokers in the near future. https://cancercontrol.cancer.gov/brp/tcrb/measures-guide/hooked-on-nicotine-checklist
title: Hooked on Nicotine Checklist (HONC)

The HFIAS is composed of a set of nine questions that have been used in several countries and appear to distinguish food insecure from food secure households across different cultural contexts. The information generated by the HFIAS can be used to assess the prevalence of household food insecurity (access component) and to detect changes in the food insecurity situation of a population over time.

description: The Immigrant Stigma Scale (ISS), measures participants' identity and experience as an immigrant in the United States: what the participant thinks about immigrants, what the participant thinks the public thinks about immigrants, self-perceptions because of the participant's immigrant status, and reasons the participant respects themselves less. Items are rated from 1= Strongly disagree to 5= Strongly agree. The Immigrant Stigma Scale is based on the Corrigan Theory of Self Stigma, which states that “people who agree with the stigma of mental illness and apply it to themselves suffer lowered self-esteem and self-efficacy.” https://doi.org/10.1007/s10597-011-9445-2

description: The Impact of Event Scale–Revised (IES-R) is an easy-to-administer questionnaire used to evaluate the degree of distress a patient feels in response to trauma. The IES-R is a self-administered, 22-item questionnaire based on three clusters of symptoms identified in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, third edition (DSM-III), as indicators of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD).
title: Impact of Events Scale Revised (IES-R)

description: Assesses inappropriate and excessive guilt in children 7-14 years with hypothetical scenarios with negative outcomes and ambiguous fault (e.g., “You are late getting home. It is my fault.”) and the other reflecting excessive guilt (“Would you think: Just thinking about what I did would make me want to cry.”). Participants rate the statements in regard to how true it is to them on a 3-point Likert scale (0=not at all, 1=a little, 2=a lot).

description: The Interaction Rating Scale - IRS is used to measure the child’s social competence and the caregiver’s child rearing competence through five minute observations of caregiver-child interactions. The original Interaction Rating Scale composed of 70 items for a behavioral score and 11 items for an impression score, grouped into ten subscales. Five subscales focus on children’s social competences: 1) Autonomy, 2) Responsiveness, 3) Empathy, 4) Motor regulation, and 5) Emotional regulation. Another five items assess the caregiver’s parenting skills: 6) Respect for autonomy development, 7) Respect for responsiveness development, 8) Respect for empathy development, 9) Respect for cognitive development, and 10) Respect for social-emotional development. And one item assesses an overall impression of synchronous relationships. It was then adapted to the Short Form of Interaction Rating Scale Advanced (IRSA-Brief), that consist of 39 items.
title: Interaction Rating Scale

description:
The IIFAS was designed to assess maternal attitude towards infant feeding methods and to predict breastfeeding intention and exclusivity. The scale is composed of 17 items with a 5-point Likert scale ranging from 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). The total IIFAS score can range from 17 to 85 with higher scores reflecting positive attitude towards breastfeeding. Total IIFAS scores can be further categorized into groups: 1) positive to breastfeeding (IIFAS score 70–85), 2) neutral (IIFAS score 49–69), and 3) positive to formula feeding (IIFAS score 17–48).
Mora A, Russell D, Dungy C, Losch M, Dusdieker L. The Iowa infant feeding attitude scale: analysis of reliability and validity. J Appl Soc Psychol. 1999;29(11):2362–80.
title: Iowa Infant Feeding Attitudes Scale (IIFAS)

description:
Steel, P. D. G. (2002). The measurement and nature of procrastination. University of Minnesota.

Its validity and reliability are later published here: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886910000930#bib173

It is a 9 item self-report scale measuring the degree which an individual self-reports their procrastination. Raw scores have been validated to be interpretable to identify "High", "Medium" or "Low" procrastinators. It has been demonstrated to be highly correlated with other measures of procrastination such as the General Procrastination Scale, the Adult Inventory of Procrastination, and the Decisional Procrastination Questionnaire. It has been suggested to be used as a general measure of procrastination (Smith 2010)

Steel, P. (2010). Arousal, avoidant and decisional procrastinators: Do they exist?. Personality and Individual Differences, 48(8), 926-934.
title: Irrational Procrastination Scale

description: Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (K-SADS) Consensus Diagnosis refers to a diagnosis reached through a collaborative or consensus process among clinicians after reviewing all available data. Information is gathered from the K-SADS interview with integration of multiple perspectives, such as input from different clinicians, family members, and caregivers, as well as school reports and observations. In this process, professionals (such as psychiatrists, psychologists, or mental health workers) discuss the case to arrive at a unified diagnosis. This method helps ensure that the diagnosis is comprehensive, based on multiple perspectives, and less reliant on individual bias. The consensus is often based on the child's symptoms across different settings (e.g., home, school), developmental history, and clinical presentations.
title: Kiddie-SADS Consensus Diagnoses

description: The Kiddie-SADS-Present and Lifetime DSM-5 (K-SADS-PL-5) combines dimensional and categorical assessment approaches to diagnose current and past episodes of psychopathology in children and adolescents according to DSM-5 criteria. It is administered by interviewing the parent(s), the child, and finally achieving summary ratings which include all sources of information (parent, child, school, chart, and other). Agoraphobia is addressed in Section 3: Anxiety, Obsessive Compulsive, and Trauma-Related disorders. https://www.pediatricbipolar.pitt.edu/sites/default/files/KSADS_DSM_5_SCREEN_Final.pdf and https://www.pediatricbipolar.pitt.edu/sites/default/files/KSADS_DSM_5_Supp3_AnxietyDO_Final.pdf page 7

description:
The Klein Sexual Orientation Grid (or KSOG) is a system for describing a person's sexual proclivities in a way more detailed and informative than previous methods. It was introduced by Dr. Fritz Klein (1932-2006) in his book The Bisexual Option. The Klein Sexual Orientation Grid is shown in the table below. For each person, it sets out the seven component variables of sexual orientation, listed as A through G down the left side. The three columns indicate three different points at which sexual orientation is assessed: the person's past, their present, and their ideal. The person then receives a rating from 1 to 7 for each of the 21 resulting combinations, one rating for each empty box.
Klein, Fritz (1993). The Bisexual Option. The Haworth Press. p. 19. ISBN 978-1-56024-380-9.
title: Klein Sexual Orientation Grid (KSOG)

description:
The letter–number sequencing task is a working memory capacity measure that involves hearing a series of letters and digits, and then reporting back the stimuli with the letters in alphabetical order and digits in ascending numerical order. Results suggest that performance differences on the letter–number sequencing task may arise as a function of language background and task administration modality. 

https://doi.org/10.1002/acp.3468
title: Letter Number Sequencing

description: The Level of Personality Functioning Scale-Self Report (LPFS-SR) is an 80 item self-rated personality functioning assessment scale that corresponds directly to the DSM-5 alternative model of personality disorders, Criterion A. Each item asks the individual to rate how well the item describes him or her generally. It assesses four interrelated core functions of personality: Identity, Self-Direction, Empathy, and Intimacy. https://doi.org/10.1037/pas0000450
title: Level of Personality Functioning Scale

description: The Levels of Emotional Awareness Scale (LEAS) is a cognitive-developmental measure of emotion. 20 interpersonal scenarios (or 10 for the short version), constructed to elicit various emotions of different complexity, are described in a few sentences. Subjects write open-ended responses for "how would you feel?" and "how would the other person feel?" which are scored for the emotion described for self and other. The lowest score (Level 0) is for nonemotion responses; Level 4 scores represent two or more differentiated emotions. A "total" score of 5 may be given a scenes where both self- and other- responses were rated Level 4. Higher scores reflect responses with higher emotion based on specific criteria. https://doi.org/10.1080/00223891.1990.9674052
title: Levels of Emotional Awareness Scale (LEAS)

The Levenson Self-Report Psychopathy Scale was developed in 1995 for use in psychological research. It measures on two scales: primary psychopathy (psychopathic emotional affect) and secondary psychopathy (psychopathic lifestyle).
https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.68.1.151
title: Levenson Self-Report Psychopathy Scale

description: The Life Satisfaction Index (LSI) measures a participant’s current general satisfaction across multiple areas of life: sexual or marital relationships, family relationships, general level of happiness, place of residence, how life is going so far, and school and work situations. Higher scores on the LSI indicate greater levels of satisfaction It is one of four cross-section indices in the Global Appraisal of Individual Needs (GAIN), a multipurpose assessment used to accurately and efficiently identify a wide range of life problems among adolescents and adults in both clinical and general populations. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsat.2021.108414
title: Life Satisfaction Index

description: The Youth Life Stress Interview (LSI) assesses the nature and intensity of life stress using the contextual threat method. It is an adaptation of the Child Episodic Life Stress Interview (Rudolph & Hammen, 1999; Rudolph et al., 2000). Specific, detailed probes were used to elicit objective information separately from youth and caregivers about youths’ experience of episodic stressors across several life domains during the preceding year. For each life event, the subject and third-party raters use a 5-point scale to rate the objective stress or negative impact that would be associated with the event for a typical youth in the same circumstances. Events are also categorized by interpersonal or noninterpersonal. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0954579407070241
title: Life Stress Interview (LSI)

The 10 items of the LiCQ are designed to reflect pure loneliness items that are not confounded by hypothesized causes of loneliness. description:
The Longitudinal Interval Follow-Up Evaluation Eating and Feeding Disorders Version (LIFE-EAT 3) is a semi-structured interview that assesses the relative severity of diagnostic features of feeding and eating disorders over a pre-specified length of time determined by the study purpose. The assessor uses a calendar to collect detailed weekly data to track longitudinal fluctuations in eating disorder symptoms on a 6-point severity scale, ranging from "extreme" to "recovered." Assessors will use the LIFE-EAT to characterize the longitudinal course of A/R eating prior to the study and between study visits.
Eddy K. T., Murray, H.B., & Thomas, J. J. (2015). Longitudinal Interval Follow-Up Evaluation Eating and Feeding Disorders Version. (LIFE-EAT 3). Adapted from Keller et al., 1987.
title: Longitudinal Interval Follow-Up Evaluation Eating and Feeding Disorders Version (LIFE-EAT 3)

description: The Magical Thinking Questionnaire (MTQ) assesses the prevalence and intensity of magical thinking, particularly beliefs that seemingly unrelated thoughts or actions can influence outcomes. It consists of Thought and Action subscales; the Thought subscale asks whether it is possible to make some events happen just by thinking about it and the Action subscale asks whether it is possible by some action to make some events happen, the specified action being rationally or causally unrelated to the specified event. https://doi.org/10.1348/026151002760390819
title: Magical Thinking Questionnaire (MTQ)

description:
The Maltreatment and Abuse Chronology of Exposure (MACE) scale was developed to gauge severity of exposure to various types of maltreatment during each year of childhood. The MACE-X is the original, developmental version of the assessment, which consists of 75 items. The MACE-X asks age of exposure for each item, also inquires about dating violence, and whether specific events led to helplessness or terror.

This data structure contains part 1 of 2 of the MACE-X.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4340880/
title: Maltreatment and Abuse Chronology of Exposure (MACE-X) Part 1

The MACE-X asks age of exposure for each item, also inquires about dating violence, and whether specific events led to helplessness or terror.

This data structure contains part 2 of 2 of the MACE-X.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4340880/
title: Maltreatment and Abuse Chronology of Exposure (MACE-X) Part 2

description: Evaluates current and previous use of cannabis (up to two months prior to completing the questionnaire). Adapted from the DDQ: Lee, C. M., Kilmer, J. R., Neighbors, C., Atkins, D. C., Zheng, C., Walker, D. D., & Larimer, M. E. (2013). Indicated prevention for college student marijuana use: A randomized controlled trial. Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 81, 702-709.
title: Marijuana Behavior Questionnaire

description: The Marijuana Consequences Checklist (MCC) represents a range of negative consequences of marijuana use, specifically targeting a young adult audience. Higher scores on the MCC are associated with more frequent use and higher probability of meeting the cannabis use disorder. This checklist was designed to target marijuana-specific consequences from several other marijuana measures adapted from alcohol consequences: the Rutgers Marijuana Problem Index (RMPI, White et al 2005), the Marijuana Problem Inventory (MPI), and the Marijuana Adolescent Problem Inventory the Young Adult Alcohol Consequences Questionnaire (YAACQ, Read et al 2006). https://doi.org/10.1007/s11121-020-01171-x
title: Marijuana Consequences Checklist

description: The Marijuana Consumption Questionnaire (MCQ) assesses frequency and quantity of cannabis use, age of first use, and availability of cannabis. Frequency and quantity of use is collected according to mode of administration and is inclusive of smoking, vaporizing, edible (ie, capsules, food, drinks), topical, and concentrate (high potency THC) use. Respondents who endorse any use of a product type are then asked how many times per day, how much, and what proportions of THC and CBD are used. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9511952/
title: Marijuana Consumption Questionnaire

description: The Marijuana Craving Questionnaire (MCQ) was developed to study cravings of marijuana. It is based on the Craving and Mood Questionnaire (CMQ). Items are rated on a scale from 0-100. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1360-0443.2008.02292.x
title: Marijuana Craving Questionnaire

description: The Marijuana Dependence Scale (MDS) is a self-report of nine dependence criteria based on the DSM-III. Participants respond whether they have experienced each symptom in the past 3 months/90 days. Endorsements of symptoms are counted to yield the number of dependence criteria acknowledged, with the two tolerance and withdraw items each counting as a single criteria. See also the Marijuana Problem Scale (MPS, mj_ps01) https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-006X.68.5.898
title: Marijuana Dependence Scale (MDS)

description: Measures participant's attitude about marijuana use and effects.
title: Marijuana Effect Expectancy Questionnaire (MEEQ-B)

description: The Marijuana Motives Measure (MMM) was developed to examine motivations for marijuana use. The types of motives assessed include coping, conformity, social, enhancement, and expansion. DOI: 10.1037/0022-0167.45.3.265
title: Marijuana Motives Measure

description: Used to assess perceived descriptive marijuana use norms amongst a typical man/woman/person. https://doi.org/10.15288/jsa.1991.52.580
title: Marijuana Norms Rating Form

description: Measures frequency of marijuana-related problems.
title: Marijuana Problems Index

description: The Marijuana Problems Scale (MPS) is a widely used instrument that assesses negative consequences of cannabis use. Participants reported problems related to the use of marijuana, alcohol, and other drugs using a list of 19 negative psychological, social, occupational, and legal consequences (e.g., problems in your family, memory loss, procrastination). Endorsements of items on the list as either minor or serious problems were counted to create indices of the total number of problems (range = 0-19) for marijuana, alcohol, and other drug use.(mean alpha = .90), alcohol (mean alpha = .77), and other drug use (mean alpha = .92). See also the Marijuana Dependence Scale (MDS, mj_ds01) https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-006X.68.5.898
title: Marijuana Problems Scale (MPS)

description: The Marijuana Purchase Task (MPT) is a behavioral economic measure of individualized cannabis value (i.e., demand). The MPT follows purchase tasks for other substances (e.g., alcohol, tobacco), though presents with unique caveats due to its mixed illicit status, non-uniform units of purchase and use, and substantial within substance variability in strain, potency, and quality.
title: Marijuana Purchase Task (MPT)

description: Identifies how people feel about their own cannabis use. Measures stages of change in people who use cannabis.  https://doi.org/10.1080/16066350801900321
title: Marijuana Readiness to Change Questionnaire Treatment Version Revised

description: The Marijuana Withdraw Checklist (MWC) is used to assess the incidence and severity of perceived marijuana withdrawal symptoms. Originally designed with 22 items (Budney, 1999), the revised version contains 15 of the most frequently endorsed items (Budney, 2003). Respondents rate each symptom experienced during their most recent period of marijuana abstinence or over the past week, based on a 4-point scale where 0 = none, 1 = mild, 2 = moderate, and 3 = severe. The MWC is also included as supplemental information for the PhenX Toolkit Substance Abuse and Addiction Collections. https://doi.org/10.1037/0021-843X.112.3.393
title: Marijuana Withdrawal Checklist

description:
The Markers of Adulthood (MoA) scale captures the shift in the transition to adulthood using markers associated with becoming an adult, including marriage, having children, and living independently.

title: Markers of Adulthood

description: The Massachusetts General Hospital (MGH) Hairpulling Scale to provide a brief, self-report instrument for assessing repetitive hairpulling. title: Massachusetts General Hospital (MGH) Hairpulling Scale

description: Plasma from gestational samples is tested for autoantibodies to STIP1, CRMP1 and 2, LDHA and B, YBX1, NSE, and GDA by ELISA using an assay developed in the Van de Water lab. The designation of positive or negative is determined using and ROC curve with true positive samples to create a signal cutoff. There are combinations of autoantibody patterns that are used to determine if the sample contains the maternal autoantibody related autism reactivity profile.
title: Maternal Autoantibody

description: Interview measure conducted with a participating child's biological mother. Assesses a wide range of maternal and familial risk and protective factors, including but not limited to demographics, socioeconomics, maternal and family health history, pregnancy and childbearing history, pregnancy-related medication and environmental exposures, current and pregnancy-related alcohol and other substance use by quantity, frequency, and gestational timing, and current and pregnancy-related nutrition.

description:
Maternal responsiveness to infant cues during the first year of life is crucial in forming a secure mother–infant attachment relationship and preventing the onset of behavior problems.

description:
The Maternal Self-Efficacy Scale (MSES) is an assessment that measures a woman's perception of her ability to perform in the maternal role. It characterizes a mother’s own judgment about her competence and effectiveness as a mother.  
Maternal Self Efficacy Scale (MSES). From Teti D.M., & Gelfand D.M. Behavioral competence among mothers of infants in the first year: the mediational role of maternal self-efficacy. Child Development. 1991; 62:918-929.
title: Maternal Self-Efficacy Scale (MSES)

description: Evaluates the social support systems of mothers. Pascoe, J. M., Ialongo, N. S., Horn, W. F., Reinhart, M. A., & Perradatto, D. (1988). The reliability and validity of the maternal social support index. Family medicine, 20(4), 271-276.
title: Maternal Social Support Index (MSSI)

description:
The Maternity Social Support Scale (MSSS) measures social factors associated with postnatal depression (lack of family support, low friendship network, lack of help from spouse/partner, conflict with spouse/partner, feeling controlled by spouse/partner, and feeling unloved by spouse/partner) in a 6-item, self-report scale. Higher scores indicate increased support.

DOI: 10.1046/j.1523-536x.2000.00097.x
title: Maternity Social Support Scale (MSSS)

description:
The Matrix Reasoning Item Bank (MaRs-IB) is a task-based assessment containing 80 abstract reasoning items. Each MaRs-IB item consists of an incomplete matrix containing abstract shapes. Participants complete the matrices by identifying relationships between the shapes.

description:
A 24-item scale is used to measure the person’s perception of how much he or she matters to others based on three factors: awareness, importance, and reliance.

Elliott, G. C., Kao, S., & Grant, A. (2004). Mattering: Empirical validation of a social-psychological concept. Self and Identity, 3, 339–354.

France, M. G., & Finney, S. J. (2009). What matters in the measurement of mattering? A construct validity study. Measurement and Evaluation in Counseling and Development, 42, 104–120.

description: The Mayo Clinic Biobank Questionnaire, is a collection of health information donated by Mayo Clinic patients. The Biobank collects samples and health information from patients and other volunteers regardless of health history. Once a participant becomes a part of the Biobank, he or she becomes a part of ongoing health research.
title: Mayo Clinic Biobank Questionnaire

description:
The McGill Friendship Questionnaire–Friend's Function (MFQ–FF) taps respondents' assessments of the degree to which a friend fulfills 6 friendship functions (stimulating companionship, help, intimacy, reliable alliance, self-validation, and emotional security).
The items on this form concern the kind of friend your friend is to you. Imagine that the blank space in each item contains your friend's name. With him or her in mind, decide how often the item applies. On the scale directly to the right of each item circle the number that indicates how often your friend is or does what the item says. There are no right or wrong answers because adult friendships are very different from one another. Just describe your friend as he or she really is to you. 
https://doi.org/10.1037/h0087080
title: McGill Friendship Questionnaire

description: The M-FED is based on the Longitudinal Interval Follow-up Evaluation interview, modified by Stewart Agras et al. 2009 to assess outcomes from patients with eating disorder psychopathology, including questions and definitions similar to those of the Eating Disorder Examination interview. This version has been further modified to serve as a self-report measure.
title: McKnight Follow-up of Eating Disorders (M-FED)

title: Meals in Our Household (MOH)

description: Mean Length of Utterance (or MLU) is a measure of linguistic productivity in children. It is traditionally calculated by collecting 100 utterances spoken by a child and dividing the number of morphemes by the number of utterances. A higher MLU is taken to indicate a higher level of language proficiency.Provided by Dr. Camarata's lab at Vanderbilt University

description: The Memory Matrix served as a Mobile Cognitive Test to test Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI), specifically measuring visual working memory. Participants are asked to memorize and recall the location/pattern of yellow tiles in a 6, 12, or 18-tile matrix. Each administration includes three trials of 9 patterns each; 1 point per pattern correct recreated. https://doi.org/10.3389/fdgth.2022.946685

description: Memory Specificity Memory Task Structure that evaluates individuals' memory at two levels of specificity for stimuli encountered during a reinforcement learning task. Memory Specificity Learning Task Structure evaluates how individuals learn to make good choices across repeated presentations of: the same stimuli, stimuli drawn from the same broader categories (e.g., two different cows), and stimuli from different categories (e.g., cows vs. goats). DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-59379-w

description:
The Mental Health Continuum Short Form (MHC-SF) is derived from the long form (MHC-LF) which consists of 7 items measuring emotional well-being, 18 items measuring psychological well-being, and 15 items measuring social well-being (40 items total). The MHC-SF consists of 14 items that were selected to represent each fact of well-being. The short form consists of 3 emotional well-being items (reflects hedonic well-being), 6 psychological well-being items, and 5 social well-being items (when combined, reflects eudemonic well-being). These response options assess the frequency with which respondents experience each symptom of positive mental health. This scale also provides a flourishing and languishing mental health indicator based on these three subscales. 

doi: https://doi.org/10.1002/jclp.20741
title: Mental Health Continuum

description: This structure is an assessment tool for diagnostic and treatment knowledge based on Mental Health Gap Action Program (mhGAP) assessment questions. It has been used globally for mhGAP training and with over 200 health workers in Nepal.
title: Mental Health Gap Action Program Knowledge Assessment

description: Mental Health History
title: Mental Health History
Mental Health History and Treatment Questionnaire

description: The Mental Health Impacts (MHI) is a module within the Johns Hopkins COVID-19 Community Response Survey which helps researchers understand how the novel coronavirus (COVID-19) pandemic affected people’s lives. The MHI specifically examines the impact the COVID-19 pandemic has had on participants' mental health.
title: Mental Health Impacts

description: This scale measures the implementation outcomes including acceptability, appropriateness, feasibility, organizational climate of an intervention with consumers and providers. doi: 10.1186/s43058-022-00301-6.
title: Mental Health Implementation Science Tools (mhIST)

description:
The Mental Health Knowledge Schedule (MAKS) is a 12-item questionnaire that contains 6 items assessing mental health knowledge areas (help seeking, recognition, support, employment, treatment, and recovery), and 6 items about knowledge of mental health conditions. Items were scored on a scale where 0 = Strongly disagree, and 4 = Strongly agree. Total score was calculated by adding the response values for each item, and then coding 'don't know' as 2 for neutral. 
DOI: 10.1177/070674371005500707
title: Mental Health Knowledge Schedule (MAKS)
Mental Health Knowledge, Attitude, and Behavior Assessment

description:
The Mental Health Practice Readiness Inventory evaluates a professional's practice in terms of community resources, health care financing, support for children/families, clinician information system, delivery system redesign, and decision support for clinicians. It is used with populations of professionals in primary care settings (e.g., staff members of a pediatric practice). 
https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2010-0788D
title: Mental Health Practice Readiness Inventory

description:
The Mental Health Services for Kids in Primary Care (MH-SKIP) survey examines changes in treatment obstacles, use of outside referral, and competency and effectiveness in delivering psychosocial services. It can be used with a variety of different populations of professionals in clinical care settings (e.g., staff members of a pediatric practice). 
DOI: 10.1542/peds.2013-2516
title: Mental Health Services for Kids in Primary Care

description: Evaluates whether participants have taken prescription medication for their mental health, participated in therapy, or feel they need therapy. https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/covid19/pulse/mental-health-care.htm
title: Mental Health Treatment

description: The Mental Help Seeking Attitudes Scale (MHSAS) is a 9-item questionnaire to measure respondents’ overall evaluation (favorable vs unfavorable) of their seeking help from a mental health professional if they found themselves to be dealing with a mental health concern. Participants use a 7-point differential scale with 3...0...3 as prompt labels where 3 is the most favorable or unfavorable opinion and 0 is a neutral stance. These ratings are converted to a scale of 1-7, where higher numbers indicate more favorable attitudes. https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1037/cou0000248
title: Mental Help-Seeking Attitudes Scale (MHSAS)

description: The Mental Illness Microaggressions Scale (MIMS) is an 11-item questionnaire that asks about experiences of microaggressions related to have a mental health problem. Questions are rated on a scale from 0 = Never to 4 = Very often. A sum score and mean score are calculated at the end of the assessment.
title: Mental Illness Microaggressions Scale (MIMS)

description:
The MIRECC measures occupational functioning, social functioning, and symptom severity on three subscales of the Global Assessment of Functioning (GAF) scale.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17412856/
title: Mental Illness Research, Education, and Clinical Center version of GAF scale

description: Mental imagery in suicidality helps to better understand the cognitive processes underlying suicidal thinking for improved treatment development. A clinical measure of the severity of suicidal ideation was associated with both preoccupation with suicide-related imagery and perceived imagery realness. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbtep.2007.10.004
title: Mental Imagery Scale

description: The Meta-Cognitions Questionnaire (MCQ) measures beliefs about worry and intrusive thoughts. 65 items measure factors including Positive Beliefs About Worry, Negative Beliefs About the Controllability of Thoughts and Corresponding Danger, Lack of Cognitive Confidence, Negative Beliefs about Thoughts in General including Themes of Superstition, Punishment, and Responsibility (SPR), and Cognitive Self-Consciousness. Items are rated on a 4-point scale from Do not agree to Agree very much. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0887-6185(97)00011-X
title: Meta-Cognitions Questionnaire

description: Data Structure to link Metabolomics samples to the database they are stored in.

description:
The Metacognition Assessment Scale is used to assess metacognition from free narratives of subjects' personally relevant episodes and relationships. The assessment is designed to evaluate changes in metacognitions within psychotherapy transcripts and has also been adapted to interview situations with subjects diagnosed with schizophrenia spectrum disorders.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/cpp.362

description: The MAWQ is a 30-item, rater-scored instrument using a four-point Likert-type scale that reviews drug withdrawal-related symptom clusters, separated into functional, emotional, physical and additional symptoms, along with a review of vital signs. https://datashare.nida.nih.gov/instrument/methamphetamine-withdrawal-questionnaire
title: Methamphetamine Withdrawal Questionnaire (MAWQ)

description:
This research evaluates the properties of a measure of culturally linked values of Mexican

description: The Mexican-American Ethnic Pride Scale adapts select items from other studies involving Mexican-American adolescents, such as the Multigroup Ethnic Identity Measure, in order to assess identification with Mexican/Mexican-American culture. Items assess identification with Mexican/Mexican-American background and values, as well as American background and values.
title: Mexican-American Ethnic Pride Scale

description: Home based biospecimen sampling and questionnaire completion provide meaningful information about daily mood, steroid hormonal awakening patterns, and sleep schedules/quality that would not have been possible in our laboratory setting alone. This information is gathered through an iPad customized and simplified for our study purposes. Home-based sample (saliva) collection is accompanied by daily stress and mood ratings (Trumpff et al., 2022). Participants are asked to complete a daily morning and evening questionnaire on the provided iPad. The questionnaires include the Daily Stress questionnaire (morning) and the Modified Differential Emotions Scale (evening).
title: MiSIBE Home Logbook

description:
The Michigan Nicotine Reinforcement Questionnaire (M-NRQ) is a self-report inventory used to assess the positive and negative reinforcement consequences of smoking, helping to understand the motivations behind smoking behavior. The M-NRQ aims to differentiate between smokers who primarily smoke for positive reinforcement (enjoyment, pleasure) versus those who smoke primarily for negative reinforcement (to relieve withdrawal symptoms or negative emotions). 
There is an alcohol version which was adapted from this original version. This version assesses negative reinforcement principles related to alcohol use, including craving, symptoms of withdrawal, etc.
https://doi.org/10.1037/t69924-000
title: Michigan Nicotine Reinforcement Questionnaire

description: Microbial populations and their diversity are used to study microbiota associated with health and diseases. Microbial diversity indices are used to compare diversity among samples and between treatments with controls. Common indices include the Shannon-Weaver Diversity Index and Simpson Diversity Index.
title: Microbial Diversity

description: Milwaukee Inventory for Subtypes of Trichotillomania-Adult Version (MIST-A), which was designed to assess “automatic” and “focused” hair pulling subtypes of trichotillomania (TTM). description:
The Mind Excessively Wandering Scale (MEWS) is a 15-item self-report measure designed to reflect mind wandering in ADHD, derived from patient reports of subjective experiences of their thought processes. The scale captures the main characteristics of the mental state described by adults with ADHD: thoughts on the go all the time, thoughts that jump or flit from one topic to another, and multiple lines of thoughts at the same time.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1177/1087054716651927
title: Mind Excessively Wandering Scale

description: The Mind Wandering Task uses the Sustained Attention Response Task (SART) combined with a probe-caught paradigm where participants completed 18 blocks of trials. During each block, participants were repeatedly asked to press the spacebar if they saw a “O” symbol, and not respond if “=” symbol appeared, and at the end of each block they received a probe question asking “What was just going through your mind?” (select from focused, worrying, ruminating, mind wandering, distracted by something external). Ottaviani, C., & Couyoumdjian, A. (2013). Pros and cons of a wandering mind: a prospective study. Frontiers in Psychology, 4, 00524.
title: Mind Wandering Task

description: The Effortless Assessment of Risk States (EARS) is a HIPAA-compliant software tool that collects data via an app downloaded to participants’ personal smartphones or a smartphone provided to the participant. EARS collects active response data (i.e., EMA, video diary, custom questionnaires pushed to the phone) as well as passive data on language, facial expression, voice tone, geographic location, physical activity, sleep, and music choice, through normal phone usage. This structure provides a set of derived daily features extracted from the EARS tool.

description: The Monetary Effort-Based Decision-Making (EBDM) Task is a behavioral paradigm used to assess how individuals evaluate trade-offs between reward magnitude and required effort. Participants typically choose between low-effort/low-reward and high-effort/high-reward options, allowing researchers to quantify motivation, reward sensitivity, and cost-benefit decision processes, often in clinical and neuroimaging contexts.
title: Monetary Evidence-Based Decision Making (EBDM)

description:
Monitoring the Future (MTF) assesses lifetime and past-30 day substance use. The questionnaire is based on a long-term study of substance use and related factors among U.S. adolescents, college students, and adult high school graduates through age 60. MTF has been conducted annually by the University of Michigan’s Institute for Social Research since its first
data collection in 1975 and is supported under a series of investigator-initiated, competitive research grants from the National Institute on Drug Abuse.
title: Monitoring the Future - Substance Use

description: The Montefiore Social Determinants of Health Survey is a social needs screening tool used to identify social determinants of health needs that can be addressed through community services. Derivative of a recommended screening tool by Health Leads.

Four subscales were theorized: Amusement (e.g., I feel like laughing), Anxiety (e.g., I feel nervous, like I won’t know how to respond), Frustration (e.g., I feel irritated by the sound), and Empathy/Sympathy (e.g., I feel sad for the baby).

description: The parent selects an activity (e.g., stacking blocks) that his or her infant cannot perform and then helps the infant to perform the task using supplies provided. Interaction will be coded from videotapes using the NCAST Scale by masked raters yielding 6 subscales: parent sensitivity to cues, parent response to infant's distress, parent social-emotional growth fostering, parent cognitive growth fostering, infant clarity of cues, and infant initiation and responsiveness to caregiver. A contingency subscale measures the amount and quality of parental contingent behavior based on 73 binary items that produce two summary scores, one for the parent and one for the infant. Total infant and parent scores can be derived. As defined by Dr. Sally Rogers' group.

description:
The National Alcohol Survey (NAS) is a repeated, cross-sectional, national epidemiological survey with commensurate, detailed alcohol use pattern and problem measures administered in 5-year intervals. The Graduated Frequency (GF) section of the NAS assesses overall 12-month alcohol consumption frequencies in a series of drinking amount ranges based on an item querying categorical maximum amount consumed, i.e., 12 or more (12+), 8-11, 5-7, 3-4, 1-2 drinks in any day.  These QF data at the various quantity levels can be combined into a 12-month overall alcohol intake volume.  For calculations, use the mid point of the categorical frequencies (e.g., for 8-11 drinks, use 9.5, for 12+ drinks, use 14).  Days or volume above 4+ (women) or 5+ (men) can also be derived, as well as high intensity drinking quantities and frequencies (8-11 and 12+). 
https://arg.org/center/national-alcohol-surveys/
title: National Alcohol Survey - Graduated Frequency

description:
The US National Institute of Mental Health modified the methods of Kraepelin and created the Life-chart Methodology (NIMH-LCM). The major variable in the NIMH-LCM is four levels of depression based on the degree of functional impairment related to affective symptoms (from −1 slight or mild to −4 severe and incapacitating depression) and similarly four degrees of mania (from +1 mild to +4 severe mania), with 0 representing a balanced, well-functioning euthymic state. Additionally, medications, life events, drug abuse, hours of sleep, irritability, ultradian cycling, and co-morbidities are documented on a daily basis in the prospective Life-chart.

doi: 10.1186/1471-244X-14-130
title: National Institute of Mental Health - Life-Chart Methodology Self Ratings

description:
The Nebraska Outness Scale is composed of two separate 5-item subscales, one measuring disclosure (NOS-D) and one measuring concealment (NOS-C). All responses were
given on 11-point Likert-type scales, which was increased from the 10-point response scale
in the OI (Mohr & Fassinger, 2000) to allow for a midpoint. For the NOS-C this ranged
from 0 “Never avoid” to 10 “Always avoid” and for the NOS-D from 0% “None” to 100%
“All.” These two separate subscales were selected because of the conceptual independence
of disclosure and concealment in LGB individuals. The NOS-D assesses disclosure by asking the proportion of a group that was aware of the participant’s sexual orientation. The
NOS-C assesses concealment by asking the proportion of time around a group that they
avoid indicating their sexual orientation regardless of whether members of that group are
aware of their sexual orientation or not.
DOI:10.1037/sgd0000080
title: Nebraska Outness Scale

description:
The Nonbinary Resilience Scale (Nbi-RS) was evaluated for validity and reliability in the current study and aimed to measure unique resilience factors experienced by nonbinary people. For all subscales (i.e., nonbinary community connectedness, nonbinary pride, critical consciousness, gender validation, gender euphoria, and giving back), participants were asked, “How much do you currently agree or disagree with the following statements?” Response options were on a 7-point Likert scale from strongly disagree (1) to strongly agree (7), with an NA option (0). All scales and subscales for all three measures can be scored by taking the mean.
https://dx.doi.org/10.1037/sgd0000719
title: Nonbinary Resilience Scale

description: CESD is the summary score of how people might have felt or behaved during the past week computed from the 20-item Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (CES-D).  For OAI Visits V0-V12, the results of all twenty CESD items and the summary CESD score are provided.  For OAI Visits V13 and V14, only the 10-item form was collected, thus the summary score is not provided for these visits.  Calculate of the SHORT FORM CESD-10 summary score for all participants can be made using Andresen et al. Am J Preventative Medicine 1994; 10: 77-84.

description:
This scale is a 19-item, self-report measure designed to assess an individual’s perceptions of being ignored by or excluded from the social group. The scale items represent general perceptions of being ostracized and are not specific to any one source (i.e., a particular friend, romantic partner, relative, and so on). All items begin with the stem “In general, others…” followed by wording that reflects each ostracism subtype. The response to each item is made on a 5-point rating scale (1 = never, 5 = always). Scale scoring was such that higher scores reflected higher levels of perceived ostracism.
https://doi.org/10.1037/a0030913
title: Ostracism Experience Scale for Adolescents

description: The Other as Shamer Scale (OAS) was adapted from Cook’s (1993) Internalised Shame Scale (ISS) to measure "external shame" (Allan‚ Gilbert & Goss‚ 1994; Goss‚ Gilbert & Allan‚ 1998). The scale consists of 18 items rated on a five-point scale according to the frequency of evaluations about how others judge the self‚ (0 = Never to 4 = Almost always). http://ccare.stanford.edu/wp-content/uploads/2014/02/An-exploration-of-shame-measures-I-the-other-as-shamer-scale.pdf
title: Other as Shamer Scale (OAS)

description: The PROMIS Ability to Participate in Social Roles and Activities item bank assesses the perceived ability to perform one’s usual social roles and activities. Items are worded negatively in terms of perceived limitations, but responses are reverse-coded so that higher scores represent fewer limitations (better abilities). The item bank does not use a time frame (e.g. over the past seven days) when assessing ability to participate in social roles and activities.
title: PROMIS Ability to Participate in Social Roles and Activities

description:
The PROMIS adult Alcohol Use item bank assesses drinking patterns (e.g. quantity and frequency of consumption, time spent drinking, episodes of heavy drinking), cue-based drinking (internal states and external contexts), cravings to drink (e.g. urgency, compulsivity), and efforts to control drinking (e.g. difficulty in limiting drinking) that indicate problematic drinking, particularly at the high end of the severity continuum.

Pilkonis, P. A., Yu, L., Colditz, J., Dodds, N., Johnston, K. L., Maihoefer, C., ... & McCarty, D. (2013). Item banks for alcohol use from
the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System (PROMIS®): Use, consequences, and expectancies. Drug and Alcohol
Dependence, 130(1-3), 167-177.
title: PROMIS Alcohol Use

description:

description:
Neuropathic pain is caused by damage to the peripheral somatosensory nervous system, part
of the nervous system involved in bodily feelings. This damage can be caused by an
abnormality, trauma or disease. The PROMIS Neuropathic Pain Scale asks to what degree (from “not at all” to “ very much”) the respondent’s pain shows qualities typical of neuropathic pain (i.e., felt “numb”, “tingly”, like “pins and needles”, “stinging”, or “electrical”). https://www.healthmeasures.net/index.php?option=com_instruments&view=measure&id=783&Itemid=992
title: PROMIS Neuropathic Pain Quality

description:
PROMIS Pediatric Sleep Practices
This structure asks the child about their own sleep practices. The first half of questions are collected on a 5-point Likert scale ranging from Never to Always, and asks the child about their sleep environment, bedtime routine, and sleep consistency. The second half of the survey asks the child about their sleep and wake times on the weekends and weekdays.
PROMIS Pediatric Sleep Practices Parent
This structure asks parents about their child’s sleep practices. The first half of questions are collected on a 5-point Likert scale ranging from Never to Always, and asks parents questions about the child’s sleep environment, bedtime routine, and sleep consistency. The second half of the survey asks parents of the child’s sleep and wake times on the weekends and weekdays.
title: PROMIS Sleep Practices

description: The Pandemic Emotional Impact Scale (PEIS; Ballou et al., 2020) is a 16-item self-report measure assessing the effects of COVID-19 on the lives and emotional wellbeing on participants. For each item on the PEIS, respondents are asked how much their wellbeing and functioning has been different recently, compared to how it was before the beginning of the pandemic. The response options are “Not at all (0)”, “A little bit (1)”, “Moderately (2)”, “A lot (3)” and “Extremely (4)”. The index score is the sum number of all PEIS items given a "Moderate" or greater rating by each respondent.	https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.bbih.2020.100161

description:
The Panic Disorder Screener (PADIS) is a brief tool designed to identify symptoms of panic disorder, enabling early diagnosis and appropriate intervention to improve mental health care. The PADIS was developed to fill a gap in existing screening measures that assess severity over a brief period of time.

DOI: 10.1016/j.psychres.2015.04.016
title: Panic Disorder Screener (PADIS)

description: The PDSR is a brief 24-item measure of panic disorder (PD) originally designed based on the DSM-IV (APA, 1994). The initial items assess if participants have experienced a panic attack as defined as a “sudden rush of intense fear or anxiety,” and if so, recency and frequency of the attack(s). If lifetime panic attacks exceed 1 attack, presence of physiological symptoms, fear of dying and losing control, as well as functional impairment and distress related to the panic attacks are assessed. The PDSR has demonstrated strong psychometric properties, with good test-retest reliability high internal consistency, and discriminant and convergent validity in the original validation study (Newman et al., 2006). A diagnostic cut-off score of 8.75 has demonstrated high agreement with a diagnostic interview in identifying those who meet criteria for PD (k = .93; Newman et al., 2006).  For T4, the time frame will be modified to “In the last 7 days.”
title: Panic Disorder Self-Report

description:
The Paranoia Reversal Learning Task (PRLT) uses reversal learning behavior and computational modeling to estimate belief updating across individuals with and without mental illness.

Participants completed a three-option reversal-learning task in which they chose between three decks of cards with hidden reward probabilities. They selected a deck on each turn and received positive or negative feedback (+100 or 50 points, respectively). Participants were instructed to find the best deck with the caveat that the best deck may change. Undisclosed to participants, reward probabilities switched among decks after selection of the highest probability option in nine out of ten consecutive trials (‘reversal events’). Thus, the task was designed to elicit expected uncertainty (probabilistic reward associations) and unexpected uncertainty (reversal events), requiring participants to distinguish probabilistic losses from change in the underlying deck values.
title: Paranoia Reversal Learning Task

The items asked about a variety of domains in which parents and children frequently fight (chores, homework, romance and more). Each child was asked the scale twice, once for their mother and once for their father. Parent’s perceived conflict with adolescent children was assessed using a scale adapted from Smetana (1988) that measured the frequency of conflict in 12 domains, specifically: chores, appearance, homework and schoolwork, social life, romantic relationships, bedtime and curfew, health, choosing activities, money, behavior and personality, relationships with brothers and sisters, and relationships with friends.

The Pediatric Daytime Sleepiness Scale is a self-reported assessment, designed to assess the relationship between daytime sleepiness and school related outcomes. Drake C, Nickel C, Burduvali E, Roth T, Jefferson C, Pietro B. The Pediatric Daytime Sleepiness Scale (PDSS): Sleep habits and school outcomes in middle-school children. Sleep 2003. Jun 15;26(4):455-8

description:
The Pemberton Happiness Index (PHI) is a validated multi-dimensional measure of happiness that was developed to provide a comprehensive assessment of individual and societal well-being. The scale includes eleven items
related to different domains. https://doi.org/10.1186/1477-7525-11-66
title: Pemberton Happiness Index (PHI)

description: The computerized neurocognitive battery (CNB) takes approximately an hour to administer and includes tests that measure the following domains: abstraction and mental flexibility, attention, working memory, episodic memory (word, face, and spatial recognition memory), language reasoning, spatial processing, sensorimotor, motor speed, and emotion identification. The CNB yields measures of accuracy (number of correct responses) and speed (median response time (RT) for correct items). https://doi.org/10.1016/S0893-133X(01)00278-0, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jneumeth.2009.11.017, https://doi.org/10.1037/a0026712
title: Penn Computerized Neurobehavioral Battery (PennCNB)

description: Based on facial emotional stimuli research by Gur et al https://doi.org/10.1016/S0165-0270(02)00006-7 the Penn Emotion Recognition Test (PERT) comes in two forms. The ER-40 utilizes 40 color photographs featuring the emotions happy, sad, anger, fear and no emotion and mild and extreme intensities. https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.ajp.2007.07101604 The PERT-96 utilizes the same photographs as the ER-40 plus more for a total of 96 color photographs featuring the emotions happy, sad, anger, fear, disgust, and no emotion in mild and extreme intensities. https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.ajp.160.10.1768 Both studies sought to correlate face emotion recognition/processing, race, and schizophrenia.
title: Penn Emotion Recognition Task

description: This questionnaire collects data from both the therapist and patient to measure the strength of the bond between therapist and patient, called the "therapeutic alliance."
title: Penn Helping Alliance Questionnaire

description: The Penn Letter N-Back Test measures working memory, the ability to keep and refresh goal-related information. The task consists of 16 blocks of 20 trials for a total of 320 trials. There are 160 0-back trials (Trial Types = Target vs. Non-Target), 80 2-back trials (Trial Types: Target vs. Non-Target vs. Lures) and 60 3-back trials (Trial Types: Target vs. Non-Target vs. Lures). Therefore, all trials were one of the following: (1) 0-back Target, (2) 0-back Non-Target, (3) 2-back Target, (4) 2-back Non-Target (5) 2-back Lure, (6)3-back Target, (7) 3-back Non-Target, (8) 3-back Lure.
title: Penn Letter N-Back Task

description:
Everyone at some point experiences problems with memory, attention, or concentration,
but these problems may occur more frequently for individuals with neurologic diseases like
MS. The following questions describe several situations in which a person may encounter
problems with memory, attention or concentration. If you are marking your own answers,
please circle the appropriate response (0, 1, 2,...) based on your cognitive function during
the past 4 weeks. If you need help in marking your responses, tell the interviewer the
number of the best response. Please answer every question. If you are not sure which
answer to select, please choose the one answer that comes closest to describing you. The
interviewer can explain any words or phrases that you do not understand.
During the past 4 weeks, how often did you....
title: Perceived Deficits Questionnaire

description: Questionnaire about an individual's perception of their food deprivation
title: Perceived Deprivation Questionnaire

description: The Perceived Devaluation-Discrimination Scale is a 12-item questionnaire used to measure perceived alcohol stigma. Seven PDD items assessed perceived discrimination (e.g., “Most employers will pass over the application of a former alcoholic in favor of another applicant”). Five items assessed perceived devaluation, (e.g., “Most people feel that entering alcohol treatment is a sign of personal failure”). Responses were measured with a 6-point Likert-type scale, ranging from strongly agree to strongly disagree. Six items used reverse wording to prevent response biases.
title: Perceived Devaluation-Discrimination Scale

description:
The Perceived Discrimination (PD) scale, developed for the international study, consists of seven items: three items assess perceived frequency of being treated unfairly or negatively because of one’s ethnic background by peers, teachers, and other adults; and four items assess feeling unaccepted in society because of one’s ethnicity. Participants respond on a scale ranging from 1 (almost never) to 5 (very often).
https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1559-1816.1998.tb01661.x
title: Perceived Discrimination (PD) Scale

description: Self-report questionnaire evaluating aspects of discrimination that the participant has faced in multiple facets of life such as ethnicity, race, gender, age, sex, or appearance.
title: Perceived Discrimination Scale

description: The Perceived Ethnic Discrimination Scale assesses perceived ethnic discrimination with a scale built from three items. These items were set in a 4-point scale ranging from never to always. https://doi.org/10.1037/t15350-000
title: Perceived Ethnic Discrimination Scale

description:
Questionnaire about an individual's perceived knowledge of sexual health.
https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-014-2093-y
title: Perceived Knowledge Questionnaire

description: The Perceived Maternal Parenting Self-Efficacy (PMP S-E) survey is used to measure parenting self-efficacy in mothers with relatively healthy hospitalized preterm newborns. 20 items are divided among four subscales: caretaking procedures, evoking behaviors, reading behaviors/signaling, and situational beliefs. Responses to each item range from 1= Strongly Disagree to 4= Strongly Agree and higher scores indicate a higher level of maternal self-efficacy. Score interpretation requires consideration of variables such as time from birth to completion of the PMP S-E and gestational age of the newborn. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2648.2007.04445.x
title: Perceived Maternal Parenting Self-Efficacy Questionnaire (PMP S-E)

description:
The Perceived Need for Treatment for Alcohol Use Disorders is used to evaluate participants' attitudes towards controlling their drinking behaviors and determine utilization of any treatments for alcohol use disorders.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1176/ps.2009.60.12.1618
title: Perceived Need for Treatment for Alcohol Use Disorders

description: The Perceived Online Racism Scale (PORS) assesses perceived online racist interpersonal interactions and exposure to online racist content among people of color. Factors include a) personal experience of racial cyber-aggression, (b) vicarious exposure to racial cyber-aggression, and (c) online-mediated exposure to racist reality. Permission to distribute granted by author. https://doi.org/10.1037/cou0000205
title: Perceived Online Racism Scale (PORS)

description: Self-report scale adapted for children on which participants indicate the frequency with which they have personally experienced racist events. DOI: 10.1207/S15374424JCCP3202_11
title: Perceived Racism Scale

description:
The Perceived Risk of HIV Scale (PRHS) is used to assess perceived risk of HIV infection and covers various dimensions of risk perception. Higher scores on the PRHS are associated with a greater number of sex partners, episodes of unprotected sex, and having sex while high.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10461-011-0003-2
title: Perceived Risk of HIV Scale

description: The PSSQ is a self-report measure that assess the participant’s perceived level of social support through three subscales: support from family, support from friends, and support from a significant other.
title: Perceived Social Support Questionnaire (PSSQ)

description: The Perceived Stress Questionnaire (PSQ) is intended to survey patients with chronic relapsing diseases regarding life situations that trigger symptoms. It asks participants about their life during the last year or two (General questionnaire) or the last month (Recent questionnaire). 30 elements are rated on a 4-point Likert scale from 1= Almost never to 4= Usually. https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-3999(93)90120-5
title: Perceived Stress Questionnaire

title: Perceived Structural Stigma Scale

https://doi.org/10.1037/0021-9010.87.3.565
title: Perceived Supervision Support

Asssesses perceived risk of using alcohol

description: Assesses the benefits or results that accrue from supervision, including client-centered supervision. Formal supervision includes feedback on job performance; teaching/coaching on job-related skills or knowledge; making suggestions about consumer progress; discussing worker feelings about specific cases or the job as a whole; dealing with administrative matters such as vacation requests or paperwork requirements; disseminating information on policies and procedures; or coordinating work. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10488-013-0470-z

description: The PBCL-36, also known as the Fetal Alcohol Behavior Scale (FABS), is a 36-item Personal Behavior Checklist (PBC) parent questionnaire that assesses problem behaviors in children with Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD). https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1530-0277.1998.tb03656.x
title: Personal Behaviors Checklist (PBCL-36) / Fetal Alcohol Behavior Scale (FABS)
