title: Personal Experience Inventory (PEI)

description:
The Personal Feelings Questionnaire examines an individual's proneness to feelings of shame and guilt. The questionnaire contains 16 items including a 10-item Shame subscale and a 6-item Guilt subscale.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1037/t65212-000
title: Personal Feelings Questionnaire

description:
The Personal Rejection Scale is a subscale, apart of the Paediatric Self-Stigmatization Scale (PaedS). The PaedS is a scale developed for measuring stigma in children and adolescents; it consists of 4 subscales that measure societal devaluation (14 items), personal rejection (5 items), self-stigma (5 items) and secrecy of receiving mental health treatment (7 items).
DOI: 10.1016/j.eurpsy.2017.01.004
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpsy.2017.01.004

description:
The Personal Self-Concept (PSC) Questionnaire is a 22-item assessment made up
of four scales: Self-fulfillment, Autonomy, Honesty and Emotional self-concept. The
purpose of this assessment is to measure this domain of the self-concept.
Goñi, Eider & Madariaga, Jose & Axpe, Inge & Goni, Alfredo. (2011). Structure of the Personal Self-Concept (PSC) Questionnaire. International Journal of Clinical and Health Psychology. 11. 509-522.
title: Personal Self-Concept (PSC) Questionnaire

description: The Personal Suicide Stigma Questionnaire (PSSQ) is used to assess the levels of suicide-related stigma experiences of suicidal individuals. Participants rate items from three subscales - Rejection, Minimization, and Self-Blame - on a Likert scale of 0= Never to 4= Very often. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph20053816
title: Personal Suicide Stigma Questionnaire (PSSQ)

description: The Personal Wellbeing Index - Intellectual Disability (PWI-ID) is a version of the Personal Wellbeing Index - Adult (PWI-A) scale developed specifically for those with intellectual disabilities or other cognitive impairment. Individuals rank answers on a scale of "happiness" instead of "satisfaction" and are pre-tested to determine the most appropriate range - whether the full 0-10 scale (same as PWI-A), or a "reduced" version (either 5-face, 3-face, or 2-face). http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/978-90-481-9650-0_3
title: Personal Wellbeing Index - Intellectual Disability

Chisholm DM, Collis ML, Kulak LL, Davenport W, Gruber N, Stewart GW, et al.'PAR-Q validation report: the evaluation of a self-administered pre-exercise screening questionnaire for adults. Victoria, BC: BC Ministry of Health and Health and Welfare Canada; 1978.

description:
The Physician Belief Scale evaluates a provider's beliefs and feelings about providing psychosocial interventions with patients. It can be used with a variety of different populations of professionals in clinical care settings (e.g., staff members of a pediatric practice). Lower scores on the Scale represent a more psychosocial approach to patient care. The instrument may be used to evaluate effectiveness of behavioral science teaching, describing regional or other differences in physician beliefs within and between specialties and estimating changes in provider beliefs.
DOI: 10.1016/0277-9536(84)90376-9
title: Physician Belief Scale

description:
Picture Description Task (PDT): The PDT assesses general narrative style that does not rely on prospection or episodic memory. Participants are asked to describe a complex image (e.g. photographed nature scene) for 3 minutes. Their audio-recorded descriptions are coded for number of internal details (information about the picture) and external details (information not directly relevant to the picture, or general semantic information) provided. 
Reference: Madore KP, Gaesser B, Schacter DL. Constructive episodic simulation: Dissociable effects of a specificity induction on remembering, imagining, and describing in young and older adults. J Exp Psychol Learn Mem Cogn. 2014; 40(3):609-622. doi:10.1037/a0034885. PMCID: PMC4006318.
title: Picture Description Task

description: The Picture Sorting Task requires children to indicate whether they would engage in risky tasks described in verbal prompts and depicted in illustrated photos. This task was adapted to feature 7 social and 7 non-social risk-taking prompts. Following each prompt, children will be asked whether they would engage in the behavior described. Children’s decisions on the Picture Sorting Task are dichotomously coded as safe (“No”) or risky (“Yes”). To ensure that children are carefully considering their responses, they will be told that they may be asked to demonstrate the behaviors selected. Risky responses across 14 scenarios (7 social and 7 non-social) are summed. Higher scores represent greater risk-taking tendencies which can be evaluated cumulatively or by social versus non-social context.
title: Picture Sorting Task

description: The Piers-Harris Children's Self-Concept Scale (PHCSCS), also titled "The Way I Feel About Myself," is a self-report instrument that assesses how children and adolescents feel about themselves using six key dimensions of self-concept, including behavioral adjustment, intellectual and school status, physical appearance, anxiety, popularity, and happiness. The scale provides insights into self-perceptions and overall mental well-being. There are several Piers-Harris Self-Concept Scale Editions. DOIs: 10.2466/pr0.2002.91.3.827, 10.1371/journal.pone.0135386
title: Piers-Harris Children's Self-Concept Scale (PHCSCS)

description: The Post Neurofeedback Questionnaire (PNFQ) is a self-report measure used to capture participants’ subjective experiences following neurofeedback sessions. It typically assesses perceived changes in cognitive, emotional, and physiological states, as well as engagement and side effects, helping researchers evaluate intervention effectiveness and tolerability. The tool is commonly used in neurofeedback and biofeedback studies to complement objective neurophysiological data with patient-reported outcomes.

description: The Post-Task Questionnaire for Horizon and EEfRT refers to a brief self-report instrument administered after completing effort-based decision-making tasks (e.g., EEfRT and Horizon Task) to capture subjective influences on performance such as reward sensitivity, perceived probability, fatigue, and motivation. It is typically composed of Likert-scale items assessing how different task parameters affected decision-making and effort allocation, helping contextualize behavioral outcomes with subjective experience. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8797221

description: The Post-Task Questionnaire for the Tap-To-Safety Task is a self-report measure administered after completing an avoidance-based behavioral paradigm in which participants repeatedly perform actions (e.g., tapping) to prevent or delay an aversive outcome. It assesses subjective experiences such as perceived threat, control, effort, and anxiety during the task, providing complementary data to behavioral indices of avoidance and safety learning.
title: Post-Task Questionnaire for Tap-To-Safety Task

description: The PDS–5 is a 24-item self-report measure that assesses PTSD symptom severity in the last month according to DSM–5 criteria. The PDS–5 begins with two trauma screen questions, meant to assess the respondent’s trauma history and to single out the traumatic event that currently bothers the respondent the most. Twenty questions assess the presence and severity of the PTSD symptoms. The symptom items are rated on a 5-point scale of frequency and severity ranging from 0 (not at all) to 4 (6 or more times a week/severe). An additional four items ask about distress and interference caused by PTSD symptoms, as well as onset and duration of symptoms.
title: Posttraumatic Diagnostic Scale (PDS-5)

description: The PTSD-8, a short PTSD inventory, is derived from the first sixteen items of the Harvard Trauama Questionnaire Part IV (HTQ), which corresponds to the DSM-IV criteria for PTSD. Items are rated 1= Not at all; 2= A little; 3= Quite a bit; and 4= Most of the time. Subscales include intrusion (4 items), avoidance (2 items), and hyper vigilance (2 items). https://doi.org/10.2174%2F1745017901006010101
title: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD-8)

description: The PrEP Contemplation Ladder is a single-item, stage-based measure adapted from behavioral change models to assess an individual’s readiness to initiate pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for HIV prevention. It uses a visual or numerical ladder format in which respondents select the rung that best represents their current level of contemplation, ranging from no intention to active preparation for PrEP uptake. The tool is primarily used in HIV prevention research and clinical settings to evaluate motivational readiness and inform targeted interventions.
title: PrEP Contemplation Ladder

description: Probabilistic reversal learning (PRL) is typically conducted in a similar fashion to reversal learning as described above, except that the outcomes are stochastic. Primarily, by analyzing behavior on a trial-by-trial basis to generate “win–stay” and “lose–shift” proportions, it is possible to determine the impact of “spurious” feedback on immediately subsequent choices.
title: Probabilistic Reversal Learning

description:
The Problem Behavior Frequency Scale (PBFS) is an assessment that measures the frequency of aggressive behaviors on three dimensions: physical, non-physical, and relational. Respondents are required to indicate on a 6-point scale (1=never, 6=20 or more times) how frequently they engaged in a range of aggressive behaviors in the last 30 days, for example, ‘shoved or push another kid’ (physical), ‘picked on someone’ (nonphysical), and ‘spread a false rumor about someone’ (relational). 
doi: 10.1037/pas0000571
title: Problem Behavior Frequency Scale

description: The Problem Gambling Severity Index (PGSI) measures the nine scored elements of the Canadian  Problem Gambling Index (CPGI). The CPGI and PGSI are used as gambling screening and prevalence measures. The PGSI draws from the South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS) and DSM-IV (Diagnostic and Statistical Mental of Manual Disorders, 4th Ed). Items are scored on a scale of 0= Never to 3= Almost Always; cut-off scores are 1-2= Low risk; 3-7= Moderate risk; 8-27= Problem gambler.

description: Prototype Matching is an alternate and simplified method for improving diagnosis of personality disorders to count diagnostic criteria and applying cutoffs (count/cutoff). It features a greater ease of use, minimization of artefactual comorbidity, compatibility with naturally occurring cognitive processes, and readiness to translate into categorical and dimensional diagnoses. Diagnosticians rate the overall similarity (match) between a patient and the prototype using a 5-point scale from 5= Very good match to 1= Little or no match, considering the prototype as a whole rather than individual symptoms. Ratings of 4 or 5 denote categorical diagnosis ("caseness"); ratings of 3 represent "features" or subthreshold pathology. https://doi.org/10.1176/ajp.2006.163.5.846
title: Prototype Matching

description:
The PRISM was designed to provide clear guidelines for differentiating between the expected effects of intoxication and withdrawal, substance-induced disorders, and primary disorders. It is also useful for studying the effects of comorbidity on the longitudinal course of alcoholism or alcoholism treatment outcome. The PRISM can also be used to differentiate subjects in order to study treatment-matching strategies when psychopathology is one of the matching variables. This instrument was designed to maximize reliability and validity in heavy drinkers and users of drugs. Although primarily designed as a research instrument, the PRISM provides systematic coverage of alcohol- and drug-related experiences and symptoms that may be useful in identifying areas of focus for treatment.
DOI: 10.1176/ajp.153.9.1195

Each category is screened for with a stem question followed by more targeted questions. For each domain, a screening question establishes the presence of the symptom with additional questions then confirming that the experience is incongruent with norms in the society.

description: Quality of life in Alzheimer’s disease (QOL-AD) is a subjective and multidimensional construct that measures how patients and caregivers perceive the impact of the disease on their well-being. The QOL-AD is a 13-item questionnaire that covers various domains such as physical health, mood, memory, and social activities. The interview is administered in interview format to research participants. Participants are asked questions about their quality of life and to rate different aspects of their life using one of four words: poor, fair, good or excellent. DOI: 10.1111/j.1365-2702.2004.01050.x

description: The Quality of Life in Bipolar Disorder scale (QoL.BD; Michalak and Murray, 2010b) assesses cardinal life areas directly impacted by Bipolar Disorder (mood, sleep, physical health, cognition), pragmatic and functional outcomes (home, work, education, leisure, finances), and psychosocially-orientated constructs (relationships, self-esteem, spirituality, identity, independence). The full 56-item has an overall score range 48-240 calculated by summing responses to the 48 items of the core 12 domains. Higher scores represent greater satisfaction with life. The Brief QoL.BD was also created from the 12-items representing the core QoL domains, score range 12-60. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jad.2020.09.017
title: Quality of Life in Bipolar Disorder (QoL.BD)

description: The Quality of Life in Epilepsy Inventory for Adolescents (QOLIE-AD-48) assesses health-related quality of life for children with epilepsy (used in PNES participants by instructing them to consider their PNES when asked about "seizures"). The QOLIE-AD-48 contains 48 items in eight subscales: epilepsy impact (12 items), memory/concentration (10), attitudes toward epilepsy (four), physical functioning (five), stigma (six), social support (four), school behavior (four), health perceptions (three), and a total summary score, with higher scores indicating better HRQOL.
title: Quality of Life in Epilepsy Inventory for Adolescents (QOLIE-AD-48)

description:
The Quick Dementia Rating Scale (QDRS) is a 10-item dementia staging tool, derived from an empiric review of the literature and the clinical experience of the author caring for patients at memory disorder clinics, cover (1) memory and recall, (2) orientation, (3) decision-making and problem-solving abilities, (4) activities outside the home, (5) function at home and hobbies, (6) toileting and personal hygiene, (7) behavior and personality changes, (8) language and communication abilities, (9) mood, and (10) attention and concentration. These domains capture prominent symptoms of MCI, AD, and non-Alzheimer neurocognitive disorders including Lewy body dementia, frontotemporal degeneration, vascular dementia, chronic traumatic encephalopathy, and depression. Each domain has five possible answers increasing in severity of symptoms.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4484882/
title: Quick Dementia Rating Scale

description: QuickSCID-5 is a fully structured diagnostic instrument that employs interview questions adapted from the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-5 (SCID-5). The QuickSCID-5 is a briefer, more time-efficient version of the SCID designed almost entirely with closed-ended questions that can be answered YES or NO by the patient. QuickSCID has a modular design so that only modules of interest need be administered. Modules include an optional Overview, Module A (Mood Episodes and Disorders), Module B (Psychotic Symptoms Screen), Module C (Alcohol and Other Substance Use Disorders), Module D (Anxiety Disorders), Module E (Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder); Module F (Adult Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder), Module G (Posttraumatic Stress Disorder), Module H (Eating Disorders), Module I (Screening for Other Disorders), and Module J (Rule Out Mental Disorders Due to a Medical Condition and Substance/Medication-induced Mental Disorders). ISBN 978-1-61537-363-5
title: Quick Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-5 Disorders (QuickSCID-5)

performance in noise.

description:
The Revised Children’s Anxiety and Depression Scale-25 (RCADS-25) is a 25-item scale that measures levels of anxiety (e.g. “I worry when I think I have done poorly at something”) and low mood (e.g. “I feel sad or empty”). The scale has two subscales (Total Anxiety and Total Depression) and an overall score. Both the anxiety and depression subscales have strong relation to anxiety and depression diagnostic groups. All items assess the frequency of symptoms and are rated on a 4-point Likert scale.

The child self-report scale can be used by children and adolescents between the ages of 8 to 18. A parent (or caregiver) version is also available to rate a child or adolescent's level of anxiety and depressive symptoms based on personal observations (e.g., “Nothing is much fun for my child anymore.”)
title: RCADS-25 Parent/Caregiver Assessment

description:
The Revised Children’s Anxiety and Depression Scale-25 (RCADS-25) is a 25-item scale that measures levels of anxiety (e.g. “I worry when I think I have done poorly at something”) and low mood (e.g. “I feel sad or empty”). The scale has two subscales (Total Anxiety and Total Depression) and an overall score. Both the anxiety and depression subscales have strong relation to anxiety and depression diagnostic groups. All items assess the frequency of symptoms and are rated on a 4-point Likert scale. 
The child self-report scale can be used by children and adolescents between the ages of 8 to 18.

description: The Rapid Eating Assessment for Participants is a simple measure that quickly, in less than 10 min, assesses diet quality in a clinical or research setting. The Rapid Eating and Activity Assessment for Patients (REAP) survey was developed in 2003 to quickly assess nutrient intake and assist in brief lifestyle counseling by a healthcare provider. In 2004, the REAP was shortened to focus on food intake and the practicality of use in low-literacy populations.
title: Rapid Eating Assessment for Participants Shortened (REAP-S)

description: The Rapid Estimate of Adult Literacy in Medicine (REALM) is a word recognition test of common medical terms used to identify patients who may have difficulty reading patient education materials and communicating orally with their provider. The score reveals a grade range estimate for reading level. A Short Form (REALM-SF; doi 10.1097/MLR.0b013e3180616c1b) and Revised (REALM-R; doi 10.1111/j.1525-1497.2003.10651.x) version are also available.
title: Rapid Estimate of Adult Literacy in Medicine

description: The RCI-S measures counselor ratings of suicidality improvement at each treatment session. We also captured additional treatment session information (e.g., session number, type, involvement) alongside this form.
title: Rating of Clinical Improvement - Suicidality

description: Raven's Progressive Matrices provide three ability levels to minimize the effect of language skills and cultural bias. The Advanced Progressive Matrices (APM) provide a "Difficult" level test that spreads scores of the top 20% of the population. See also: https://nda.nih.gov/data-structure/ravens_standard01 and https://nda.nih.gov/data-structure/ravenspcm_200402 DOI: https://doi.org/10.1037/t10893-000
title: Raven's Advanced Progressive Matrices (APM)

description: Raven's Coloured Progressive Matrices (CPM) (2004)
title: Ravens Coloured Progressive Matrices (CPM)

description: ReTaine (Real Time Assessment In the Natural Environment) EMA (Ecological Momentary Assessment) is the momentary data collection system participants fill out on their smart phone during the study (14 days). They are signaled on their phone each day - once in the morning (Morning Assessment) and 4 times in the afternoon/evening (Signaled); additionally they are able to self-report a drinking event (After drinking). Participants are asked questions about their alcohol consumption - in the moment - rather than recalling after an extended period of time.
title: ReTaine EMA

description: Parent-reported questionnaire on the attachment-related behaviors of the child. DOI: 10.1037/h0100508
title: Reactive Attachment Disorder Symptom Checklist

description: Defined by Dr Sukhodolsky lab at Yale Child Study Center
title: Reactive-Proactive Questionnaire

description:
The Readiness Assessment Tool is a rating scale assessing dimensions of campus support for alcohol prevention efforts. This rating scale was developed for a community/group to complete which would yield a community readiness score. This version was filled out by individuals rating their own community. 
(1) Oetting, E. R., Donnermeyer, J. F., Plested, B. A., Edwards, R. W., Kelly, K., & Beauvais, F. (1995). Assessing community readiness for prevention. The International Journal of the Addictions, 30(6), 659-683. 
(2) Oetting, E. R., Jumper-Thurman, P., Plested, B., & Edwards, R. W. (2001). Community readiness and health services. Substance Use and Misuse, 36(6&7), 825-843.
title: Readiness Assessment Tool

description: Motivational interviewing (MI) is an effective evidence-based approach to overcoming the ambivalence that keeps many people from making desired changes in their lives, even after seeking or being referred to professional treatment.
title: Readiness Confidence and Importance of Behavior Change

description: Gathers self-report of confidence and readiness for change related to alcohol use. Items are presented as slider style rating scales or multiple-choice response options.
title: Readiness Rulers for Importance & Confidence - Alcohol Version

description:
The Readiness to Change Questionnaire measures the precontemplation, contemplation, action, and maintenance stages of change in excessive drinkers.

https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1360-0443.1992.tb02720.x
title: Readiness To Change Questionnaire

description: Reading the Mind in the Eyes Task (RMET)
title: Reading the Mind in the Eyes Task (RMET)

description: Measures frequency and affective outcomes of reappraisal tactics. https://doi.org/10.1037/emo0001259
title: Reappraisal Tactics Task

description: Students were asked to report their reasons for alcohol nonuse and cannabis nonuse separately, such that they could endorse different reasons for nonuse by substance. https://doi.org/10.1111/acer.14693
title: Reasons for Alcohol and Cannabis Nonuse

description:
Reasons for Heavy Drinking Questionnaire. The RHDQ included seven items and was constructed by one of the authors (R.F.A.) after interviews with hundreds of individuals
participating in pharmacotherapy trials over many years, as a brief self-report tool to capture domains relevant to reasons people may drink to excess. Participants rated each item by marking a position on a visual analog scale ranging from 0 (strongly disagree) to 100 (strongly agree) presented as a line on a tablet computer; scores were computed by the software based on the relative position marked on each line.
doi: 10.15288/jsad.2016.77.354
title: Reasons for Heavy Drinking Questionnaire

description: Evaluates why adolescents choose not to use alcohol or marijuana.
title: Reasons for Not Using Alcohol or Marijuana

description: The Reasons for Quitting Questionnaire (RFQ) was originally created to assess motivation for abstinence among treatment-seeking tobacco users, but was later modified to incorporate cocaine, marijuana, and other substance users. Four motivation dimensions emerged: self-concept issues, health concerns, legal issues, and social influence.
title: Reasons for Quitting Questionnaire - Tobacco, Marijuana, Cocaine (RFQ)
Reasons for School Non-Attendance

description: Rating of the reasons a participant chooses to limit his/her alcohol consumption
title: Reasons for not drinking alcohol

description: Recent societal events that have taken place (in 2020) caused extra stress for many people. This evaluates significant stressors present over the past 2 weeks.
title: Recent Events Stress Screener

description: The Timeline Followback (TLFB), a retrospective calendar-based measure of daily substance use, was initially developed to obtain self-reports of alcohol use. Since its inception it has undergone extensive evaluation across diverse populations and is considered the most psychometrically sound self-report measure of drinking. The TLFB has been extended for cocaine, cannabis, and cigarette use.
title: Recent Substance Use Timeline Followback
Recognition Ratings for Measuring Change in Interpretation Bias

description: Recovery House Environment Scale is an 8-item scale used to measure the current social environment of a recovery residence to see to what extent peer support activities that are part of the social model are being followed. Items are scored on 5-point scale.

description: The Recovery Identity Scale contains participant reports of their recovery identity using an adapted identity development scale that captures three dimensions of dynamic identity processes: commitment to a recovery identity, in-depth exploration of a recovery identity, and re-consideration of the recovery identity.

description: This measure requires respondents to rate the extent to which they agree, on a scale from 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree), with seven statements (e.g., "I am able to do the things needed to settle our conflicts"). Higher scores reflect greater perceived efficacy and correlate directly with higher quality problem solving behavior among wives and inversely with the amount of anger displayed during a problem-solving discussion by both husbands and wives. DOI: 10.10371/0893-3200.14.2.267
title: Relationship Efficacy Measure

description: Relationship Quality is measured in five dimensions: intimacy, autonomy, equality, constructive problem solving (based on the Conflict Resolution Inventory), and Barriers. https://doi.org/10.2307/353528
title: Relationship Quality

description:
The Relationship quality assessment consist of 6 subscales regarding the quality of partner relationship. Subscales include Intimacy, Spirituality, Partner Support, Sexual Satisfaction, Decision Making, and Communication.
Emotional intimacy subscale of the Sternberg triangular scale of love (Sternberg, 1988). This subscale was validated by CRS (CFAT) in Malawi. Used only five items from the CFAT version that remained after the factor analysis.
title: Relationship Quality Assessment

description: Current Version
title: Relationship Questionnaire

description: The Relationship Scales Questionnaire (RSQ; Griffin & Bartholomew, 1994) was developed as a continuous measure of adult attachment. The RSQ contains 30 short statements drawn from Hazan and Shaver's (1987) attachment measure, Bartholomew and Horowitz's Relationship Questionnaire (rq01, 1991), and Collins and Read's Adult Attachment Scale (arq01, 1990). On a 5-point scale ranging from "not at all like me" to "very much like me", participants rate the extent to which each statement best describes their characteristic style in close relationships. Five statements contribute to the secure and dismissing attachment patterns and four statements contribute to the fearful and preoccupied attachment patterns. Scores for each attachment pattern are derived by taking the mean of the four or five items representing each attachment prototype. See also: Adolescent Relationship Scales Questionnaire arq01. https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1037/t10182-000
title: Relationship Scales Questionnaire (RSQ)

description: The Relative Orientation task requires participants to remember the orientations of two sequentially presented arrow stimuli, and report back the orientations using a mouse click. The difference between the two stimulus orientations is manipulated across trials such that sometimes the two arrows point in roughly the same direction, while other times they point in wildly different directions. Data files consist of the orientation for stim1 and stim2, as well as the orientation for response1 and response2, as well as the order in which participants are probed on the two stimuli (sometimes participants first report the orientation of the first arrow, then the second arrow, and other times the report sequence is reversed).
title: Relative Recall Orientation

description: The Resilience Appraisals Scale was based on the Schematic Appraisals Model of Suicide (SAMS) of suicide, which suggests three types of positive self-appraisals (emotion coping appraisals, situation coping appraisals, and social support appraisals) are important for promoting resilience. These are appraisals of the individual’s ability to cope with emotions, solve problematic situations, and gain social support; and these positive self-appraisals are believed to buffer the impact of negative life events on later suicidal ideation. PMID: 19906364, DOI: 10.1016/j.brat.2009.10.007
title: Resilience Appraisals Scale (RAS)

description: The Resilience Scale (RS) by Wagnild and Young contains 25 elements measuring resilience on five subscales: Self-Reliance, Meaningfulness, Equanimity, Perseverance, and Existential Aloneness. Subjects rate these statements on a scale of 1= Totally disagree to 7= Totally agree. https://doi.org/10.1891/1061-3749.17.2.105 A short form version, the RS-14, contains 14 elements. https://doi.org/10.1002/ijgo.14072
title: Resilience Scale (RS)

description: The Rewarded Antisaccade Task (RAS) is a task-based behavioral and oculomotor data structure used to assess cognitive control and reward sensitivity. It measures participants’ ability to inhibit reflexive eye movements (antisaccades) under neutral and reward conditions, capturing accuracy, error correction, latency, and dropout rates alongside subjective affect (VAS ratings). The intended outcome is to evaluate how reward modulation influences inhibitory control and attentional processes, often in studies of mood, sleep, and vulnerability to internalizing disorders.
title: Rewarded Antisaccade Task (RAS)

description:
The Locus of Control scale measures generalized expectancies for internal versus external control of reinforcement. Those with an internal locus of control believe that their own behavior determines events in their lives, while those with an external locus of control believe that life events are generally outside of their control. There are 29 items (including 6 filler items). Items representing external choices are summed, yielding a range from 0 to 23. Higher scores indicate greater levels of external locus of control.
https://www.mccc.edu/~jenningh/Courses/documents/Rotter-locusofcontrolhandout.pdf

description: Questions capture participants' experiences throughout a neutral non-social experience on Zoom, involving watching a non-emotionally evocative video. Questions capture participants' subjective emotional experience at baseline (alone_video_1a::alone_video_1c), after receiving instructions about the upcoming experience (i.e., of watching a video) and reflecting on the upcoming experience for two minutes (alone_video_2a::alone_video_2j), immediately after the experience (i.e., of watching a video; alone_video_3a::alone_video_3i), and after reflecting on the experience they just completed for two minutes  (alone_video_4a::alone_video_4k). Parts of their emotional experience assessed by items include anxiety, evaluation by others, and satisfaction with their performance.
title: SIMS Alone Experience Surveys

description: Questions capture participants' experiences throughout a dyadic non-evaluative and evaluative social experience on Zoom, involving having a 4-minute conversation with 1 other participant; participants were told they would not evaluate each other's performance following their interaction. Questions capture participants' subjective emotional experience at baseline, after receiving instructions about the upcoming experience (i.e., of having a conversation with 1 other participant, who would NOT evaluate them) and reflecting on the upcoming experience for two minutes, immediately after the experience (i.e., of having a conversation, and after reflecting on the experience they just completed for two minutes. Parts of their emotional experience assessed by items include anxiety, evaluation by others, and satisfaction with their performance.
title: SIMS Dyad/Group Evaluative and Nonevaluative Experience Surveys

description: The SLOAN Visual Acuity Test uses a set of optotypes, or specially designed letters, created by Dr. Louise Sloan in 1959 to assess visual acuity. These letters (C, D, H, K, N, O, R, S, V, Z) are standardized to ensure consistent legibility across different lines on the chart. They are commonly employed in Snellen charts and logMAR charts. The Sloan Contrast Sensitivity Test assesses a person's ability to distinguish objects from their background under varying levels of contrast. Unlike standard visual acuity tests that focus on identifying letters at high contrast, contrast sensitivity tests are important for detecting subtle vision issues that may not be noticeable in typical vision screenings. DOI: 10.1016/s0042-6989(96)00190-3
title: SLOAN Visual Acuity and Contrast Sensitivity Tests

description: This measure gathers information about participant satisfaction with text messages received as a study intervention component. Some of the items are related to participant alcohol use. They also assess characteristics of the text messages (i.e., understandability, personalization/tailoring), participant reactions to the content of the messages, participant perceptions of the messages' helpfulness and effect on their alcohol use, participant concerns about privacy, and participant satisfaction with the messages. This measure is intended to be administered following completion of the text messaging portion of the intervention and items are presented using 5-point rating scales or yes/no response options.

description: In the Schandry Task, participants were instructed to count their heartbeats silently, without feeling their pulse, during intervals of variable length (25, 35 and 45 seconds), with two trials for each interval (i.e., 6 trials in total). “SchandryHRDiff” reflects the average difference of participant-counted and ECG-recorded number of heartbeats across the six trials, using the following formula for differences for each trial:  (1 – (|recorded heartbeats - counted heartbeats|) /recorded heartbeats). Higher scores indicate better agreement between counted and recorded heart beats. Outliers more than 3SD from the mean were removed at the trial level before computing the average difference. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-8986.1981.tb02486.x

description: The Self-Determination Scale (SDS) was designed to assess individual differences in the extent to which people tend to function in a self-determined way. The SDS is a short‚ 10-item scale‚ with two 5-item subscales. The first subscale is awareness of oneself‚ and the second is perceived choice in ones actions. description:
The Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) is used to rate the affective dimensions of valance/pleasure, arousal, and dominance. For each dimension, participants mark on or between any of the five figures presented to them for a total 9-point rating scale. The figures range from smiling and happy to frowning and unhappy (valence), excited and wide-eyed to relaxed and sleepy (arousal), and small to large sizes (dominance).
https://doi.org/10.1016/0005-7916(94)90063-9
title: Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM)

description: The 5-item Smoker Self-Concept Scale and 4-item Abstainer Self-Concept Scale have been shown to predict smoking cessation. Subjects are asked to rate the extent to which they strongly agree or disagree with items designed to assess the importance of being a smoker or an abstainer/nonsmoker to their self-concept (Shadel & Mermelstein, 1996). The Alcohol Self-Concept Scale (ASCS) and the Cannabis Self-Concept Scale (CSCS) were adapted from the Smoker Self-Concept Scale. They assess drinking identity and cannabis use identity, respectively, as a tool to predict consumption, problems and cravings. DOI: 10.1007/BF02883391. DOI: 10.1016/0306-4603(95)00088-7. DOI: 10.1016/j.addbeh.2017.12.021. DOI: 10.1037/a0027640
title: Self-Concept Scale

description: A brief self-report measure that assesses adults’ identity consolidation and clinically relevant identity disturbance. DOI: 10.1080/15283488.2019.1566068
title: Self-Concept and Identity Measure (SCIM)

description:
Assesses perceptions of private and public self-consciousness
https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1559-1816.1985.tb02268.x
title: Self-Consciousness Scale

description: Self-Construal Scale
title: Self-Construal Scale

description: The Self-Determination Inventory (Shogren et al., 2017) measures the young adult’s own perception (self-report) and a parent/family member's perception of a young person's self determination (parent report). https://doi.org/10.1177/1534508418788168
title: Self-Determination Inventory (SDI)

description: The AASPIRE Outcomes Measurement Toolkit is a set of survey instruments developed or adapted to be accessible for use with neurodiverse samples that may include autistic participants or people with intellectual disability. The AASPIRE version of the Self-Determination Inventory evaluates personal sense of autonomy and confidence in ability to direct, plan, and achieve one's own goals. Adaptation of the Self-Determination Inventory (https://nda.nih.gov/data-structure/sdis01) by AASPIRE for increased accessibility. Original Self-Determination Inventory DOI: 10.1177/2165143415594335
title: Self-Determination Inventory (SDI) - AASPIRE

description: Descriptive norms perceptions related to helping prevent self-directed violence. Adapted from work by Aldrich (2014) https://doi.org/10.1080/07481187.2012.738763 and Mitchell et al (2021) and Banyard et al (2021) doi.org/10.3390/ijerph18168606 interpersonal violence models of social norms and from social norms measures related to SDV developed by Aldrich.
title: Self-Directed Violence Helping Social Norms

description: Self-reported self-efficacy and confidence in different social situations for individuals with a schizophrenia-spectrum diagnosis. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/1097-4679(199505)51:3<320::AID-JCLP2270510302>3.0.CO;2-%23
title: Self-Efficacy Scale for Schizophrenia

description: The Self-Efficacy for HIV Risk Behaviors measure is a self-report instrument designed to assess an individual’s confidence in their ability to engage in behaviors that reduce HIV transmission risk, such as consistent condom use, negotiating safer sex, refusing unsafe sex, or avoiding substance use before sexual activity. Grounded in Bandura’s Social Cognitive Theory, it evaluates perceived behavioral control as a predictor of preventive action and sustained risk reduction. The measure is commonly used in HIV prevention research and intervention studies to assess psychosocial determinants of behavior change and treatment outcomes.
title: Self-Efficacy for HIV Risk Behaviors

description: The Self-Efficacy for Parenting Adolescents Scale (SEPA) measures caregivers' confidence in managing key aspects of parenting adolescents, such as emotional support, communication, discipline, and academic guidance. It is a reliable tool used to assess self-efficacy in handling adolescent challenges.  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10826-021-02046-7
title: Self-Efficacy for Parenting Adolescents Scale (SEPA)

description:
The Self-Hate Scale (SHS) assesses an individual's level of self-hate over the past year. Items are rated from 1= Not at all true for me to 7= Very true for me. The average of all items is calculated, with higher ratings indicating higher levels of self-hate.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jad.2018.11.047
title: Self-Hate Scale (SHS)

description: The Self-Injurious Thoughts and Behaviors Interview (SITBI) assesses the presence, frequency, and characteristics of a wide range of self-injurious thoughts and behaviors, including suicidal ideation, suicide plans, suicide gestures, suicide attempts, and nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI). https://doi.org/10.1037/1040-3590.19.3.309 Continuation of https://nda.nih.gov/data-structure/sitbi01
title: Self-Injurious Thoughts and Behaviors Interview (SITBI) Part 2

description: Part 2 of https://nda.nih.gov/data-structure/sitbi01
title: Self-Injurious Thoughts and Behaviors Interview Part 2

description: This 18-item measure adaptation to the Self-Monitoring Scale (Snyder, 1974) is designed to target items with the highest correlation to the latent self-monitoring causal variable. High self-monitoring individuals tend to answer in the keyed direction. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.51.1.125
title: Self-Monitoring Scale

description: The Self-Objectification Beliefs and Behavior Scale (SOBBS) is a measure of self-objectification. Participants rate their agreement with 14 statements on a 5-point scale; higher scores indicate higher levels of self-objectification. There are two subscales - Observer's Perspective and Body as Self. https://doi.org/10.1177/0361684317692109
title: Self-Objectification Beliefs and Behavior Scale (SOBBS)

description: The Self-Rating Scale (SRS) is an eight item measure that assesses the presence of a 'defective self' cognitive schema as well as for beliefs that one deserves to suffer or be punished. The items in the scale describe feelings or situations that directly relate to masochistic ideation, self-directed anger, and feelings of worthlessness. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psychres.2011.12.050
title: Self-Rating Scale (SRS)

description:
The Self-Rating of the Effects of Alcohol (SRE) is a retrospective measure of the level of response to alcohol and asks participants to report the number of standard drinks (10–12 g of ethanol) required to experience the following behavioral effects of intoxication.

Schuckit MA, Smith TL, Tipp JE. The Self-Rating of the Effects of alcohol (SRE) form as a retrospective measure of the risk for alcoholism. Addiction. 1997 Aug;92(8):979-88. PMID: 9376780.
title: Self-Rating of the Effects of Alcohol (SRE)

description:
The Self-Referential Encoding Task (SRET) is a measure of depressotypic self-schemata. It measures individuals’ tendency to describe themselves using positive and negative adjectives as well as their self-relevant memory biases, or the tendency to remember positive and negative adjectives that they said did or did not describe them. 
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1037/0021-843X.90.4.286
title: Self-Referential Encoding Task

description: The Self-Reflection and Insight Scale (SRIS) measures self-consciousness. There are two factors: Self-Reflection (SRIS-SR) which measures the inspection and evaluation of one's thoughts, feelings, and behavior and Insight (SRIS-IN) which measures the clarity of understanding of one's thoughts, feelings, and behavior. Self-Reflection includes two scales: Engagement in self-reflection and Need for self-reflection. Items are coded on a six-point scale from strongly disagree to strongly agree. https://doi.org/10.2224%2Fsbp.2002.30.8.821
title: Self-Reflection and Insight Scale (SRIS)

description:
The Self-Stigma Scale is a subscale, apart of the Paediatric Self-Stigmatization Scale (PaedS). The PaedS is a scale developed for measuring stigma in children and adolescents; it consists of 4 subscales that measure societal devaluation (14 items), personal rejection (5 items), self-stigma (5 items) and secrecy of receiving mental health treatment (7 items).
DOI: 10.1016/j.eurpsy.2017.01.004
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eurpsy.2017.01.004
title: Self-Stigma Scale

description: The Self-Stigma of Depression Scale (SSDS) comprises 16 items representing subscales of Shame, Self-Blame, Social Inadequacy, and Help-Seeking Inhibition. The SSDS distinguishes self-stigma from perceptions of stigma by others, yields in-depth information about self-stigma of depression, and possesses good psychometric properties. Individual items are scored on a five-point scale.
title: Self-Stigma of Depression Scale (SSDS)

description: This is an adapted version of the 14-item questionnaire to be a clinician-reported measure of supervision practices. This measure was revised to be inclusive of clinicians who see adult clients and to prompt specifically about supervision practices as they relate to our CBT constructs of interest. The original measure is listed here:  doi: 10.1177/2633489521992553.
title: Self-reported Therapist Intervention Fidelity for Youth (SeRTIFY)

description:
The Semi-structured Assessment for Drug Dependence and Alcoholism (SSADDA) is a diagnostic instrument developed for studies of the genetics of substance use and associated disorders. The SSADDA provides more detailed coverage of specific drug use disorders, particularly cocaine and opioid dependence, than existing psychiatric diagnostic instruments. A computerized version of the SSADDA was developed to permit direct entry of subject responses by the interviewer. This study examines the diagnostic reliability of the SSADDA for substance use disorders and for other DSM-IV disorders that are commonly associated with substance use disorders.
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.drugalcdep.2005.04.005
title: Semi-Structured Assessment for Drug Dependence and Alcoholism (SSADDA)

description: Qualitative scales evaluating parent-child interaction during a variety of challenge situations. The authors observed sensitive and negative intrusive parenting behaviors during infancy and toddlerhood in conjunction with self-reported maternal beliefs about the importance of discipline/control and concerns regarding spoiling. Multiple regression techniques modeled interactions between parenting self-reported parenting beliefs and observed parenting behaviors to predict mother-reported child internalizing symptoms and mother-reported child externalizing symptoms. https://doi.org/10.1080/15295190903014604

description:
The SERI is a measure of frequency and efficacy (i.e., proximal change in the targeted emotion) of 9 strategies (e.g., social support seeking, self-medication) in regulation of a specified emotion type and intensity (e.g., severe anxiety) during the past 30 days.
doi.org/10.1037/t58777-000
title: Semi-Structured Emotion Regulation Interview-Adapted Version (SERI)

description:
The Sense of Agency Rating Scale (SOARS) is a measure for quantifying alterations to subject agency following hypnosis. SOARS includes factors of involuntariness and effortlessness, both of which correlate with hypnotizability. SOARS consists of 10 items on a 7-point Likert scale.

description: The Sexual and Gender Minority Conflict Tactics Scale 2 (SGM-CTS2) is a newly developed version of the Conflict Tactics Scale adapted to be culturally appropriate for SGM populations. title: Sexual and Gender Minority Conflict Tactics Scale 2 (SGM-CTS2)

description: Participants make a decision about either the color or shape of a target stimulus. On some trials, participants will make a decision about the same attribute as the prior trial ("stay" trials); on others, participants will make a decision about the other attribute ("switch" trials).

description: Patients and providers communicate shared decisions regarding treatment and care. The Shared Decision Making (SDM) Scale includes 9 items (scored as absent, partial, or complete) and is used for coding transcripts of audio from the subject's appointment with their mental health medication prescriber. Scores range from 0 to 27, with higher scores indicating more shared decision-making.
title: Shared Decision-Making Scale

description:
Sherer's Self-Efficacy Scale (SSES) is a brief self-report questionnaire designed to assess a person's overall confidence in their ability to handle challenges, persist, and succeed across a range of situations.

description:
The Shipley Institute of Living Scale (SILS; Shipley & Burlingame, 1941) is a measure of intellectual deficit and provides a screening measure for intelligence. The SILS contains two sections: a multiple choice vocabulary section and a verbal reasoning section. The vocabulary section consists of 40 multiple-choice items requiring participants to select the one word out of four choices that is closest in meaning to a target word. The abstract reasoning section consists of 20 items requiring participants to determine solutions for abstract verbal and arithmetic problems.
DOI: 10.1007/978-0-387-79948-3_1070
title: Shipley Institute of Living Scale

description: The Short revised almost perfect scale (SAPS) has 8 items scored on a 7-point scale. There are two subscales: Standards: How high are the standards people set for themselves. description: The Short Defeat and Entrapment Scale (SDES) is developed from the existing Defeat Scale (shortname: ds01) and Entrapment Scale (shortname: es01) (DOI: 10.1017/s0033291798006710). description: The Shortened Acceptance and Fusion Questionnaire for Youth (AFQ-Y8) is an 8-item self-report measure assessing psychological inflexibility in children and adolescents, specifically experiential avoidance and cognitive fusion. description:
Processes of sibling influence were assessed in the home interviews via younger siblings’ reports on an 18-item measure designed for this study. Specifically, on a scale ranging from 1 (never) to 5 (very often), youth answered questions about how much they tried to be like or different from their older sibling, the degree to which their older sibling set a positive or negative example, and the extent to which their sibling encouraged them to participate in
particular activities. 
DOI:10.1111/j.1741-3737.2010.00698.x

description: The Simultaneous Polydrug Use Questionnaire (DUQ) assesses frequency of simultaneous Alcohol and Marijuana (SAM) use over the past 12 months. Simultaneous use was defined as use of the two substances occurring within a few hours of each other. description:

Sine-wave speech (SWS) is a form of acoustically degraded speech, derived by synthesizing tones that track the amplitude and frequency of speech formants.

description: Six Item Cognitive Impairment Test (6CIT) is a brief cognitive function test which takes less than five minutes and is widely used in primary care settings ( Brooke & Bullock, 1999). ** The scale uses an inverse score and questions are weighted to produce a total out of 28. Scores of 0-7 are considered normal and 8 or more significant.

description: The Skills of Cognitive Therapy (SoCT) measure is used for depressed adults and their cognitive therapists. The 8-item SoCT assesses patients' understanding and use of basic cognitive therapy (CT) skills rated from the perspectives of both observers (SoCT-O) and patients (SoCT-P).

description: Electrodermal activity (EDA) is a reliable biomarker of arousal and part of the sympathetic nervous system response (George I. Christopoulos, 2016). Therefore, it is relevant to track changes in skin conductance during an induced stress task. Electrodermal activity has been shown to be a reliable tool for the classification of mental stress levels (Rahma et al., 2022).
title: Skin Conductance Response

description: The Skin Picking Impact Scale (SPIS) is a self-report instrument developed to assess the psychosocial consequences of repetitive skin picking. http://dx.doi.org/10.1176/appi.psy.42.5.397

description: The Skin Picking Scale (SPS) is modeled after the Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (https://nda.nih.gov/data-structure/ybocs01). It consists of eight individual items including frequency of urges, intensity of urges, time spent on skin picking, interference due to skin picking, distress associated with skin picking, resistance, degree of control, and avoidance. All items were rated on a five-point scale ranging from 0 (none) to 4 (extreme). https://doi.org/10.1016/S0022-3999(01)00215-X
title: Skin Picking Scale (SPS)

description: The Sleep Condition Indicator (SCI) evaluates insomnia symptoms and severity in clinical practice, based on the DSM-5. Eight items encompassed sleep continuity, sleep satisfaction/dissatisfaction, severity, and daytime consequences. Answers are scored from 0-4 with higher scores meaning better sleep; scores than 2 represent the threshold criteria for Insomnia Disorder. A two-item short form version, the SCI-02, is also available. https://doi.org/10.1136%2Fbmjopen-2013-004183
title: Sleep Condition Indicator (SCI)

description: The Sleep Disorders Symptom Checklist (SDS-CL) is an instrument of either 17 (SDS-CL-17) or 25 (SDS-CL-25) items developed to screen for sleep disorders including insomnia, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), restless leg syndrome/periodic limb movement disorder (RLS/PLMD), circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorders, narcolepsy, and parasomnias. Participants rate how frequently certain descriptions apply to them; each answer contributes to a disorder's subscore. https://doi.org/10.17241/smr.2017.00010
title: Sleep Disorders Symptoms Checklist

description: Evaluates the level of sleep disturbance in the Serious Mental Illness (SMI) population. The Neuro-QOL consists of 13 short forms assessing quality of life (QOL) across neurological conditions. The item banks include: anxiety, depression, fatigue, emotional and behavioral dyscontrol, sleep disturbance, ability to participate in social roles and activities, satisfaction with social roles and activities, applied cognition (general concerns and executive function), positive affect and well-being, stigma, upper extremity function (fine motor, ADL), and lower extremity function (mobility). DOI: 10.1212/WNL.0b013e318258f744

description:
The Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (SDSC) was designed both to evaluate specific sleep
disorders in children, and to provide an overall measure of sleep disturbance suitable for use in clinical screening and research. The SDSC consists of 26 items.

description: The Sleep and Vulnerability to Internalizing Disorders (SVID) framework is a longitudinal, neurodevelopmental research structure that examines how sleep duration influences reward processing, cognitive control circuits, and vulnerability to depression and anxiety. It integrates behavioral task performance metrics (reaction time and accuracy across emotionally valenced trials) with subjective affective state measures (VAS ratings of mood and arousal) and, in some studies, neuroimaging data. The intended outcome is to identify developmental pathways linking sleep disruption to altered cognitive-emotional processing and increased mental health risk.
title: Sleep and Vulnerability to Internalizing Disorders (SVID)

description: The Slosson Oral Reading Test-Revised 3 (SORT-R3) evaluates reading abilities on a grade or age-level equivalency. There are 10 lists of 20 words each of ascending grade levels. The total number of words pronounced correctly composes a 'raw' score, from which age and grade equivalents may be looked up using a table.
title: Slosson Oral Reading Test (SORT-R3)

description:

description: Snack Delay: This experiment measures a child’s self control skills and ability to delay responding. A motivating snack is placed in front of the child and they are asked to wait until the bell rings before retrieving the treat. After an explanation and demonstration there are 4 trials with delays of 5, 10, 15, and 20 seconds with a reminder of the rules before every trial. The DV is the number of trials with a full wait. The advanced version of this task is Delay of Gratification.
title: Snack Delay

description:
The Snack Delay episode from the Laboratory Temperament Assessment Battery - Preschool Version (Lab-TAB; (Goldsmith & Rothbart, 1996) requires subjects to wait for a signal before eating a snack placed in front of them for six trials, with the wait time for the signal changing for each trial. Fidgeting, distractibility, and inhibitory control are rated for each trial.

DOI: 10.1016/j.ridd.2014.09.015

description: The Social Effort Task is a behavioral paradigm used to assess how much effort an individual is willing to exert to obtain social rewards such as approval or positive feedback. description:
Social Loss Aversion Task - decision-making task in which participants choose between accepting a gamble or rejecting the gamble in favor of the safe option under three task conditions (self, other, stranger). Decisions are made first in a baseline ‘self ’ condition (i.e., outcomes of choices affected only themselves), and two other sets of identical choices were made in two ‘other’ conditions for a close friend that was brought with them and for a stranger they met the day of the experimental session.
Post Task Ratings - assesses participants’ feelings towards losing or winning money during the Social Loss Aversion (SLA) task during each of the task conditions (self, other, stranger).
title: Social Loss Aversion Task and Post Task Ratings

description:
The Social Media and Video Game Measure was created to assess social function and interaction by way of social media use and video game use. Recent literature suggests that people with psychosis are more likely to use anonymous online social interactions such as Reddit, or avatar based games, rather than platforms that foster online interactions amongst people that know each other offline (Fett et al., 2016). The screen time portion was taken from the ABCD screen time survey.
We are also looking at how video games may affect psychosis symptoms. There is little research regarding this association, so insights on this subject could provide foundation for further research.
https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291716000015
title: Social Media and Video Game Measure

description:
The Social Relationships Index (SRI) was designed to examine positivity and negativity in social relationships. Unique features of this scale include its brevity and the ability to examine relationship positivity and negativity at the level of the specific individual and social network. The SRI instructs individuals to list the initials of individuals in the following domains: (a) spouse / significant other, (b) father, (c) mother, (d) other family, (e) friends, (f) co-workers, and (g) social acquaintances.
https://doi.org/10.1002/jcop.20308

description: The Social Skills Improvement System Social-Emotional Learning Edition (SSIS-SEL) is a comprehensive, evidence-based, social-emotional learning system that assesses key academic skills and integrates the different components with an aligned, multi-tiered intervention. The SSIS-SEL is teacher-completed, and uses a criterion-referenced set of rubrics to describe social emotional learning skills in 5 domains (self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationships, and responsible decision making) and academic enabling skills in 3 domains (motivation to learn, reading, and mathematics).

description: The Social Tolerance Index (STI) examines the perceptions about personal tolerance to alcohol using social cues. description: The Social and Behavioral Determinants of Health (SBDH) contains SBDH information manually annotated from 7,025 discharge summary notes in the MIMIC-III dataset. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0082131

description:
Th e 20-item Somatoform Dissociation Questionnaire (SDQ-20; Nijenhuis et al., 1996)

in clinical settings had appeared upon activation of particular dissociative parts of the
personality and that could not be medically explained. Th e items pertain to both negative (e.g., analgesia) and positive dissociative phenomena (e.g., site-specific pain). 

 DOI: 10.1111/j.1600-0447.1997.tb09922.x

description: The Stanford Social Dimensions Scale is a 71-item questionnaire used to assess social behaviors and skills, such as initiating social interaction, and can be used as an accompanying diagnostic tool for deeming autistic characteristics. The first 58 items are relevant to those of any language level but the last 13 are related to those that are verbal.

description: The Stanford Acuity Test (StAT) is an advanced visual acuity exam designed to improve the precision and efficiency of traditional eye tests. It incorporates Bayesian techniques and artificial intelligence to reduce prediction errors and improve the reliability of results. StAT dynamically adapts to each user's responses, accounting for uncertainty and adjusting to prior data, leading to a significant reduction in test error and faster completion times. It is highly effective for diagnosing vision impairments and tracking changes, particularly in patients with eye diseases. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1609/aaai.v34i01.5384
title: Stanford Visual Acuity Test (StAT)

description: The State Mindfulness Scale (SMS) is a self-report questionnaire designed to measure an individual's mindfulness at a specific moment in time, rather than as a trait or long-term characteristic. It assesses mindfulness across two dimensions: mindfulness of the body and mindfulness of the mind, capturing a person’s awareness of both physical sensations and mental activities during a given moment. The SMS is used in mindfulness-based research to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions aimed at increasing present-moment awareness. DOI: 10.31231/osf.io/3x7ck

description: Participants were randomly assigned to one of three conditions: stress (socially evaluated cold pressor) immediately before learning, stress ~15 minutes before learning, or no stress. Memory was assessed 24h later to isolate stress effects on encoding/learning rather than retrieval. https://osf.io/preprints/psyarxiv/546nv

description: The Subjective Vitality Scale (SVS) is a self-report instrument to measure one’s level of vitality, which is a positive feeling of aliveness and energy. Ryan and Frederick (1997) developed two versions: Individual Difference Level Version, an ongoing characteristic of individuals, and State Level Version which assesses the state of subjective vitality rather than its enduring aspect. The original scale had 7 items and was validated at both levels by Ryan and Frederick (1997). Subsequent work by Bostic, Rubio, and Hood (2000) using confirmatory factor analyses indicated that a 6-item version worked even better than the 7-item version. DOI 10.1111/j.1467-6494.1997.tb00326.x and DOI 10.1023/A:1007136110218
title: Subjective Vitality Scale (SVS)

description:

description: The Suicide Stroop Task is a modified Stroop task designed to measure attentional bias toward suicide-related and negatively valanced stimuli. Participants are shown different words and are timed how quickly they identify the color of the word. Interference for suicide-related stimuli and negatively valanced stimuli were calculated by subtracting latencies for neutral words from latencies for suicide-related words and latencies for negatively valanced words, respectively. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0019710

description: The Sussex-Oxford Compassion for Others Scale (SOCS-O) and Sussex-Oxford Compassion for the Self Scale (SOCS-S) are self-report measures for compassion towards others and the self. The five subscales - recognizing suffering, understanding the universality of suffering, feeling for the person suffering, tolerating uncomfortable feelings, and motivation to act/acting to alleviate suffering - are based on the definition of compassion. https://doi.org/10.1177/1073191119860911
title: Sussex-Oxford Compassion Scale (SOCS)

description: The Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART) is a computerized test designed to assess an individual’s sustained attention, by asking participants to respond quickly to presented stimuli, while withholding responses when a specific stimulus (i.e. the number 3) appears. Responses to the non-target stimulus (i.e. the number 3), indicate a lapse in attention. The SART-2 provides visual feedback for the participant when pressing the correct button. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-012-0266-1
title: Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART)

description: This measure is used to evaluate participant’s reactions to eating sweets, craving for sweets, and degree of control over eating sweet food. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eatbeh.2005.09.005

description: The Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT) is a screening instrument commonly used in clinical and research settings to assess neurological dysfunction. Like other substitution tasks, performance on the SDMT is underpinned by attention, perceptual speed, motor speed, and visual scanning. In addition to its clinical utility, the SDMT features in many studies of age-related cognitive decline. Participants are required to use a coded key to match nine abstract symbols paired with numerical digits. Participants were given 10 practice items before commencing the test. The final score is the correct number of substitutions in 90 seconds, and scores range between 0 and 110. https://doi.org/10.1093/arclin/acu055
title: Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT)

description: The Symbol Imagery Test (SIT) is used to measure participant's symbol imagery for sounds and letters and phonological awareness. Participants are asked to recall non-words and spell one syllable and multisyllable non-words. SIT - Bell, N. (2010). Symbol Imagery Test Examiner's Manual. Gander Publishing.

description: The SDM_model_elements structure is a metadata wrapper for system dynamics (SD) model iterations, storing links to files that contain each iteration’s parameters, variable lists, time-series outputs, narrative results, descriptive notes, and additional metadata. This kind of structure supports transparent, reproducible modelling by tracking how an SD model is specified, calibrated, and refined over multiple runs used for policy and service-planning decisions.
title: System Dynamics Model

description: The TELE-ASD-PEDS-Preschool, or TAP-P, was designed specifically for caregiver-mediated telemedicine evaluation of Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD). It is based on the ASD-PEDS; both are scored based on clinicians' ratings on seven behaviors. Each behavior is rated dichotomously (yes/no) and on a Likert scale of 3= Behaviors of characteristic ASD clearly present; 2= Behaviors characteristic of ASD present at subclinical levels; 1= Behaviors characteristic of ASD not present. There are two versions - one for verbal and one for non/minimally verbal children. This is the NON-VERBAL version. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-023-06176-3
title: TELE-ASD-PEDS-Preschool - Non-Verbal (TAP-P - NV)

There are two versions - one for verbal and one for non/minimally verbal children. This is the VERBAL version. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-023-06176-3

description:
Adaptative Delay Discounting (2 min)
Cognitive test assessing decision-making. Participants indicate whether they would prefer differing amounts of hypothetical money now vs. in the future.
title: TMB Adaptive Delay Discounting

description:
The TestMyBrain Choice Reaction Time subscale of the TestMyBrain battery specifically tests reaction time during decision-making. The TestMyBrain Simple Reaction Time subscale of the TestMyBrain battery specifically tests general reaction time.
https://doi.org/10.3389/fnhum.2015.00131
DOI: 10.3758/bf03213780
title: TMB Choice/Simple Reaction Time

description:
TMB Classify Test (5 min)
Participants are presented with the same negative and neutral stimuli as those in the TMB Emotional Stroop. They are asked to rate each image as Neutral or Negative based on their own feelings about the images. These ratings contextualize the TMB Emotional Stroop results and may inform data cleaning decisions.
title: TMB Classify Test

description:
*Digit Symbol Matching (2 min)
Participants have to match a set of symbols to the numbers 1, 2, or 3, based on a key presented on screen. This is a test of processing speed.
title: TMB Digit Symbol Matching

description: Recall sequences of digits of increasing length, either in the same order as presented (forward digit span) or in the opposite order (backward digit span). This test measures auditory span, short term memory, attention, and working memory (backward version). The two subtests can be administered alone/individually or together for any given project.
title: TMB Digital Span

description:
7) Emotional Stroop (7 min)
A classic stroop of cognitive interference, however using threat and neutral scenes instead of color words. Scenes will be presented in blue, yellow, or red. Scenes will depict either neutral content (e.g. inside of a building) or threat content (e.g. violent scenes). See Threat Stimuli Images. We will then look at reaction times for threat vs neutral images. See Threat Stimuli Images.
title: TMB Emotional Stroop

description:
Gradual Onset Continuous Performance Test (7 min)
Participants see a series of city or mountain scenes. The participant is asked to press a button whenever they see a city scene and withhold a response whenever they see a mountain scene. This a test of sustained attention and response inhibition.
title: TMB Gradual Onset Continuous Performance Test

description:
Matrix Reasoning (10 min)
Participants solve a series of visual puzzles. This is a test of nonverbal reasoning and general cognitive ability.
title: TMB Matrix Reasoning

description:
Multiple Object Tracking (5 min)
Cognitive test assessing visuospatial working memory. Participants track dots as they move across the screen.
title: TMB Multiple Object Tracking

description:
TMB Multiracial Face Emotion Recognition (5 min)
Participants have to choose which of four emotion words best describes the emotion being expressed by a participant.
title: TMB Multiracial Emotion Recognition

description: Connect a series of numbers in ascending order (Part A) or numbers and letters in alternate ascending order (Part B). This test measures processing speed, task switching (Part B), and cognitive flexibility (Part B). The two subtests can be administered alone/individually or together for any given project.
title: TMB Trail Making Test

description:
Visual Paired Associates Memory (5 min)
Cognitive test assessing visual memory. Participants learn a set of picture pairs and have to indicate which pictures go together based on the set they learned.
title: TMB Visual Paired Associates Memory

description: Vocabulary battery within the TestMyBrain (TMB) tool. Participants are asked to select the word that is closest in meaning to the target word. The TMB Vocabulary test was modeled after the well-validated Wordsum test used in the General Social Survey (Smith, Marsden & Hout, 2013). https://doi.org/10.1080/13854046.2020.1811893
title: TMB Vocabulary

description:
TMB Word Sentence Association Paradigm (4 min)
Given an ambiguous sentence, participants decide which of two words provides the best interpretation of the sentence. This test measures negative interpretation biases in information processing.
title: TMB Word Sentence Association Paradigm

description: The tapping and beat synchronization task assesses participants' ability to synchronize with an auditory beat. It is a tapping task including indices of tapping accuracy (mean asynchrony) and consistency (asynchrony SD), measured at two stimulus rates (2Hz and 3.2Hz).

description: Three measures assess executive function: switching flexibly between tasks or mental sets. In Number Letter Task Switching, participants make a classification about a target in terms of property of number (even or odd) or property of letter (vowel or consonant) depending on position of number-letter pair to center line (above or below). In Category Task Switching, participants make a classification about a target (living-nonliving or smaller-larger than a soccer ball) depending on the cue (cross or heart). In Color Shape Task Switching, participants make a classification about a target in terms of color (red or green) or shape (circle of triangle) depending on the cue (C or S). https://doi.org/10.1177/0963721411429458
title: Task Switching Assessments

description: After drinking each 3 ml sample from a plastic tasting cup, subjects rate each sample via E-Prime software by answering “How much do you like the flavor?” and “How intense is the flavor?” Subjects receive one sample of each stimuli per “block” in the taste test, with a total of two blocks and four total samples. The purpose is to acquire the potential covariates of flavor intensity and liking for adjusting analyses of BOLD signal activation.
title: Taste Test Subjective Ratings

description: The Teen Transition Inventory (TTI) is a measure of psychosexual functioning of adolescents with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) compared to Typically Developing (TD) adolescents. It covers the three domains: psychosexual socialization, psychosexual selfhood and sexual/intimate behavior. The TTI is aimed at individuals between the ages of 12 and 21 years old. To obtain multiple perspectives, the TTI consists of a parent-report version and a self-report version. Both versions have considerable overlap in items, however, the parent-report includes additional items and scales on topics that parents were considered to be better able to adequately judge and reliably report on and the self-report includes additional items and scales regarding very private and subjective matters. DOI: 10.1007/s10803-017-3071-y
title: Teen Transition Inventory (TTI)

The TCU Drug Screen 5 screens for mild to severe substance use disorder, and is particularly useful when determining placement and level of care in treatment.
