description: The Adolescent Life Goal Profile Scale (ALGPS) provides a new approach to the measurements of personal goals, meaning, structure and direction in adolescent lives—a focus that falls in line with the increasing awareness of the prospects and benefits of congruent living. The objective is to measure the perceived importance of four life goal categories (Relations, Generativity, Religion and Achievements) and the perceived attainability of these goals. Participants used a 5-point Likert scale measuring the importance/attainability of each goal. https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1007/s10902-011-9306-2
title: The Adolescent Life Goal Profile Scale (ALGPS)

description: The Adolescent Self-Regulatory Inventory (ASRI) is a theoretically-based questionnaire that taps two temporal aspects of self-regulation (regulation in the short- and long-term). Distinguishing between short- and long-term temporal frames is crucial because of changes taking place in adolescence. Questions assess the degree to which adolescents or adults are able to activate, monitor, maintain, inhibit and adapt their emotions, thoughts, attention, and behavior. Respondents rate how true each item is for them, ranging from 1 (not at all true for me) to 5 (really true for me). https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1007/s10964-006-9107-9
title: The Adolescent Self-Regulatory Inventory (ASRI)

description: The scale consists of 11 items designed to measure self-reported aggressive behaviors among middle school students (sixth, seventh, and eighth graders). The scale is brief, is easy to administer, and focuses on overt behaviors. Thus, the Aggression Scale could be a useful tool for program evaluation and for further research on violence prevention in schools.
title: The Aggression Scale

description:
This structure is based on the 12-item version of the AASE provides a brief clinical method for measuring self efficacy domains of confidence and temptation. Moreover, use of the measure provides a single item indicator of the four domains originally identified within the 40-item scale: negative affect, social pressure, physical pain/illness, and thoughts about using.

https://doi.org/10.1080/1533256X.2011.593445
title: The Alcohol Abstinence Self-Efficacy Scale

description:
The Altarum Consumer Engagement Measure (ACE Measure) is a scientifically-validated survey tool that measures three domains of patient engagement with just 12 questions. The instrument was created to broaden the scope of how engagement is measured and understood, and to update the concept of engagement to include modern information sources, such as online health resources and ratings of providers and patient health.
doi: 10.1007/s40271-015-0142-z.
title: The Altarum Consumer Engagement Measure

description:
Measures gastroparesis symptoms and severity.
Revicki DA, Lavoie S, Speck RM, Puelles J, Kuo B, Camilleri M, Almansa C, Parkman HP. The content validity of the ANMS GCSI-DD in patients with idiopathic or diabetic gastroparesis. J Patient Rep Outcomes. 2018 Dec 13;2(1):61. doi: 10.1186/s41687-018-0081-2. PMID: 30547386; PMCID: PMC6292831.
title: The American Neurogastroenterology and Motility Society Gastroparesis

description: The Amsterdam Executive Function Inventory (AEFI) is a newly developed brief self-report questionnaire to assess three important components of the executive aspects of daily-life behavior—that is, Attention, Self-Control and Self-Monitoring, and Planning and Initiative. https://doi.org/10.1080/13803395.2011.625353
title: The Amsterdam Executive Function Inventory

description: The ASHI, adapted from the Social Motivation Interview, assesses the following dimensions of social health: person’s satisfaction with their interpersonal relationships, motivation to socialize, quantity of social relationships and encounters, and willingness to persist in social interaction.  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-019-04311-7
title: The Autistic Social Health Interview (ASHI)
The Awareness of Social Inference Test (TASIT)

description:
The BMPN scale is designed to measure subject well-being and is intended to be an improvement over the BPNS (Basic Psychological Needs Scale). It asks 18 questions to which the subject rates their agreement.

DOI 10.1007/s11031-012-9279-4
title: The Balanced Measure of Psychological Needs (BMPN) Scale

description:
The Barriers and Facilitators Scale was originally developed to assess barriers to and facilitators of receiving health care for African American men seeking men (ie, men seeking homosexual relationships). Our research partners (ie, people in recovery who were co-researchers for the study) reworded several of the items from the original measure to ask about accessing health care in general. Reliability analysis of our sample showed that the 45 items on the Barriers subscale had a Cronbach α of 0.79 and the 33 items on the Facilitators scale had a Cronbach α of 0.79.
Bradford JB. Peer health navigation toolbox element, barriers and facilitators. HPTN 061 peer health navigators operations manual version 2.0. HIV Prevention Trials Network (HPTN) website.
title: The Barriers and Facilitators Scale

description: The Brazilian Economic Classification Criteria survey (Critério de Classificação Econômica Brasil, or CCEB), included data about different household possessions, such as freezers, color televisions, and cars, as well as the level of education of the head of the family. Each possession and quantity of items, as well as the level of education, were attributed a certain number of  points. The total number of points was used to classify the individual into one of the following 8 classes: A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, C2, D, and E.
title: The Brazilian Criteria of Economic Classification

description: The Brief Autism Mealtime Behavior Inventory (BAMBI) was developed as the first standardized informant report measure to capture mealtime and feeding behaviors explicitly in children with ASD. The BAMBI was developed in response to the limitations of other measures not being sensitive to the behaviors found in children with ASD
title: The Brief Autism Mealtime Behavior Inventory(BAMBI)

description: The Brief Index of Sexual Functioning for Women (BISF-W) is a self-report assessment of current levels of female sexual functioning and satisfaction. Some BISF-W items were adapted from the Brief Sexual Function Questionnaire (BSFQ) for men (https://doi.org/10.1016/0165-1781(88)90106-0) which measures sexual interest, activity, satisfaction, and preference in men. Citation: Taylor, J.F., Rosen, R.C. & Leiblum, S.R. Self-report assessment of female sexual function: Psychometric evaluation of the brief index of sexual functioning for women. Arch Sex Behav 23, 627–643 (1994). https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01541816
title: The Brief Index of Sexual Functioning

description: Brief Marijuana Consequences Questionnaire - 21 item version of the 50 item MACQ (Marijuana Consequences Questionnaire), which was modeled after the YAACQ (Young Adult Alcohol Consequences Questionnaire. Simons et al. (2012) https://doi.org/10.1016/j.addbeh.2012.01.008
title: The Brief Marijuana Consequences Questionnaire

description:
This structure is based on The Brief Young Adult Alcohol Consequences Questionnaire and The Young Adult Alcohol Consequences Questionnaire (YAACQ).

The Brief Young Adult Alcohol Consequences Questionnaire (B-YAACQ; Kahler et al., 2005). It was developed using item response modeling to provide a brief and readily interpretable measure of negative alcohol consequences over the past year among college students.
DOI: 10.1097/01.alc.0000171940.95813.a5

The Young Adult Alcohol Consequences Questionnaire (YAACQ) is a 48-item measure designed to capture a broad range of alcohol-related consequences (Read et al., 2006; doi: 10.15288/jsa.2006.67.169).
title: The Brief Young Adult Alcohol Consequences Questionnaire

description:
This structure is a multi-faceted clinician-administered measure of incompleteness. The Brown Incompleteness Scale (BINCS) consists of various items; each rated on a 5-point scale, with higher scores indicating a greater degree of incompleteness.

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jocrd.2017.12.008
title: The Brown Incompleteness Scale

description: The Career Engagement Scale measures of the degree to which somebody is proactively developing his or her career as expressed by diverse career behaviors. https://doi.org/10.1177/1069072713514813
title: The Career Engagement Scale

description:
The CCAS-S is designed to identify the deficits in executive function, linguistic processing, spatial cognition and affect regulation that accompany Cerebellar Cognitive Affective Syndrome.  
https://doi.org/10.1093/brain/awx317
title: The Cerebellar Cognitive Affective/Schmahmann Syndrome Scale

description: This survey is designed to collect data that would allow for examining the impact of COVID-19 stressors on hazardous drinking in a longitudinal samples of sexual minority women, the Chicago and Life Experiences of Women (CHLEW) study. Questions pertaining to the impact of COVID-19 and hazardous drinking were framed using a specific time period: between April 1, 2020 and March 31, 2021.
title: The Chicago and Life Experiences of Women (CHLEW) COVID-19 Survey

description: Child Monitoring questionnaire is a 60-item self-report used to gauge adolescent perceptions of caregiver (e.g., mother, father, aunt, uncle, parent’s significant other, other) child monitoring. Adolescents are asked to answer questions regarding the caregiver’s knowledge of the teen’s life and frequency of how much the identified caregiver tries to influence and control aspects of the teen’s life in different areas (e.g., choice of friends, who they are, what they are like).
title: The Child Monitoring Questionnaire

description:
The CREV is a self-report questionnaire developed to serve as a screening instrument for children (aged 9 to 15 years). The instrument comprises 32 items, 29 of which ask respondents to rate the frequency of their lifetime exposure to various types of community violence. The children are asked whether they have "ever" been exposed to specific acts of violence whereby frequency is evaluated on a 5-point Likert scale using the following categories: no/never, one time, a few times, many times, or every day.
https://doi.org/10.1097/00004583-199502000-00015
title: The Children's Report of Exposure to Violence

description: The Children’s and Adolescents’ Sleep Environment Scale (CASES) is a 13-item parent-report questionnaire designed to assess environmental factors influencing sleep among youth aged 5–18. It evaluates domains such as environmental hazards, bedding conditions, and the presence of electronics, with the goal of predicting sleep quality and daytime functioning beyond traditional sleep hygiene measures. doi.org/10.5664/jcsm.10110
title: The Children’s and Adolescents’ Sleep Environment Scale (CASES)

description: Screening assessment for violence
title: The Columbia Initial Screen for Violence

description: A self-report instrument for older children and adults with communication impairments or a self-report instrument focusing on pragmatic difficulties https://www.researchgate.net/publication/215626897_Communication_Checklist_-_Self-Report_CC-SR
title: The Communication Checklist Self Report(CC-SR)

description: The Comprehensive Assessment of Traits relevant to Personality Disorder (CAT-PD) was born from a federally funded project designed to develop a comprehensive model and efficient measure of traits related to personality pathology.  The model includes 33 facet traits of personality pathology, which can be represented within a broader PSY-5 structure of personality traits (Wright & Simms, 2014), consistent with the Alternative Model of Personality Disorder (AMPD) in DSM-5.
title: The Comprehensive Assessment of Traits relevant to Personality Disorder (CAT-PD)

description: Evaluates parent's behaviors towards their young children (1-4 years). DOI: https://doi.org/10.1080/17405629.2016.1182017
title: The Comprehensive Early Childhood Parenting Questionnaire (CECPAQ)

description: App experiences is a measurement with open-ended and closed-ended questions modified from the The Computer-Mediated Communication Questionnaire (CMCQ) (Yen & Tu, 2011) is administered at three weeks and post intervention. The CMCQ measures attitudes to computer mediated communication, consisting of four factors: (social context, privacy, interactivity, and online communication) α = .83. The questionnaire modified from the CMCQ measures acceptability of the app, perceptions of social interactivity, security and privacy, interactions with the facilitator, barriers to using the app, mood states while using the app, and app contents. A more in-depth semistructured qualitative interview with probes for each scaled response was assessed using a convenience sample of at least 15 participants.
title: The Computer-Mediated Communication Questionnaire (CMCQ)

description: Confidence Expectation Reward Computation Mechanism (CER) dataset consists of measurements for participants performance during a simple perceptual decision-making task under various conditions. It comprises 3 items assessing participants Type-1 perceptual or cognitive performance and 2 items evaluating Type-2 metacognitive performance. Additionally, the dataset includes 4 items describing the specific condition of a given trial.
title: The Confidence Expectation Reward Computation Mechanism

description:
The Cornell Medical Index (abbreviated C.M.I.) is a self-administered instrument that collects a large body of significant medical and psychiatric data without the physician's participation, so that the physician may have available, even before he interviews the patient, information on which to base tentative diagnostic appraisals of the patient's total medical problem. It is a self-report questionnaire in which the respondent is asked questions about the presence of symptoms in each of 18 areas, including the nervous system, fatigability, depression, anxiety and tension (Brodman et al., 1949).
doi:10.1001/jama.1951.02920210024006
title: The Cornell Medical Index

description:
The Coronavirus Health and Impact Survey (CRISIS) was developed for use as an easy to implement and robust questionnaire covering key domains relevant to mental distress and resilience during the pandemic.

https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-87270-3
title: The Coronavirus Health Impact Survey

description:
Creature of Habit: A self-report measure of habitual routines and automatic tendencies in everyday life 
Karen D. Ersche, Tsen-Vei Lim, Laetitia H.E. Ward, Trevor W. Robbins, Jan Stochl

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2017.04.024
title: The Creature of Habit Scale

description: The DSM Cannabis Symptoms Questionnaire - To assess problematic levels of substance use and to enable diagnosis, a highly structured paper and pencil version of the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-IV is administered. This version was adapted by Molina, in consultation with PAARC staff, for use by bachelor-level interviewers. The SCID was initially adapted by staff at PAARC to include substance abuse and dependence symptoms relevant for adolescents, and to be commensurate with DSM-IV diagnostic criteria.
title: The DSM Cannabis Symptoms Questionnaire (DCSQ)

description:
The Depression Coping Self-Efficacy Scale (DCSES) was developed to measure depressed individuals' confidence in their ability to follow treatment recommendations. The DCSES is a 24-item assessment to measure coping self-efficacy of depressed psychiatric patients on a scale of 0% confident to 100% confident. (DCSES; Perraud, 2000)
https://doi.org/10.1037/t59673-000
title: The Depression Coping Self-Efficacy Scale (DCSES)

description: The Developmental, Dimensional and Diagnostic Interview (3Di) is a standardized, computer-based diagnostic interview in which individual scores of children in both clinical and general populations with suspected autism spectrum disorder (ASD) can be scored in terms of their severity, frequency, and comorbidity. The short version (3Di-sv) is a validated shortened 53 item form of the 3Di. DOIs https://doi.org/10.1097/00004583-200405000-00008 (full version), https://doi.org/10.1007/s00787-009-0004-0 and https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyt.2024.1234929 (short version)
title: The Developmental, Dimensional and Diagnostic Interview Short Version (3Di-sv)

description: The Digital Parental Mediation Scale (DPMS) is a 44-item scale pertaining to digital technology and social media. The adolescent version of the DPMS asks adolescents to report on interactions with their parents regarding digital technology and social media. The parent version of the DPMS is used to measure parents’ attitudes and parenting practices related to digital and social media.
title: The Digital Parental Mediation Scale

description:
The Discrimination and Stigma Scale was specifically developed to measure experienced and anticipated discrimination reported by people with mental health problems.
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.schres.2019.07.018
title: The Discrimination and Stigma Scale

description: The Disturbing Dream and Nightmare Severity Index (DDNSI) is a questionnaire that specifically measures nightmares and is used in clinical settings
title: The Disturbing Dream and Nightmare Severity Index (DDNSI)

description: The Drinker Inventory of Consequences (DrInC) is based on the Inventory of Drug Use Consequences (InDUC-2L).
title: The Drinker Inventory of Consequences

description:
The Early Childhood Screening Assessment (ECSA) is parent-report instrument that contains 36-child items assessing child emotional and behavioral problems. Items are scored on a Likert scale ranging from 0 (never/rarely) to 2 (always/almost always) and a clinical cutoff score of 18 results in strong sensitivity and specificity. Item stems are written at a fifth grade reading level.

 https://doi.org/10.1002/imhj.20259
DOI: 10.1177/0009922816638077
doi: 10.1097/DBP.0000000000000384
title: The Early Childhood Screening Assessment (ECSA)

description: The Executive Interview (EXIT25) is a clinically based measure of executive control functions. EXIT25 scores are used to identify the presence and severity of executive impairments and to predict problems in self-care, functional status, decision-making capacity, and problem behavior. It assesses the cognitive functioning in older adults. Royall, D. R., Mahurin, R. K., & Gray, K. F. (1992). Beside Assessment of the Executive Cognitive Impairment: The Executive Interview. Journal of the American Geriatrics Society, 40, 1221–1226.
title: The Executive Interview (EXIT 25)

description: The Financial Strain Questionnaire, adapted from The Economic Strain Model by Pearlin et al, includes 9-items rated on a scale from one to four. Total scores range from 9 to 35. Higher scores indicate greater financial strain. https://doi.org/10.5993%2FAJHB.41.4.14
title: The Financial Strain Questionnaire

description: The Friendship Scale, is a six items measure, that measures six of the seven important dimensions that contribute to social isolation and its opposite, social connection.
title: The Friendship Scale

description:
The Gilles de la Tourette Syndrome-Quality of Life Scale (GTS-QOL) is a quality of life measure for individuals with Tourette Syndrome or a Chronic Tic Disorder. The GTS-QoL contains 27, 5-point items asking how problematic different areas of life have been over the past 4 weeks and one visual analog scale asking how satisfied they feel with life.

DOI: 10.3233/BEN-120274
title: The Gilles de la Tourette Syndrome-Quality of Life Scale

description:
The Gratitude Questionnaire-Six-Item Form (GQ-6) is a six-item self-report questionnaire
designed to assess individual differences in the proneness to experience gratitude in daily life.
McCullough, M. E., Emmons, R. A., & Tsang, J. (2002). The grateful disposition: A conceptual
and empirical topography. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 82, 112-127.
title: The Gratitude Questionnaire (GQ-6)

description:
Patients ranked a list of 43 life events based on a relative score. Each event, called a Life Change Unit (LCU), had a different ‘weight’ for stress. More events mean a higher score. The higher the score, and the larger the weight of each event, the more likely the patient would become ill. 
DOI: 10.1093/occmed/kqx099
title: The Holmes Rahe Life Stress Inventory

description:
The DWECK-E measure, also known as the Implicit Theories of Emotion scale, and it is based on Carol Dweck's Implicit Theories of Intelligence Scale. The theories of emotion scale was developed for use by Maya Tamir, Oliver P. John, Sanjay Srivastava, and James J. Gross in 2007, based on Carol Dweck’s Implicit Theories of Intelligence scale (Dweck, 1999). It’s used to measure beliefs about the malleable nature of emotion. There are four items, and the scale takes less than five minutes to complete.
https://doi.org/10.4324/9781315783048
title: The Implicit Theories of Emotion Scale (DWECK-E)

description: The Personality Inventory for ICD-11 is a 60-item self-report measure of the dimensional trait model for the ICD-11. Five scales containing twelve items each are rated from 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree) to assess five maladaptive trait domains. https://bmcpsychiatry.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12888-024-05640-3#:~:text=The%20Personality%20Inventory%20for%20ICD,each%20domain)%20%5B61%5D.
title: The Informant Personality Inventory for ICD-11 (IPiC)

description:
The Interpersonal Hopelessness Scale (IHS) assesses suicide ideation through hopelessness, specifically in the context of thwarted belongingness and perceived burdensomeness, and students will complete this measure. The IHS consists of 10 items rated on a 5-point scale.
https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/10731911231161766
title: The Interpersonal Hopelessness Scale

The Irritability and Dysregulation of Emotion Scale (TIDES-13) to test whether irritability consisted of several sub-dimensions that would correlate differentially with internalizing/externalizing psychopathology, age, and gender.

doi: https://doi.org/10.1101/2022.02.08.22270624
title: The Irritability and Dysregulation of Emotions Questionnaire-13 (TIDES-13)

description:
The KMSK scale is a relatively rapid method to quantify self-exposure to opioids (including heroin and illicitly used prescription opioids), cocaine, alcohol, tobacco and cannabis, as well as other substances. The KMSK scale assesses the frequency, amount, and duration of exposure to each substance during a person’s period of greatest use (lifetime score). For each substance, the scale also has a separate form for measurement of use in the last 30 days. The KMSK also captures simple information on age trajectory, especially age of first use and age of heaviest use.

69:137-50. PubMed PMID: 12609695.
title: The Kreek-McHugh-Schluger-Kellogg (KMSK) Scale

description:
The LGBT People of Color Microaggressions Scale is an 18-item self-report scale assessing the unique types of microaggressions experienced by ethnic minority LGBT adults. The measure includes three subscales: (a) Racism in LGBT communities, (b) Heterosexism in Racial/Ethnic Minority Communities, and (c) Racism in Dating and Close Relationships.
doi: 10.1037/a0023244
title: The LGBT People of Color Microaggressions Scale

description: LSASII: The Linehan Suicide Attempt Self-Injury Interview (LSASII) is a structured clinical interview used to gather detailed information about individuals' history of self-injurious behavior and suicide attempts over the course of their lifetime. The LSASII includes questions about the circumstances surrounding each self-injurious episode and suicide attempt, as well as the individual's thoughts, feelings, and motivations at the time.
title: The Linehan Suicide Attempt Self-Injury Interview (LSASII)

description: The MCI Fidelity Scale examines the responses of the medical professional regarding their mental health intervention with the adolescent and the caregiver. The medical professional must respond to questions in the following categories: the overall assessment, stage of change, barriers, and role play.
title: The Making Connections Intervention (MCI) Fidelity Scale

description: Mexicans/Mexican-Americans may act differently when they are with other Mexicans/Mexican-Americans than when they are with Whites (Gringos; individuals of European American backgrounds). Participants are asked how comfortable they are in these different situations.
title: The Mexican American Biculturalism Scale

description: The Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale-Self Report (MADRS-SR) is a nine-item questionnaire individuals with mood disorders use to rate the severity of their depressive episodes. Items are scored from 0-3 and measure mood, feelings of unease, sleep, appetite, ability to concentrate, initiative, emotional involvement, pessimism, and zest for life. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0165-0327(00)00242-1
title: The Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale-Self Report (MADRS-SR)

description:
The Motivation for Solitude Scale, is a measure grounded in Self-Determination Theory that differentiates between intrinsic versus extrinsic motivations for solitude.
DOI: 10.1016/j.adolescence.2018.11.004
title: The Motivation for Solitude Scale

description: Self-report measure which assesses unusual sensory experience across six modalities: auditory, visual, olfactory, gustatory, bodily sensations, and sensed presence. Doi: 10.3389/fpsyg.2017.01363
title: The Multi-Modality Unusual Sensory Experiences Questionnaire (MUSEQ)

description: Brief self-completion measure to assess residents’ evaluations about the quality of their neighborhoods.
title: The Neighborhood Quality Evaluation Scale (NQES)

description: The Nine Item Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID) Screen (NIAS) is a brief multidimensional instrument to measure ARFID-associated eating behaviors. It measures picky eating, appetite, and fear response in individuals 14+. Parent report is for parents of children aged 7-17.  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.appet.2017.11.111; https://doi.org/10.1002/eat.23520
title: The Nine Item Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID) Screen (NIAS)

description: The Oxford Cognitions and Defenses Questionnaire (O-CDQ) is a 33-item scale that assesses the key cognitions and behaviors common in agoraphobia. It includes three subscales: threat cognitions, anxious avoidance, and within-situation safety-seeking behaviors.
title: The Oxford Cognitions and Defenses Questionnaire

description: The Oxford-Agoraphobic Avoidance Scale (O-AS) is an 8-item self-report questionnaire measuring agoraphobic avoidance. The O-AS presents various everyday tasks of increasing difficulty, from standing outside the home to sitting in a café and each item is then rated on two separate response scales: avoidance and distress.
title: The Oxford-Agoraphobic Avoidance Scale

description:
The Oxford-Liverpool Inventory of Feelings and Experiences (O-LIFE): Further description and extended norms

Oliver Mason

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.schres.2005.12.845
title: The Oxford-Liverpool Inventory of Feelings and Experiences Questionnaire

description: The Perceived Need for Care Questionnaire (PNCQ) was designed for the Australian National Survey of Mental Health and Wellbeing. The PNCQ complemented collection of data on diagnosis and disability with the survey participants' perceptions of their needs for mental health care and the meeting of those needs. The four-stage design of the PNCQ mimics a conversational exploration of the topic of perceived needs. For unmet need and partially met need, information on barriers to care is collected.  DOI: 10.1007/s001270050260
title: The Perceived Need for Care Questionnaire (PNCQ)

description: Phubbing, an amalgamation of the words ''phone'' and ''snubbing,'' can be described as an individual looking at his or her mobile phone during a conversation with other individuals, dealing with the mobile phone and escaping from interpersonal communication.. The Phubbing Scale is a 10-item measure, originally developed in a Turkish study by Karadag et al. (2015) with two factors: Communication Disturbance and Phone Obsession. https://doi.org/10.1556/2006.4.2015.005
title: The Phubbing Scale

description: The Positive and Negative Affect Schedule.This scale consists of a number of words that describe different feelings and emotions.
title: The Positive and Negative Affect Schedule Adult Version

description: This self-report questionnaire evaluates problematic and risky Internet use; DOI: 10.1016/j.chb.2014.01.035
title: The Problematic and Risky Internet Use Screening Scale

description:
The Reported and Intended Behaviour Scale (RIBS), is an instrument based on the Star Social Distance Scale, to assess reported (past and current) and intended (future) behavioural discrimination among the general public against people with mental health problems.
DOI: 10.1017/s2045796011000308
title: The Reported and Intended Behaviour Scale (RIBS)

description: The Ritvo Autism and Asperger Diagnostic Scale-Revised (RAADS-R) was based on the 80 item RAADS. The RAADS is a self-evaluation questionnaire used to aid in the identification of patients who may have undiagnosed ASD. doi: 10.1186/2040-2392-4-49
title: The Ritvo Autism and Asperger Diagnostic Scale

description: The Self-Rated Health Questionnaire is a 32-item measure of current health and health behavior. Srh11 asks individuals to rate their general health and has been consistently shown to predict mortality across studies, even after accounting for known health risk factors (see Idler & Benyamini, 1997).
title: The Self-Rated Health Questionnaire

description: The self-regulation questionnaire (SRQ) developed by Brown et al. in 1999 is a 63-item instrument designed to measure the generalized ability to regulate behavior so as to achieve desired future outcomes. The short form version, Short Self-Regulation Questionnaire (SSRQ), is a 31-item measure. Carey KB, Neal DJ, Collins SE. A psychometric analysis of the self-regulation questionnaire. Addict Behav. 2004 Feb;29(2):253-60. doi: 10.1016/j.addbeh.2003.08.001. PMID: 14732414.
title: The Self-Regulation Questionnaire (SRQ)

description: The self-report Eating Disorders 100,000 Questionnaire (ED100K) captures lifetime history of eating disorder symptoms and can yield lifetime diagnoses of AN, BN, and BED according to DSM criteria (PMID: 30287268). The ED100K was developed with selected diagnostic questions from the clinician-administered Structured Clinical Interview for DSM Disorders-Module H (SCID) that were adapted for self-report. The ED100K has been validated against the eating disorder module of the SCID (PMID: 30287268). This is version 2 of the ED100K; version 2 uses DSM-5 criteria, whereas version 1 uses DSM-IV criteria. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cct.2018.09.015.
title: The Self-Report Eating Disorders 100,000 Questionnaire (ED100K)

description:
The Severity of Dependence Scale (SDS) was devised to provide a short, easily administered scale which can be used to measure the degree of dependence experienced by users of different types of drugs. The SDS contains five items, all of which are explicitly concerned with psychological components of dependence. These items are specifically concerned with impaired control over drug taking and with preoccupation and anxieties about drug use. 

DOI: 10.1046/j.1360-0443.1995.9056072.x
title: The Severity of Dependence Scale

description: A short, disease-targeted, multidimensional health-related quality of life (HRQOL) instrument in patients with cirrhosis and advanced liver disease. The Short Form of Liver Disease Quality of Life includes 36 disease-targeted items representing nine domains: symptoms of liver disease, effects of liver disease, memory/concentration, sleep, hopelessness, distress, loneliness, stigma of liver disease and sexual problems. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1365-2036.2008.03817.x
title: The Short Form of Liver Disease Quality of Life (SF-LDQOL)

description: The Short Internalized Homonegativity Scale (SIHS) measures internalized homophobia in gay men, capturing negative self-directed attitudes stemming from societal stigma. It’s a validated tool used in both research and clinical contexts. https://doi.org/10.1177/0013164404264845
title: The Short Internalized Homonegativity Scale (SIHS)

description:
The Situational Confidence Questionnaire is a self-report questionnaire developed to assess situation-specific coping self-efficacy  for use of a particular substance of abuse; it includes the BSCQ, an 8-item measure that assesses self-efficacy to resist alcohol use in tempting situations.

Annis, H. M., & Graham, J. M. (1988). Situational Confidence Questionnaire (SCQ 39): User’s guide. Toronto, Ontario, Canada: Addiction Research Foundation.
title: The Situational Confidence Questionnaire

description: The Social Attribution Task-Multiple Choice; Versions 1 and 2
title: The Social Attribution Task-Multiple Choice

description: The Social Media Communication Questionnaire (SMCQ) is a self-report measure of social media use frequency (hours per day on average, visits per day), number of friends/followers, platforms/modalities used (e.g., social media apps, text messaging, gaming), preferences to use technology versus face-to-face interactions to communicate emotions, as well as perceived burden, and interference of technology in daily life.
title: The Social Media Communication Questionnaire

description: This questionnaire is modified from the Thoughts About Abstinence Scale (TAAS) to use for alcohol. There is no scoring. The Thoughts About Abstinence Scale was adapted from Marlatt, Curry, and Gordon (1988). The primary measure of commitment to abstinence is an item asking subjects to endorse one of six abstinence goals. The goals are (a) total abstinence, never use again; (b) total abstinence, but realize a slip is possible; (c) occasional use when urges strongly felt; (d) temporary abstinence; (e) controlled use; and (f) no goal. Subjects were also asked to assess on 10-point scales their desire to quit, their expected success in quitting, and the expected difficulty of quitting.
title: Thoughts about Abstinence Scale

description:
Translational Bandit Task is a variant of the restless Bandit task that uses choice options (neutral images) that are rewarded probabilistically. The rewarded stimulus with the highest probability of reward is changed over time. State learning associated with exploring or exploiting choice options and staying or switching stimuli too quickly (lose-switching) can be evaluated.
DOI: 10.1111/tops.12145
title: Translational Bandit Task

description: A variant of the Dot Pattern Expectancy (DPX) task, the Translational Orientation Pattern Expectancy task consists of a series of visual gabor pattern sequences. One pattern is designated the “A” cue, and another the “X” cue, which requires one response (the correct sequence, AX, 60-70% of trials, e.g. respond with the left button), while other sequences require a different response (AY or BX, 12-15% of trials each, or BY, 6-10% of trials, e.g. respond with the right button). Given the strong expectation that X’s evoke a valid response, BX trials place demands on the fidelity (stability, memory) of the “B” cue state representation to overcome this tendency. The TOPX task measures cognitive control and state estimation.
title: Translational Orientation Pattern Expectancy Task

description: Spectral data collected during the Trier Social Stress Test (TSST). The TSST reliably activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis in most participants (Frisch et al., 2015), leading to a change in blood pressure, heart rate, and respiratory rate. Heightened or blunted blood pressure reactivity to acute stress can provide meaningful insights into an individual’s health (Wright et al., 2014). Chronic stress can contribute to negative health outcomes, including hypertension, and alter long-term stress reactivity patterns, including delayed recovery (Spruill, 2010).
title: Trier Social Stress Task Spectral Analysis

description: The Two-Alternative Forced-Choice Identification Task (2-AFC) serves as a measure of reward devaluation, measured at the perceptual level. Participants are presented very briefly with a valenced word indicating happiness or sadness, then participants are presented with two words (one being the stimulus word and another a distractor) and are asked to choose which word was presented. In this particular protocol, each trial began with a central fixation cross, followed by an 8ms stimulus, then a mask. Participants are then presented with a 1000ms fixation cross and two answer choices that will remain on the screen until the participant makes a response. Once a response is selected, the next trial begins. Green, D.M., Swets, J.A.,1988. Signal Detection Theory and Psychophysics. Peninsula Publishing, Los Altos, CA.
title: Two-Alternative Forced Choice Task

description:
The University of Washington Risk Assessment Protocol is a wrap-around risk assessment procedure, as well as a management protocol, specifically designed for use in research assessments with suicidal or other high-risk participants. It involves pre- and post-assessment tracking of risk variables as well as procedures to preemptively plan ways to manage potential increases in risk that may occur during participation.

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cbpra.2010.11.008

description: An unstructured interview is a data collection method that relies on asking participants questions to collect data on a topic. description:
A binary entry indicates whether or not the participant has taken any psychopharmacological medications in the month prior to filling out the form. 
1: Indicated any medication usage
0: Indicated no medication usage

Psychopharmacological medications include those usually prescribed for: 
-hyperactivity, impulsivity, attention
-ritualistic behavior, obsessive compulsive and perseverative behaviors
-stereotypic movements, motor or vocal tics
-anxiety
-aggression/impulsivity
title: Vanderbilt Drug Questionnaire

description:
The Vanderbilt Mental Health Self Efficacy Questionnaire was developed and used by Bickman and his colleagues (1991) as part of the Family Empowerment Project. The measure consists of 25 Likert scale items measuring parents’ self efficacy beliefs and behavioral expectations about obtaining and participating in mental health treatment for their children. Self efficacy is a parent’s assessment of his or her ability to meet a desired goal. Parents rate each item from strongly agree (1) to strongly disagree (5). 
Bickman, L., Earl, E., & Klindworth, L. (1991). VMHSEQ. Nashville, TN: Vanderbilt University.
title: Vanderbilt Mental Health Self Efficacy Scale

description: Behavioral task that assesses approach and avoidance responses to alcohol images, traumatic stress-related images (i.e., military combat images), and neutral images. Participants are instructed to either push or pull a joystick in response to the orientation (landscape or portrait, or vice versa) of an image presented on a computer screen. Participants' mean reaction times (RTs) during the approach alcohol trials are subtracted from the mean avoid alcohol trial RTs. These scores are then divided by the SD across all alcohol trials. Positive scores indicate an approach bias for alcohol stimuli. This procedure will be repeated for traumatic stress stimuli.
title: Veteran Health Behaviors Approach Avoidance Task

description: This decision-making task is divided into a stable and volatile block of trials, each 90 trials long. In the volatile block, the shape with a higher probability (80%) of resulting in reward (or shock) receipt switched every twenty trials.

description: This short form from the Wechsler IQ tests uses 4 subtests common to the WAIS-III, WISC-III, and WPPSI-R.  Two verbal subtests (Vocabulary and Comprehension) and two performance (nonverbal) subtests (Block Design and Object Assembly) are administered.  A full scale IQ, verbal IQ, and performance IQ score are calculated from this short form using procedures found on pages 254-257 in Sattler (2001) which takes into account subtest reliability.  Note that Sattler refers to these scores as "Deviation Quotients" though they have been labeled here as "IQ" for simplicity.    Sattler, J. (2001).  Assessment of Children: Cognitive Applications, Fourth Edition.  San Diego: Jerome M. Sattler, Publisher, Inc.
title: WechslerIQ_ShortForm

description:
ADHD symptoms and actual impairment overlap but are distinct concepts. It is important to measure both since some patients are highly symptomatic but not impaired or vice versa. 
This scale contains those items that are most likely to represent the patient's target of treatment. Therefore, the use of the scale before and after treatment can allow the clinician to determine not only if the ADHD has improved, but if the patient's functional difficulties are also better.
DOI: 10.1177/1087054716661421
title: Weiss Functional Impairment Rating Scale

description: The Wellness Behaviors Inventory (WBI) is a 12-item measure that assesses how often common health-promoting behaviors (e.g., healthy eating, exercising) are performed. The WBI general wellness behaviours mean is based on 10 of the 12 items, and excludes two items related to vitamin and supplement use (items 9 and 12) which form a separate index of these behaviours. Sirois, F. M. (2001; 2019). The Wellness Behaviors Inventory. Unpublished manual. Department of Psychology, Durham University, UK.
title: Wellness Behaviors Inventory (WBI)

description: Wellness encompasses 8 mutually interdependent dimensions: physical, intellectual, emotional, social, spiritual, vocational, financial, and environmental. This inventory outlines wellness strategies you may use for your personal recovery.

description: The Wijma Delivery Expectancy/Experience Questionnaire (W-DEQ) is A 33-item psychological self-report questionnaire used to assess fear of childbirth during pregnancy and after delivery. description:
The Willingly Approached Set of Statistically Unlikely Pursuits (WASSUP) assesses the tendency of people with bipolar disorder, or those who are at risk for it, to set elevated goals in their lives.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10608-006-9044-7
title: Willingly Approached Set of Statistically Unlikely Pursuits

description: The Willingness To Pay (WTP) Task is a novel task that assesses willingness to pay for activities that are social in nature vs those that are nonsocial in nature. Over the course of 50 trials, participants choose between one social experience and one nonsocial experience at given price points. The WTP task is a modified Becker-Degroot-Marshack auction which is designed to identify the subjective threshold between an option’s anticipated value and its cost, i.e. the maximum amount an individual is willing to pay for an expected outcome.
title: Willingness To Pay (WTP) Task

description:

description: The Work-related Flow inventory (WOLF) measures flow at work, defined as a short-term peak experience characterized by absorption, work enjoyment, and intrinsic work motivation.

description: The Wolf Trait Reward-Aversion-Effort Scale is an unpublished self-report measure that assesses trait motivation and trait anhedonia. The Likert-rated scale evaluates motivation and responses to positive and negative outcomes across three domains: social, monetary, and cognitive achievement (correctness).
title: Wolf Trait Reward-Aversion-Effort Scale

The Survey of Well-being of Young Children (SWYC) User’s Manual (version 1.01)[manual]. Tufts Medical Center. www.theSWYC.org. https://www.tuftsmedicine.org/sites/default/files/2024-01/swyc-manual-v101-web-format-33016.pdf

description:
The Yogurt Test Meal Paradigm is designed to assess fear associated with weight and shape-focused eating disorders. Participants are given one of three pairs of test meal conditions ("high fat" yogurt only, "low fat" yogurt only, or "high fat" and "low fat" yogurt). Self-reported fear and electrodermal activity is assessed at various times before and after the test meal.

Link: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1002/eat.23637
title: Yogurt Test Meal Paradigm

description:
The Young Adult Alcohol Problems Screening Test is an instrument for assessing alcohol problems in college students that asks traditional problems related to drinking as well as problems more closely related to the college environment.
https://psycnet.apa.org/record/1993-12009-001
title: Young Adult Alcohol Problems Screening Test (YAAPST)

description: This module contains the attention deficit and disruptive behavior disorders listed in DSM-IV under Disorders Usually First Diagnosed in Infancy, Childhood, or Adolescence. The following disorders are included: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder is characterized by prominent symptoms of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity.  Oppositional Defiant Disorder is characterized by a pattern of negativistic, hostile, and defiant behavior. Conduct Disorder is characterized by a pattern of behavior that violates the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms or rules. Behavior disorders not otherwise specified: This category is to be used when there is significant change in attention and behavior, which does not fully meet criteria for the above disorders.
title: Young Adult Follow-up ISCA Module VI: Attention deficit and disruptive behavior disorder

description: This module contains the attention deficit and disruptive behavior disorders listed in DSM-IV under Disorders Usually First Diagnosed in Infancy, Childhood, or Adolescence. title: Young Adult Follow-up ISCA Module VI: Attention deficit and disruptive behavior disorder Part 2

Source-Walker, J. S., Thorne, E. K., Powers, L. E., & Gaonkar, R. (2010). Development of a scale to measure empowerment of youth consumers of mental health services. Journal of Emotional and Behavioral Disorders, 18(1), 51-59.
title: Youth Empowerment Scale

description: Resilience
title: Youth Prosocial Behavior Survey

description: The Youth Services Survey for Families (YSS-F) is a standardized caregiver-report questionnaire designed to assess family satisfaction and perceived outcomes of youth mental health services. Developed within the SAMHSA National Outcome Measures framework, it evaluates domains such as access, participation in treatment, cultural sensitivity, and the impact of services on the child’s functioning. https://www.samhsa.gov/sites/default/files/mhbg-youth-services-families-survey.pdf
title: Youth Services Survey for Families

description:
The Youth Strength Inventory (YSI) is a subscale of the Development and Well-Being Assessment (DAWBA), section N. A parent and adolescent version ask about the strengths/positive qualities of the child. The first part asks how various descriptions are to the child (i.e., generous, affectionate) while the second part asks about things the child does that please the parents (parent report) or the child is proud of (self-report). Answers are rated as 0= No; 1= A little; 2= A lot. https://doi.org/10.1192%2Fbjp.bp.114.144519

The DAWBA as a whole is designed to provide ICD-10 and DSM-IV psychiatric diagnoses in 5-16 year olds. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7610.2000.tb02345.x
title: Youth Strength Inventory (YSI)

description:
The Zanarini Rating Scale for Borderline Personality Disorder (ZAN-BPD) assesses the severity of past-week DSM-defined borderline personality disorder symptoms. Each of the nine criteria for BPD is rated on a five-point anchored rating scale of 0–4. The four sector scores sum to provide a total score of borderline psychopathology, which ranges from 0–36.
DOI: 10.1002/pmh.1302
title: Zanarini Rating Scale for Borderline Personality Disorder (ZAN-BPD)

description:
The Zero Suicide Workforce Survey is an assessment instrument in the Zero Suicide Training Toolkit. It was developed as an organizational needs assessment tool, not as a traditional psycho-social measurement instrument. Questions are grouped into five sections: prevalence of suicide; beliefs about suicide and people at risk of suicide; current suicide care practices; training, skills, confidence and supervisory support to work effectively with suicidal patients; and job characteristics.

DOI: 10.1177/2050312120933152
title: Zero Suicide Workforce Survey

description:
The Zoccolillo Measure of Antisocial Behavior (ZMAB) assesses parents’ conduct and adult antisocial symptoms. Items are related to both childhood/adolescence (i.e., the period before the end of high school) and adulthood (since leaving or finishing school).
Huijbregts, S. C., Seguin, J. R., Zoccolillo, M., Boivin, M., & Tremblay, R. E. (2008). Maternal prenatal smoking, parental antisocial behavior, and early childhood physical aggression. Development and psychopathology, 20(2), 437-453.
title: Zoccolillo Measure of Antisocial Behavior

description: Related to understanding the impulsivity and sensation seeking disposition. The Zuckerman-Kuhlman Personality Questionnaire (ZKPQ) captures personality using the Alternative Five-Factor Model (AFFM). The factors are: Impulsive Sensation Seeking (ImpSS), Neuroticism-Anxiety (N-Anx), Agression-Hostility (Agg-Host), Activity (Act), and Sociability (Sy). https://doi.org/10.1037/t06537-000
title: Zuckerman Kuhlman Personality Questionnaire

description: The Zung Self-Rating Depression Scale (SDS) was created to quantify the symptoms of depression using the diagnostic criteria pervasive affect, physiological equivalents or concomitants, and psychological concomitants. Patients rate how frequently items apply to them, from a little of the time to most of the time. A less depressed patient has a lower score; a more depressed patient has a higher score. https://doi.org/10.1001/archpsyc.1965.01720310065008
title: Zung Self-Rating Depression Scale (SDS)

description: The data elements in this structure are meant to describe the onset timings of events (e.g., command displayed to subject), stimuli (e.g., small sprays of a liquid stimulus), and tasks (e.g., stop signal task) during fMRI experimental paradigms. Please see associated experiment ID for further details on events of the experimental paradigm. Example fMRI experimental paradigm: https://doi.org/10.1111/acer.13158
title: fMRI Onset Timings for Stimuli Tasting & Stop Signal Task

description: Scores derived from tasks done during fMRI scan(s)
title: fMRI Task Derived Indices

description: This fMRI task assesses decision-making under stable and volatile conditions. In the stable block, the probability of receiving a reward (or shock) associated with each of two shapes remains the same across all trials, while in the volatile block, the shape with a higher probability of resulting in reward (or shock) receipt switches every twenty trials.
title: fMRI Volatility

description:
The iBelong Scale assesses the multidimensional components of racial-ethnic-cultural (REC) belonging for Black, Indigenous, and other people of color (BIPOC). Items are scored such that higher scores indicated a stronger sense of REC belonging, and lower scores indicated a lower sense of REC belonging.

DOI: https://doi.org/10.1037/cou0000716
title: iBelong Scale

description:
These scales can be used to study in greater detail the process and sequence of health behavior change and the perceptions of which behavioral components are relatively easy or difficult to change.Self-efficacy factors were significantly associated with reported diet and exercise behaviors, providing evidence of criterion-related validity. These self-efficacy scales for eating and exercise behaviors show preliminary evidence of being reliable and valid.
https://doi.org/10.1093/her/3.3.283
title: iOTA-HBHM Self-Efficacy Questionnaire - Eating and Exercise Habits

description: The task-based components of iTEST are built upon previously validated mobile cognitive tasks that target verbal learning and facial emotion recognition, including the Mobile Variable Difficulty List Memory Test (VLMT) and the Mobile Electronic Test of Emotional Recognition (METER). These tasks are administered through a smartphone software in which the list number progresses throughout the study.
title: iTEST Assessment

description: The m-Path app uses the experience sampling method to collect data via mobile app about interactions and moods from adolescent best friend dyads. Prompts to answer questions are sent several times a day; questions ask about contact with friend since last notification, communication styles, co-rumination and co-dampening, and dysphoric and positive affect. If there has not been contact with the best friend, then the participant is asked the same questions about another person with whom the participant has had contact.
title: m-Path: Teen Friendship EMA
